With Elon having only 25 chips left, he is essentially all-in. I will check to see how the action unfolds. I have no reason to bet into a huge chip leader on a board that favors them, and I'm prepared to fold if Minni shows aggression, as my Ace-high has very little equity here.

Ultra short-stacked with 25 chips (0.25BB effective) in BB on flop holding top pair top kicker (Jh pairs Jd, Qs kicker) on 8d-Tc-Jd board after calling 200 pre-flop into multiway pot. Flash (SB) checked first to act, pot 900. All-in shove of 25 is a min-sized bet (~3% pot), gets maximum thin value from opponents' wide calling ranges (worse pairs like T9/T8/97, flush/straight draws like KdQd, Adx, suited connectors). My hand crushes these (~60%+ equity vs loose ranges). Checking preserves stack but risks blank turn forcing fold or getting called lighter later; shoving realizes EV now with strong showdown value and live Queen for overcards. Opponents deep (Flash 5025, Minni 29350), pot odds force calls from ~3% equity hands we beat.

Trivial call. The flop bet is tiny relative to the pot (25 into 925) so the pot odds (~2.6%) make calling a no‑brainer with 5♦4♣ despite having no made hand — I have remote backdoor equity and virtually zero risk to see the turn. Raising here with nothing is spewy and unnecessary; I’ll call to see the turn and only continue if I pick up real equity or the action remains passive (fold to heavy turn aggression unless improved).

The current pot is 950 chips and the cost to call is only 25 chips. This gives me pot odds of approximately 38:1. Even though my hand (Ace-high) has not improved on this highly coordinated board (8-T-J), the mathematical requirement to call such a small amount relative to the pot size is absolute. I will call to see the turn and potentially see a showdown against Elon, who is now effectively all-in.

The board is extremely dangerous with a four-card straight (8-9-10-J) and three diamonds present. My hand is Ace-high with no diamonds and no straight components. Elon is already all-in for his remaining 25 chips on the flop. Minni, the chip leader, is still in the hand. Since I have no hand and no draw, I will check. If Minni bets, I will fold as my hand has virtually no equity against her range on this coordinated board.

Board is extremely wet: 8♦ T♣ J♦ 9♦ — three diamonds and four connected ranks. I hold 5♦4♣ with no pair and only a backdoor possibility (a diamond on the river gives me a flush by combining my single ♦ with four ♦ on board). My river diamond chance is ~9/46 ≈ 20%, so my raw equity is very low and a bet here is likely to be called by any made straight, pair+draw, or any two-diamond flush. Flash merely checked the turn (no heavy aggression), and Elon is effectively all-in (0) so I’m not getting fold equity worth risking a sizable chunk of my big stack. I’ll check to see the river for free and fold to any significant bet; only consider calling a very small bet that prices my draw correctly or bluff-catch on a harmless river card.

The board is extremely coordinated with a four-card straight (8-9-10-J), three diamonds, and a paired board (8s). My Ace-high (As 6h) has virtually no showdown value against Minni's range, especially since she called the flop and the turn went check-check. Elon is already all-in. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if Minni decides to bet, as my hand cannot beat any pair, straight, or flush.

Board: 8♦ T♣ J♦ 9♦ 8♣. I hold 5♦4♣ — no pair, no straight, and only four diamonds (no flush). This is an extremely connected, dangerous board: Q or 7 completes straights, two-card diamond combos make flushes, and the board is paired so full houses are possible. Flash has been passive (checked flop and turn) and just checked the river. I have essentially zero showdown value and poor blockers, so a river bluff is thin and likely to be called by any pair, straight, or diamond-heavy hand. With a deep stack and no credible fold equity here the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check and fold to a bet rather than risk a large bluff. I will check to see showdown for free.

We're UTG in a 4-handed game with Kc5c (king-five suited). This is a playable, suited king with decent postflop playability and a blocker to some big king combinations. Standard strategy in this spot is to open-raise to take the initiative and avoid multiway pots where K5s loses equity. I'll use a 3xBB sizing (300) — it's a common, effective open size here: builds the pot when we have position later, applies fold equity vs the blinds, and keeps the price reasonable if the short-stack SB shoves. If we face a large 3-bet or a shove that looks strong from Claude/Flash, we'll re-evaluate and fold; vs a wide short-stack shove we can call given their likely wide range.

I am on the button with Jack-Six suited. Minni, who is the massive chip leader and has been quite active, raised to 300 from UTG. While the hand is suited, it is relatively weak against a 3x open raise. Additionally, Elon in the Small Blind is very short-stacked (less than 10 BBs) and is likely looking for any decent hand to shove with. Calling here might induce a squeeze from the blinds or put me in a difficult spot post-flop against a wide but aggressive range from the chip leader. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in SB with AhQh, a premium suited hand (top ~8% of hands). Facing UTG raise to 300 from Minni (huge stack 29k), Flash folded. Pot 450, to call 250 (I've posted SB 50). My stack 925 (~9BB), short-stacked. AQo suited has excellent equity vs Minni's UTG range (often wide/aggressive based on history). BB (Claude) still to act with 4600 stack, but jamming applies max pressure, denies equity, and leverages fold equity. Calling risks multiway pot OOP with short stack; flat too passive. Min-raise too small. All-in shove optimal: ~55-70% equity when called, plus fold equity builds stack or doubles up. Maximizes EV long-term.

Facing a 3-bet to 925 with 8h 6h from the big blind. While 8-6 suited has some merit, I'm in a very weak position here: (1) Out of position postflop, (2) Up against a strong 3-bet range from Elon's small blind, (3) Hand has only marginal equity (~28-30%) against a reasonable 3-bet range, (4) Calling 825 more commits too much of my 4600 stack to a weak hand with poor positional advantage. The pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, but my equity doesn't justify the call given the dynamics. Folding preserves my stack for better spots where I have stronger hands and/or better position.

I opened UTG to 300 with Kc5c, but Elon (SB) 3-bet to 925 leaving me to call 625 into a 1325 pot. K5s is a marginal hand OOP — often dominated by stronger Ks and with poor post‑flop playability — so it needs very favorable pot odds or a very loose 3‑bet range to continue. Calling 625 requires ~32% equity (625 / (1325+625)), which I don’t expect vs a standard SB 3‑bet range here. With a deep stack I’m happy to preserve chips for better spots rather than play a bloated pot out of position with a weak holding. Fold.

I am dealt pocket Jacks (Jh Js) in the UTG position. This is a premium starting hand in a four-player game. My objective is to build a pot for value while also narrowing the field, as Jacks are vulnerable to overcards. Based on the previous hands, a standard opening raise size at this table has been 300 chips. I have a healthy stack of 5,000, while Elon is relatively short-stacked at 1,375. I will open to 300 to take control of the hand and extract value from worse hands that might call.

Preflop on the button facing a UTG raise to 300 into a 450 pot. Holding 5s2h offsuit, an extremely weak hand with negligible equity against any reasonable UTG raising range from Flash. Even with position, calling risks entering a multiway pot with two deep-stacked opponents behind (Claude 4550, Minni 28925), and my stack of 1375 is short enough that speculative calls are low EV. Standard fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Folding 6-3o from the small blind facing an early position raise. This is one of the weakest unpaired hands, and I'm out of position post-flop. Flash's UTG raise represents a stronger range. With 65 hands played and 85 remaining, preserving my 4550 chip stack for premium hands is the mathematically correct play.

Fold. 2♦9♠ is one of the weakest starting hands — it's heavily dominated by a typical UTG opening range and has very poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 200 into a 450 pot gives only ~30% pot‑odds, and my equity vs an early‑position opener is well below that. A light 3‑bet/bluff isn’t attractive here either: Flash opened from UTG (stronger range) and I have no blockers or postflop plan worth risking chips with. Preserve my large stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop UTG with KTo offsuit, stack ~14BB in 4-handed play. Short stack strategy dictates push/fold. KTo has good equity against calling ranges (dominated only by AK/KQ, crushes lower), high fold equity from first in. Limping or min-raising leaves me pot committed awkwardly; shoving maximizes fold equity and builds pot when called. Opponents deep except relative to me; Minni aggressive but SB might defend wide. Over 150 hands, taking +EV shoves grows stack long-term.

Elon is all-in with 1375 from UTG. I hold 5h4h on the button, an extremely weak hand with limited equity. Even in a heads-up scenario against Elon's all-in, 54 is a vulnerable holding with poor equity against most reasonable ranges. The pot offers approximately 1.1:1 odds, requiring ~35-40% equity, but my hand realistically has much less. Calling 1375 would reduce my 4550 stack to 3175, leaving me dangerously short if I lose. At hand 66 of 150, preservation is key. I should fold this weak hand and wait for premium spots to accumulate chips. The risk/reward is unfavorable.

Elon is all-in for 1375 UTG and I’m in the SB with K♠J♠ — a strong hand with good equity versus a shove range. The additional call is 1325 (total committed 1375 including my SB), which is tiny relative to my 28,875 stack (~4.6%) and gives good reward-to-risk to see a flop. Raising to create a side pot against Flash is unnecessary here (I’d be out of position and risk more chips for little extra EV). I’ll call the all-in and re-evaluate postflop if Flash acts.

I am holding 8-deuce offsuit in the big blind. Elon has moved nearly all-in for 1375, and Minni has called. While the pot odds are mathematically tempting, 82o is one of the weakest hands in poker and performs poorly in multi-way pots. Given my stack size of 5050, it is better to fold and wait for a superior starting hand than to commit over 25% of my chips to a likely losing situation.

I’m on the button with 7♣6♠ in a three-handed game — this is a playable, connected hand that plays very well in position. Limping is too passive and invites cheap multiway pots; open‑raising is the correct play to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) to get fold equity and to use a standard sizing that discourages too many callers. With deep stacks I can continue on favorable flops, but I’ll be prepared to fold to a large 3‑bet since this hand is marginal. Total to commit: 300.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The Button (Minni), who is the massive chip leader, has opened to 300. K9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive big stack. Given that I have 50 big blinds and am currently second in chips among the remaining three players, I do not need to take a high-variance, disadvantageous line here. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Calling with T9s from the big blind. This is a textbook big blind defense spot: favorable pot odds (2.25:1), solid hand with straight and flush potential, heads-up play, and a wide button-raising range from Minni. T9s has ~40-45% equity against her range and plays well post-flop out of position. Stack size (22BB) is reasonable. Folding too frequently here would allow exploitation.

Betting 200 on the flop with T♠9♠ (top pair + OESD) against Minni's wide button range. This is a standard c-bet that takes the initiative with a strong hand. I have approximately 40-45% equity and the hand has multiple ways to improve (straight, potential flush). The bet size is proportional to the pot and my stack, maintaining good risk-reward dynamics while not overcommitting. Position matters less in heads-up play when holding a strong hand—taking the lead wins equity and builds the pot appropriately.

I have 7♣6♠ and flopped a paired six on K♠ T♦ 6♦. Claude’s 200 into a 850 pot is a very small c‑bet (calling 200 would win ~1050, so I need ≈19% equity). In position with a marginal but real pair against a wide calling range, I want to realize equity and keep the pot controlled. Raising here is thin — I could get floated or face a shove on later streets with better hands or draws (two diamonds on board). Plan: call to see a turn, re‑evaluate on the card and his action (fold to clear strength on K/T/diamond aggression; probe or barreL on safe cards). Call 200.

Top pair of Tens with a reasonable stack (4050 chips / 40 BB). The turn brought an Ace, which is a significant overcard that could have improved Minni's hand if she holds AK, AQ, AJ, or similar holdings. However, her sequence (preflop raise, flop call, turn check) suggests she has a medium-strength hand rather than a premium pair or strong Ace. Betting approximately 1/3 of the pot maintains my aggressive posture with a strong holding while managing pot size appropriately. This turn bet accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) extracts value from weaker unimproved holdings, (2) charges any remaining draws in her range, (3) builds the pot while I likely have the best hand. My backdoor spade flush draw (T♠9♠) provides additional equity if called. Given my chip depth and position strength, continuation with this sizing is standard value-betting strategy. If she raises, I have clear information that she holds a stronger hand and can make an informed decision.

I have 7♣6♠ — middle pair (6s) on K♠ T♦ 6♦ A♣. Claude’s turn wager is very small (350 into a 1400 pot), so calling gives ~5:1 pot odds (need ≈17% equity). In position with a vulnerable but real pair, the correct, low-variance line is to call and control the pot rather than bluff-raising into a range that contains many A/K/T hands. Raising would commit chips with a hand that is often behind and could induce a shove/call from better. Folding is too weak given the price. I’ll call the 350 to see the river and then fold to clear strength (large shove/overbet) or proceed based on the river card and Claude’s action.

River analysis: I hold T♠9♠, making a pair of Tens with a 9 kicker on the final board (6d Td Ks Ac Js). The Ace on the turn and Jack on the river are both concerning cards that could have improved Minni's hand significantly. Her preflop raise + flop call + turn call pattern suggests a medium-strength holding—likely AK, AQ, AJ, or a King-something rather than a trash hand. The river brought a Jack, creating multiple dangerous possibilities: (1) if she has AJ, AK, or AQ, she now has two pair or trips; (2) if she has KJ, she made two pair; (3) if she has a weak ace or missed hand, I'm ahead. Given the uncertainty and the dangerous runout, checking is optimal here. My pair of Tens is a showdown hand that doesn't merit further investment. Betting 350+ would risk losing additional chips against hands that have likely improved, while checking allows me to realize my hand's current value in a showdown. Stack management is important (3700 chips = 37 BB), and this is not a spot to get aggressive with a marginal holding.

Board A♣ K♠ J♠ T♦ 6♦ and I hold 7♣6♠ — only middle pair. Claude led small on the flop and turn (tiny c‑bets) then checked the river. That line is consistent with a wide, often marginal range (missed diamond draws, Kx, Tx, Ax, occasional bluffs) or a trapping made hand. The river J helps two‑pair/Jx and completes broadway straights with a Q; many hands that beat me (A/K/T/J) will simply call or check‑behind rather than fold to a thin river bet. A small bluff/value bet here is unlikely to extract much and risks getting called (or even check‑raised) by hands that beat me. I prefer the low‑variance, +EV line: keep the pot small and take showdown. Check and see cards — fold to a large raise if Claude opts to turn the river into a bluff/shove.

In a 3-handed game, King-six offsuit is a standard opening hand from the Button. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind.

K3o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant involvement from the small blind. While I only need to call 250 more, this is a clear fold:

1. **Hand Quality**: K3 offsuit is dominated by most hands Flash might raise with (higher kickers, pairs, AK, KQ, etc.). The 3 provides minimal value.

2. **Position**: Small blind is the worst position post-flop. Playing marginal hands here with two opponents is unprofitable.

3. **Stack Considerations**: With 5400 chips in a 50/100 game, I should be selective and fold weak hands, preserving chips for stronger opportunities.

4. **Opponent Action**: Flash's button raise is reasonable but offers no information suggesting my hand is ahead.

5. **Pot Dynamics**: Even with Minni's large stack in the BB, there's no incentive to gamble here with K3o.

Folding preserves my stack for better situations and avoids a likely difficult post-flop scenario.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total) to call. I have 2♦4♦ — a small suited connector with reasonable equity vs a wide BTN opening range and good backdoor/fold-upside, but it's a weak hand to 3-bet out of position. Calling gives ~30.8% break-even equity (200 into a 650 pot after my call), which is roughly in line with this hand's equity vs a wide open range. I don't want to inflate the pot OOP with a bluff-3bet or commit more with such a speculative holding, so I will call and play the flop cautiously (fold to heavy aggression when I miss, look to realize equity or take pots when I connect).

Board is 2s 2h Ad and I hold 2d4d — I have trip deuces. This is a very strong hand heads-up vs a wide button opening range; the only realistic hands that beat me are full houses (pocket Aces or A+2 combos), which are uncommon. I want to extract value from Ax (single A on board shared), weaker pocket pairs, and broadways that will call a reasonable bet, while not pricing them out. Pot is 650, so a ~60% pot bet keeps worse hands in and charges draws. I will bet 400 to get called by one-pair hands and smaller pairs, deny free cards, and re-evaluate if I face a raise.

On a board of 2s-2h-Ad, Minni has led out for 400 into a 650 pot after calling my pre-flop raise. While I have King-high, this board favors the Big Blind's calling range or a defensive lead with an Ace. Given Minni's massive stack and the fact that I have zero equity or draws on this dry board, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Queen-Four offsuit is a weak holding and doesn't justify involvement from the button in this situation. While I have positional advantage and excellent odds (100 to call into a 150 pot), Queen-Four is too marginal to play, especially with proper hand selection. My stack of 5400 is moderate but not so short that I need to get involved with sub-standard holdings. I should wait for better cards in position rather than playing weak hands. With Minni's large stack (29850) and Flash's small stack (4600), there will be better opportunities to gain chips with premium hands or strong situational spots.

I have pocket deuces (22) in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind (Flash, ~46BB effective). Calling to set-mine here gives poor immediate pot odds, so I prefer to raise and take the initiative. Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB) isolates the BB, buys fold equity, and keeps weaker hands and mediocre broadway holdings in so I can extract value or apply pressure. The size is modest so I’m not overcommitted with a low pair; if Flash 3-bets large I can fold, but if he calls I’ll be in position postflop and can c-bet or pot-control depending on the board.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I would have position on Minni post-flop, Q3o is a very weak hand that does not flop well and is easily dominated. Given my current stack of 4600 (46 BB) and Minni's aggressive history and large stack, it is better to fold this weak hand and wait for a more profitable spot rather than bleeding chips on low-equity defenses.

I have pocket kings on the button — the best possible starting hand except aces. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call; minimum raise is 200. With two shorter stacks to my left (Flash ~45.5bb, Claude ~53bb) I want to raise for value and to isolate — a larger open will (a) get folds from weak hands, (b) get called or jammed by worse pairs/high cards and (c) avoid letting both see a cheap flop with speculative holdings. I size to 1000 total (10x BB) which is big enough to apply pressure but small relative to my 30k stack; I will happily call any shove with KK and can proceed postflop with initiative if called. Plan: take down pot preflop if they fold; if called, c-bet/continue depending on texture; if 3-bet shove I call given my hand’s equity vs likely shoving ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni, the chip leader, has opened with a large raise to 1000. Calling 950 more represents over 20% of my remaining stack and leaves me out of position with a marginal hand. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

Calling 900 to see a flop with Ts Kc. Getting roughly 2.28:1 pot odds against an aggressive opponent's wide range. KT has decent equity and multiple ways to improve on the flop. Short-stacked position means I need to accumulate chips when the math is favorable rather than fold passively.

I have top pair (tens) with a King kicker on a relatively dry flop (2d 3d Tc). This is a strong holding that beats most of Minni's continuation range, though they could have pocket pairs or connected cards. Given my short stack (4400 chips) and aggressive opponent, I should bet for value rather than check and allow Minni to dictate the action. A 1000 chip bet (roughly half my remaining stack after the call) represents strength, builds the pot when I'm likely ahead, protects against diamond draws, and leaves me with 3400 chips for the turn/river decisions. This sizing avoids overcommitting while still extracting value from weaker hands in Minni's range. The flop is favorable enough that betting out is superior to passive checking against an opponent who will continuation bet frequently.

I have KK on the button — a strong overpair to the flop 2♦-3♦-10♣. Claude (BB) called preflop from a fairly wide defending range and now bets 1,000 into ~2,050 (pot 3,050). The board has two diamonds so the main danger is opponents with two diamonds (a 4‑card flush draw) or the occasional set/10. I want to deny equity to diamond draws and extract value from worse hands (10x, mid pocket pairs, some broadways), while keeping initiative in position. A raise to the minimum allowed (2,000 total) charges draws, discourages giving a free turn, and gets value from most of Claude’s calling range. If he jams I’m willing to call — KK has good equity vs his likely shove range; if he just calls I’ll continue with position and re-evaluate on the turn.

Calling with top pair (Ts Kc on 2d 3d Tc flop). I have strong K-kicker and am receiving 7:1 pot odds. Minni's aggressive style throughout the tournament suggests a wide range that likely includes draws and marginal hands where my hand has good equity. With 3400 chips, folding a strong hand here would be overly passive. After this call, I'll have 2400 remaining, but I'm committing based on sound pot odds and hand strength rather than stack desperation. The flop is relatively dry, limiting Minni's draw possibilities while my pair is well-positioned.

I have TRIPS (three tens: Ts + Tc + Td) with a King kicker—an extremely strong holding. The turn card (Td) improved my hand dramatically from a pair to trips. Minni's check on the turn is a critical tell suggesting weakness—either a draw that didn't complete (diamonds were a concern with 2d 3d on flop, but Td didn't help flush draws), a marginal pair that missed, or cautious play with a medium hand. My hand beats most of Minni's likely range (weak pairs, draws, air). With only 2400 chips remaining against a 6050 pot, shoving all-in is optimal—it extracts maximum value while properly managing my short stack. Even if Minni called preflop with something like pocket eights or nines, I'm ahead. The all-in bet is both aggressive value extraction and necessary stack preservation.

Board: 2♦ 3♦ T♣ T♦. I hold K♦ K♠ — a very strong hand but vulnerable to trips/quads (any Ten) and a diamond flush (only possible if opponent holds two diamonds). Claude’s line (call preflop, bet/call on the flop, small bet on the turn) is consistent with a wide range: some tens/sets, two-card diamond draws that just completed, medium pairs, or bluffs. Pot is 8,450 and it’s 2,400 to call — about 4.5:1 pot odds (need ~18% equity). KK has far better than that versus Claude’s likely continuing range and calling is cheap relative to our stack. Raising is pointless if Claude is effectively all‑in and folding here would be too nitty. Call to realize equity, extract value from worse hands and unpaired diamonds, and re-evaluate on the river if a diamond or ten comes (or if any further action occurs).

I am on the button with 73s. While suited connectors or gappers can sometimes be used for a steal in three-handed play, 73s is exceptionally weak. Both Claude and Minni have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play large pots. With my stack being the shortest at the table (4550 chips), I prefer to wait for a more advantageous hand to commit chips rather than attempting a high-variance steal with a marginal hand.

With 2c 9s in the small blind, this is a straightforward fold. The hand has minimal value out of position against Minni in the big blind. I've already posted 50 chips as the small blind, and rather than speculate with a weak hand against an aggressive opponent, I should preserve my 10,800 chip stack for premium situations. Minni is well-stacked (24,500) and has shown aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament. Flash has already folded, leaving heads-up play where I'm out of position with a weak hand. Folding is the mathematically sound play here.

I'm holding 3-6 offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. While position is favorable and the pot odds are reasonable (150 to call 100), 3-6o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even accounting for looser 3-handed dynamics and my positional advantage, this hand has minimal equity and poor playability post-flop. I don't have strong blockers, and the hand will struggle against both opponents' likely ranges. Minni has a large stack (24,600) and Flash is short (4,450), but folding marginal hands remains fundamental to sound poker strategy. I should preserve my 10,800 stack for better opportunities.

I have K♥T♣ in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind. This is a strong, playable heads-up hand and I want to take the initiative and use position postflop (in heads-up the SB/button acts last after the flop). Calling 50 is too passive and lets Flash see a cheap flop; a raise extracts fold equity and isolates a single opponent. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — big enough to pressure weaker holdings but not overcommit given Flash’s ~44 BB stack. If called I’ll play the flop aggressively in position; if met with a large 3-bet/shove I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold this marginal holding to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 3x the blind (300). While I am in position post-flop and the pot odds are 3:1 to call, Jack-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well against the aggressive range of the chip leader. Minni has been very active and aggressive in recent hands. I previously folded a similar hand (Queen-Trey) to a raise from the same player. Given my stack size of approximately 44 big blinds, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with a marginal hand against a dominant stack.

I'm on the button with 5♣8♠ — a weak offsuit hand. Pot is 150 and it's 100 to call (min raise to 200). In three-handed play I can open wide, but 5‑8o is marginal and vulnerable to 3-bets from both opponents (SB has a shortish stack, BB is willing to play big pots). I prefer to keep the pot controllable and use my positional advantage: call 100 to see a flop, fold to heavy preflop aggression, and only commit further if I pair or pick up strong draws or can take the pot away on later streets.

I have 4h 2s in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Even though I am getting 5:1 pot odds to call the extra 50 chips, the hand has extremely poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against two opponents, including the chip leader Minni. Preserving my stack for better starting hands is the best strategy here.

Checking with 9-6 offsuit from the big blind. This is a weak holding that doesn't warrant aggression when out of position against a larger stack. By checking, I see the flop cheaply and can evaluate based on the board texture. If I pair or catch a draw, I can play back. If not, I can fold without committing additional chips. Minni's button call is wide, so maintaining pot control is more valuable than building early.

Betting top pair (9s with 6 kicker) on a dry flop in a heads-up pot. I have the best hand in most scenarios against Minni's wide button range. This bet builds value, takes control of the hand, and leverages my position advantage on later streets if called. The flush draw with two clubs adds additional equity.

I called preflop to see a cheap flop with 5♣8♠. The flop 2♥9♦3♠ completely misses my hand — no pair and no realistic flush/straight draw (flush is impossible with only one spade in hand and one on board). I have 6 clean pair-outs (three 5s, three 8s) — ~24–25% to hit by the river, which is slightly below the ≈28% break-even equity for calling 160 into a 410 pot. Claude’s bet is small (~39% pot), so folding would be too tight and raising with nothing is over‑committing into a deeper, capable stack. I’ll call 160 to use my positional advantage: if Claude checks or shows weakness on the turn I can take the pot away, if he barrels small I can continue as a positional float, and if he bets big I’ll fold unless I improve. Call 160.

I hold 9♣6♣ and the board is 2♥9♦3♠6♠. I have flopped top two pair (nines and sixes) with a backdoor flush draw. This is a premium holding that I need to extract value from. The turn card (6♠) is excellent for my hand - it gives me top two pair while being unlikely to have significantly improved Minni's range from her flop call. Given the current pot of 570, betting 300 (about 50% of pot) is appropriate for several reasons: (1) It builds value against Minni's wide button-call range which likely contains underpairs, AK/AQ, and weaker holdings; (2) It charges any draws she might have (flush draws, wheel draws); (3) It maintains pot control and pressure while keeping the pot manageable; (4) It sets up future streets well - if called, I can continue betting on most river cards given my strong hand. Minni's stack is deep enough that she'll likely continue with reasonable holdings, and my two pair wins a significant portion of the time. This is a straightforward value bet with a strong made hand in position.

Turn decision: I have 5♣8♠ on 2♥9♦3♠6♠. After calling the flop, Claude’s turn raise to 300 into a 870 pot leaves me with only one card to come. I have 6 clean pair-outs (three 5s, three 8s) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Calling 300 would cost 300 to win 870+300 = 1,170, so I need roughly 300/1170 ≈ 25.6% equity to break even. My equity (~13%) is far below that, so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff (min-raise to 600) would commit extra chips with almost no fold equity versus Claude’s shown aggression and will often put me in a big pot with almost no showdown equity. I’ll preserve my large stack and fold this marginal hand.

I am on the button with King-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the shortest at the table, but still comfortable at 44 big blinds. Opening for a standard raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value with a hand that plays well post-flop.

5-2 is the worst starting hand in poker with no real winning equity. I'm in the small blind facing a raise from Flash, putting me out of position. The 50 chips already committed is a sunk cost and shouldn't influence the decision on the additional 250 chips. With a healthy 11,060 chip stack at hand 74/150, there's no need to gamble with trash hands. Folding maximizes long-term profitability.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with T♠8♥ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total), which gives ~30.8% break-even equity, so the price is reasonable to see a flop. Flash has been opening to 300 often (wide/aggressive), so defending the BB by calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me exploit post-flop mistakes. I don’t want to 3-bet with this off-suit ten — it’s not strong enough to build a pot OOP or reliably fold out better hands, and a small 3-bet could induce a shove from his ~41bb stack. Plan: call to see the flop and proceed only with top pair or good draws; otherwise pot-control or fold to heavy aggression.

Board J♥ A♦ 4♥ with T♠8♥ — I have no pair and only one heart (need both turn+river hearts to make a flush). I defended preflop with a marginal hand intending to proceed only with top pair or a real draw. This is a classic pot-control spot OOP: the ace on board and two hearts make the flop dangerous, and Flash is a wide opener, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see what Flash does. If he bets small I may call as a float to see the turn, but I will fold to significant pressure (hearts/ace/overpair likely). If he checks back I’ll take a free turn to reevaluate.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has an Ace, which is a card that hits my perceived opening range very well. Minni checked to me, which is standard. I will fire a continuation bet of about half the pot to represent the Ace or a strong Jack and try to take the pot down immediately. If called, I have some backdoor diamond and straight potential to improve on later streets.

I’m on the button’s c-bet with T♠8♥ on J♥ A♦ 4♥. I checked to keep the pot small and Flash bets 300 into a 950 pot. Calling 300 to win 1250 requires ~24% equity. I have no pair, only a weak pair-by-river chance (hitting a Ten or Eight by river ≈24%) and a very small two-card backdoor flush (need both a heart turn and river ≈4%). Against Flash’s wide, aggressive opening and c-bet range I’m still behind most of his value and continuing hands (A‑x, Jx, most pairs, many overcards that can barrel). A raise/bluff OOP here is too risky and likely to be called or shoved; calling to float gives marginal equity and will often force another fold on the turn. Per my preflop plan to fold to significant pressure with air, I fold and preserve a large stack for better spots.

I'm on the button with K6o, which is a marginal hand, but position is everything in poker. I have significant positional advantage with two players already in the pot. The small blind will act first postflop, and I'll act last, allowing me to see their reactions and make more informed decisions. Flash is very short-stacked (4,650) and may be in desperation mode, while Minni has a large stack. Calling 100 to potentially win 250+ is reasonable pot odds. If the small blind folds, I'll have excellent position against the big blind. Raising K6 would be too aggressive here - I want to play in position rather than risk chips preflop with a weak hand. This is a textbook button call scenario where I can leverage positional advantage postflop.

Fold. K♦2♥ is a very weak, frequently dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop versus both the Button and the Big Blind. The call is only 50 more, but this will likely be a multiway pot with poor equity and difficult decisions OOP. Raising to 200 to try and isolate with K2o is speculative and unnecessary given my deep stack and many hands remaining. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4d. The Button (Claude) has limped into the pot, and the Small Blind (Minni) has folded. Since there is no raise and I have a very weak hand, the best and most standard play is to check and see a free flop rather than attempting a bluff or folding my equity.

I have bottom pair (nines) on a very wet board containing two high cards (King and Queen) and potential flush/straight draws. In a limped pot, I want to keep the pot small and see how the opponent reacts. Checking is the most standard and prudent play here to realize my equity and evaluate Claude's response.

Betting 150 chips (60% of pot) on the flop with top pair (K6) after Flash checked. Flash is extremely short-stacked and likely weak given his check. This bet takes the initiative, builds value from worse hands/draws, and wins the pot immediately against pure misses. Position and initiative favor a bet here.

In a limped pot, I have hit bottom pair (nines) on a board of Ks-Qs-9h. While the board is very coordinated and contains two overcards to my pair, Claude's 150-chip bet is relatively small compared to the pot size. Folding bottom pair immediately to a single bet in a limped pot is generally too exploitable, especially when closing the action on the flop. I will call to see how the turn develops, intending to fold if the board becomes more dangerous or if Claude continues with significant aggression on the next street.

The turn card is the Ten of Hearts, which is extremely dangerous. This card completes a straight for any Jack (K-Q-J-T-9) and is also an overcard to my pair of nines. Given the highly coordinated nature of the board and the fact that I have fourth pair with no significant draw, I have very little showdown value. In a limped pot, there is no reason to lead into the opponent here. Checking is the only move, and I plan to fold to most bets as the board now favors many hands in a standard limping range.

Betting 300 on the turn with top pair (K6) after Flash checked. Flash is critically short-stacked at 4,500 and has already called 150 on the flop, demonstrating willingness to commit chips. His turn check indicates weakness. The bet extracts value from his desperation while building the pot for a likely all-in river situation. Top pair is almost certainly ahead against Flash's range on this K♠Q♠9♥T♥ board. Pot odds ensure Flash will likely call, getting me closer to finishing him while building value.

The board is now extremely coordinated with King, Queen, Nine, and Ten. Any Jack completes a straight, and many of my opponent's limping range hands (like J8, J9, JT, QJ, KJ) now have me crushed. My pair of nines is currently fourth pair on a board where any King, Queen, or Ten also beats me. Claude has shown aggression on both the flop and the turn. Given that I have no draw to improve and very little showdown value on this specific texture, calling another bet is not profitable. I will fold and preserve my remaining chips.

3♦2♥ is a garbage hand — the worst part of my range. Facing both blinds (two opponents still to act) a call of 100 into a 150 pot requires roughly ~40% equity to break even, and 3‑2o has far less vs two defending ranges. Raising to try and steal is thin as well: both Flash and Claude defend and reraise frequently, and this hand has almost no postflop playability or fold equity multiway. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, preserving my stack for better spots is optimal. Fold.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong hand. Since the button folded, I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or play with initiative, even though I will be out of position post-flop.

Calling 200 to win 400, getting 2:1 pot odds. Flash is short-stacked (4,200 chips) and opened from SB—a wide range in 3-handed play. K6o is weak but playable, and I have position for the flop. With decent odds and post-flop advantage, this is a clear call. If Flash shoves, I can reassess, but seeing a flop is reasonable here. Minni's fold simplifies the decision—it's now essentially heads-up. I can fold if the flop misses and Flash comes out strong, or develop a hand if I hit top pair or better.

On a very dry board of King-Seven-Deuce rainbow, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage. This is a classic spot for a continuation bet. Even though I only have Queen-high, my opponent is likely to fold any hand that isn't a King or perhaps a Seven. By betting 300 into the 600 pot, I am applying pressure on his non-paired hands and taking advantage of the board's texture to win the pot immediately.

Calling 300 to win 1,200 (1:4 odds). I have top pair on K-7-2, which is likely ahead of Flash's range from the SB with his short 3,900 stack. The raise of 300 is moderate and fits with draws, mid-pair, broadway, or bluffs more than premium holdings. My weak six kicker warrants keeping the pot manageable rather than raising. Calling lets me see the turn and make better-informed decisions on later streets. Position disadvantage is mitigated by Flash's aggression forcing him to act first post-flop.

On the turn 3s, the board remains very dry. I continuation bet the flop and Claude called, which suggests he likely has a piece of the board (a King, a Seven, or a pocket pair) or perhaps an Ace-high that is refusing to fold yet. Since I have no made hand and no draws with Queen-Ten, and my stack is relatively short at 3900 chips (39 big blinds), firing a second barrel into a sticky opponent who called the flop is risky. I will check to see if he checks behind, giving me a free river, or if he bets, I will likely fold to preserve my remaining chips.

Flash checked the turn after being aggressive on the flop, signaling weakness. I have top pair with K-7-2-3 board and K6o—a solid holding that's likely ahead of his range from the SB.

The 3♠ on the turn is a dry card that doesn't complete most draws. Flash's turn check suggests he either missed a draw, is checking back a medium pair, or is being cautious with a weak broadway card.

With 1,200 in the pot and Flash sitting on only 3,900 chips, now is the time to bet for value. A 450 bet (representing about 38% of the pot) accomplishes several objectives:
1. **Value extraction**: Likely gets called by weaker Kx hands, mid-pairs, or draw-heavy cards
2. **Protection**: Makes draws pay if he has any remaining outs
3. **Stack pressure**: Forces Flash to commit ~12% more of his already short stack
4. **Position advantage**: I'm last to act preflop and maintained position postflop despite his aggression

This sizing is aggressive enough to take down the pot immediately if he's bluffing but not so large that it looks like an overpair. The river will likely see action either way, and I can reassess based on his response.

On the turn, after my check, Claude has bet 450 into a pot of 1200. I have Queen-high with no draws on a board of K-7-2-3. While the bet is relatively small, I am out of position and have very little equity against his calling range on the flop. Claude likely has a piece of the board (a King or a Seven) or a pocket pair. Calling with only two overcards (to the 7 and 2) is too speculative, and I would likely need to hit a Queen or Ten on the river to have any chance, and even then, I might be beat by a King. I will fold to preserve my 39 big blind stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Six suited. In a 3-handed game, this is typically within the standard opening range (top 50% of hands). Although I am the short stack with 39 big blinds, I should still look to apply pressure on the blinds, especially since I will have position post-flop if called. Claude and Minni have both been active, but a standard 3x open is appropriate here to take down the 150 in the pot or set up a post-flop play.

Raising with KJ suited from the small blind. Flash's short stack and button raise give me an excellent opportunity to apply pressure and potentially isolate him heads-up. A 3x raise is standard here, allowing me to build the pot with a strong hand while testing Minni's commitment. This is a +EV spot that capitalizes on favorable stack dynamics.

Call 750 more (850 total). Ad5d is a suited ace with reasonable playability against a button open and an SB 3‑bet: nut flush potential, wheel backdoor outs and decent showdown value when I hit an Ace. The call is relatively cheap given my deep stack and keeps the pot manageable — folding would be too tight and 4‑betting here is thin (I’m often dominated by the 3‑bettor’s value range and a min‑4‑bet to 1400 won’t credibly isolate). Pot odds: 750 into ~2000 after my call (~37.5% immediate equity needed) — I’ll see the flop and proceed based on texture (continue aggressively on A/diamond/drawy boards, exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

I opened J6s from the button, which is a standard play, but the situation has changed significantly. Claude 3-bet from the small blind and Minni cold-called the 3-bet from the big blind. A cold-call of a 3-bet from the big blind usually indicates a very strong range (medium-to-high pairs or strong suited connectors/broadways). My J6s is likely heavily dominated by both opponents' ranges. Although I have position and the pot odds are roughly 21%, calling would leave me with an SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of about 1 on the flop. Given the strength shown by my opponents, J6s is too weak to continue in a bloated pot. I will fold to preserve my 33 big blinds for a better spot.

I raised pre-flop with KdJd and Minni called from the BB. Flash folded, leaving me heads-up. The flop of As Th 3h is moderately favorable: I have K-high currently with a strong open-ended straight draw (any Q or 9 completes a straight) plus a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain momentum with a standard c-bet. I'm betting 1000 (roughly 50% of the 2000 pot), which accomplishes multiple goals: (1) applies pressure on Minni's likely wide BB defending range, (2) protects my equity with the OESD and potential straight/flush outs, and (3) keeps the pot size manageable given my 11110 stack. This is a +EV spot that balances my strong drawing potential with immediate hand strength.

Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range is value‑heavy (AK, AQ, TT+/some pairs) but his small 1/3‑pot flop stab (1,000 into 3,000) is also frequency‑balanced and contains bluffs. I hold Ad5d — top pair but a very weak 5‑kicker and no realistic backdoor diamond flush (the flop has no diamonds), so I’m vulnerable to better A‑kickers and sets. Calling 1,000 into 3,000 requires ~25% equity; versus a mixed c‑bet range my hand has enough equity to justify a call and keeps worse A‑x and bluffs in. Raising here is thin — it bloats the pot with a marginal kicker and opens me to check‑raises or being crushed by sets/two‑pair. Plan: call, control the pot size, and re‑evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously if Claude shows heavy aggression or bet for value on safe runouts).

On the turn with board As-Th-3h-7s, I'm holding KdJd and facing Minni, who has called twice (pre-flop and flop). Currently, I have K-high with no made hand. While I do have solid drawing equity with approximately 14 clean outs (3 Kings, 3 Jacks, 8 straight-completing cards: four 9s and four Queens), betting the turn with K-high into an opponent who has demonstrated commitment through two streets is risky. The 7s doesn't improve my hand. Checking here is the prudent line because: (1) It controls pot size with a marginal hand, (2) Minni may check behind and let me see the river, and (3) If she bets, I can evaluate based on pot odds and remaining stack (10110). My drawing potential combined with position flexibility makes check-and-see the optimal play. If Minni checks, I get a free river card. If she bets, I'll have a clear decision on whether my outs and pot odds justify a call.

I hold A♦5♦ on A♠ T♥ 3♥ 7♠ — top pair with a very weak 5‑kicker and no diamond backdoor (board is void of diamonds). Claude 3‑bet pre and made a small flop stab; his check on the turn is ambiguous but shows caution. Betting now would bloat the pot and invite check‑raises or big calls from hands that beat me (AK/AQ, sets, two‑pair combos). A small protection bet would extract from worse A‑x and charge some draws, but given my weak kicker and the opponent’s value‑heavy 3‑betting range I’d rather control the pot size. I’ll check, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the river: call small/medium river bets for thin value or fold to large, polarizing river aggression unless I improve.

I'm holding KdJd on a river board of As-Th-3h-7s-3c. My hand is a pair of threes with K-J-T kickers, which is very weak. Minni checked on the turn, indicating she likely doesn't have a strong made hand, but she called my flop c-bet and had committed capital. On the river, my pair of threes is vulnerable to any Ace, any pair above threes, or any strong kicker. Betting here serves no useful purpose: I'm unlikely to get a worse hand to fold (Minni showed commitment), and I'd only be putting additional chips at risk with a marginal holding. Checking allows me to either reach showdown if Minni also checks, or fold if she bets (avoiding further chip loss with a weak hand). This is the prudent line that preserves my 10110-chip stack in a tournament context where I need to play tight and profitable poker over the remaining 73 hands.

I’ll check and take this to showdown. I have top pair (A♢5♢) but a very weak 5‑kicker and the river paired the board (3s/3c), which increases the likelihood Claude holds trips or full house combos or two‑pair (A‑T). Claude 3‑bet preflop and barreled the flop — his range is value‑heavy — and his turn/river checks are ambiguous but cautious. Betting now would bloat the pot and get me check‑raised or called by all better A‑x and two‑pair hands; folding would be too nitty with showdown value. Check to control pot size; call a small river bet but fold to a large, polarized raise.

Calling with J-5o on the button in 3-handed play. This is a marginal hand but playable given position. The 1.5:1 pot odds are acceptable and I'll have positional advantage post-flop. The button is the most profitable position in poker and I should not be folding too many hands here. Minni likely has a wide range, Flash is short-stacked and may fold to aggression, and I can realize equity with position.

6♦2♥ is one of the worst starting hands — unsuited, unconnected, and has terrible postflop playability. Claude has limped (creating a multiway pot) and I’d be out of position to both the button and the big blind. Calling 50 to play a three-way pot OOP is a losing proposition long-term, and a raise here would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or jammed by the short-stacked BB. No reason to invest more chips with virtually no equity or fold equity — preserve stack for stronger spots.

Claude limped from the button and the small blind folded. I have 9-6 offsuit in the big blind. There is no reason to raise and bloat the pot with a weak hand, especially given my relatively short stack. I will check and take a free flop.

On a dry flop of Ten-Seven-Four after Claude limped from the button, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) with my Nine-Six. There's no reason to lead into the pot with only nine-high and limited equity. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve my hand or reassess based on Claude's action.

Betting 200 on the button with J5o against a weak, short-stacked opponent who checked the flop. Flash's check indicates weakness, and my position gives me significant advantage. I have a gutshot draw with 8 outs (four sixes for a straight) plus overcard equity with my Jack. A bet here wins the pot outright a high percentage of the time against a weak range, and I maintain good equity if called. This is standard continuation betting with both showdown value and drawing equity.

I have nine-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing an eight) on a board of Ten-Seven-Four. Claude limped on the button and has now bet 200 into a 250 pot. This is a large bet (80% of the pot), and I am out of position for the rest of the hand. With only 35 big blinds remaining, chasing a gutshot with poor pot odds and no showdown value is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Button with A♣T♥, 3-handed. Pot is just the blinds (150) and it’s 100 to call — this is a standard open-raise spot. ATo is a strong, positionally advantaged hand so I want to seize the initiative, get fold equity, and often isolate the short SB (Flash, 3,450). Standard sizing is ~3x the BB here, so I’ll open to 300. That size balances extracting value and keeping worse hands and short-stack shoves/defends in range. If SB shoves I can call profitably against a wide shoving range; if BB 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable boards and use position to control the pot when checked to.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand against a button raise. Minni has been highly active as the chip leader, and her button opening range is likely very wide. By 3-betting to 1000, I am raising for value and attempting to take down the pot immediately or isolate Minni while maintaining the initiative. My stack size of 34.5 BBs allows for this aggressive play without being fully committed yet, though I am prepared to play a significant pot given the strength of my hand in this short-handed context.

This is a clear fold. I'm holding 5-6 offsuit, one of the weakest hands in poker. Flash has 3-bet from the small blind to 1000 chips when his stack is only 2500, indicating a strong holding. Even though I'm getting pot odds (1400:900), the equity of 56o against a 3-bet range is far too weak. Flash's short stack means he's likely playing a tighter, stronger distribution. Minni still has options behind me with a 25K stack, adding additional complexity. This hand has virtually no value postflop and I should preserve my 10160 chip stack by folding and waiting for a better opportunity.

Call to 1,000 total (I put +700). A♣T♥ is a strong heads‑up hand and I'm on the button — position is a big advantage. Flash's 3‑bet to 1,000 from a 2,500 stack is likely a fairly wide, pressure‑oriented range; calling keeps worse A‑ and K‑hands and speculative combos in and lets me use position to extract value or fold. Pot will be ≈2,100; if he shoves his remaining ≈1,500 I can call frequently because ATo has reasonable equity vs a shove range and the pot odds are attractive. A small 4‑bet is unnecessary (it leaves him too much behind) and a shove from me would needlessly risk my large stack — so I call and plan to play the flop aggressively when favorable.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, but this monotone flop of [Kc Jc 3c] is very coordinated and dangerous. I hold the Ten of clubs, which gives me a 10-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (to a Queen). My stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is quite low at approximately 1.2. If I bet here, I am essentially committing my remaining stack. Checking allows me to see how Minni, the chip leader, reacts. If she checks behind, I get a free card to try and improve. If she bets, I will have to calculate my pot odds and equity to decide whether to commit my final 2500 chips.

Flop Kc Jc 3c and I hold AcTh — I have the nut flush in position vs Flash (who 3‑bet preflop and then checked). Pot is ~2,100. With the best possible flush I should be extracting value and denying free cards. Flash’s check can be pot‑control or a trap with clubs/strong K/J combos, so betting is the clear play. I size to ~2/3 pot to get value from Kx/Jx, worse clubs and pairs/sets that will call, while still making a shove by the short stack costly (and I’ll call a shove because my nut flush is ahead). Betting also protects against picking up disguised full‑house outs on later streets. I’m adding ~1,400 on top of my 300 preflop commit (total committed = 1,700).

On a monotone board of [Kc Jc 3c], I have a Ten-high flush draw with the Tc and an overcard with the Ad, plus a gutshot straight draw. Given that I 3-bet pre-flop and my remaining stack of 2500 is small relative to the current pot of 3800, I am effectively priced in. Minni's bet of 1700 is large, but with my pot odds (needing roughly 31% equity) and the strength of my drawing hand in a short-handed game, folding would be too tight. Since I only have 800 chips remaining beyond her bet, moving all-in is the only logical play to maximize my fold equity, however small, and ensure I realize my full equity.

I have the nut flush (A♣) on Kc‑Jc‑3c. Flash’s raise to 2,500 appears to put him all‑in (he’s at 0), so I only need to call 800 more (total committed = 2,500). Pot is 6,300, so my call is ~11% pot odds — trivially profitable with the absolute nut flush. The only realistic ways I can lose are a full house if the board pairs and he holds a pocket pair, or an extremely rare straight/straight‑flush line; those are low probability and don’t justify folding. Raising isn’t meaningful since he’s all‑in. This is a clear +EV call to extract value from worse clubs, Kx/Jx and pair combinations, so I call to see the turn/river.

Holding King-Nine suited on the button in a three-handed game is a clear opening situation. I have 35.5 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play post-flop, but I am currently the short stack at the table. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative and either win the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

Folding 3-5 unsuited from small blind. This hand is bottom of the range and offers no competitive value against Flash's raise. Out of position and weak hand strength makes this a straightforward fold. Need to preserve stack for premium hands in better spots.

I’m in the big blind with AdJc facing a single-button open to 300 (pot 450, to-call 200). AJo is a strong, playable hand versus a BTN open — ahead of a lot of their wide opening range but behind premium Ax/Kx. I’ll 3-bet to 1,000 total to (1) get heads-up with initiative, (2) extract value from worse aces and broadways, and (3) apply fold equity to medium-strength hands and small pairs. Size (1,000) is large enough to pressure their wide opens but leaves room to play postflop; if Flash jams 4-bet all-in I’ll re-evaluate (leaning to fold to a polarized shove unless I have reads that it’s a frequent bluff), and if he calls I’ll play cautiously OOP — c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dangerous textures.

I am holding King-Nine suited on the button, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I initially raised to 300, and Minni (the chip leader) responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the big blind. With a stack of 3250 (32.5 BB), calling the 700 to see a flop is mathematically justified by the pot odds, as I only need about 25% equity to continue and K9s generally retains more against a standard 3-bet range. Shoving for 32.5 BB over a 10 BB 3-bet is slightly too much of an over-shove for this hand, so calling allows me to use my position post-flop while preserving my ability to fold if the flop is completely unfavorable.

I flopped top pair with a good kicker (Ad Jc on A♠ 9♣ 4♥). I was the preflop aggressor (3-bet) and this is a very favorable, dry A-high flop to continue for value and protection. Flash is short-stacked (~2,550) and called my 3-bet, so his range is wide enough that many hands (weaker A-x, Kx, broadways, medium pairs) will pay a c-bet. I want to extract value and apply pressure (either win the pot now or get stacks committed) while keeping the price reasonable if he jams. Betting ~2/3 pot balances value extraction and fold equity. If he shoves, I’m prepared to call given my equity vs his likely calling/shoving range and the pot odds. Bet 1,400 into the 2,050 pot to charge worse hands and force a tough decision for the short stack.

The flop of 4h-9c-As significantly favors Minni's pre-flop 3-betting range, which typically includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ). Minni's continuation bet of 1400 into a 2050 pot represents significant strength. While I have middle pair with a King kicker, the Ace is a 'scare card' that makes it difficult to continue. My remaining stack is only 2550, and calling 1400 would leave me with 1150 in a pot of 4850, effectively committing me to the hand. Given the stack-to-pot ratio and the board texture, middle pair is not strong enough to stack off against a chip leader who 3-bet pre-flop. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 25.5 big blinds for a better opportunity.

6-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker and has no business being played even from the button. While position is favorable, hand strength matters more than position, especially in a multi-way pot. Minni has a dominant stack (27,290) and Flash is extremely short (2,450), so there's no reason to speculate with trash here. I should fold and wait for premium hands to exploit my position advantage. Preserving chips for better opportunities is key when deep in a 150-hand tournament.

Claude folded, so this is a heads-up SB vs BB spot. I have Kd5h — marginal but perfectly playable heads-up. Pot is 150 (I’ve already posted 50); to call is only 50 more but leaves me out of position with little initiative. A min-raise to 200 gives the BB excellent pot odds to call and I’ll frequently be up against wide calling ranges. Flash is short-stacked (2450), so I want to apply pressure and deny good odds to call. I’ll raise to 1000 total (i.e. add 950 more). That size is still small relative to my stack (~3.5%) but forces the BB to call 900 into a ~2000 pot (he needs ~45% equity) — good fold equity. If he folds I pick up the blinds; if he shoves or calls I can continue with initiative and can comfortably call a shove given my deep stack. Action: raise to 1000.

I am holding 5-2 suited in the big blind. Minni, the massive chip leader, has raised to 1000 from the small blind. This is a very large raise (10x the big blind) and I only have 2450 chips total. Calling 900 more would commit nearly 40% of my remaining stack with a very weak hand that is unlikely to be ahead of Minni's range. It's better to preserve my stack and look for a better spot to double up rather than bleeding chips with 5-high.

I'm on the button with Q♠T♣, deep stack (27,440) and two opponents: SB Flash ~2,400 and BB Claude ~10,010. Pot is 150 and there's a 100-call on the table (a limp or just the posted blinds). QTs is a strong, position-first hand with good flop playability and fold equity when raising. I want to seize the initiative, isolate the limper/SB, and keep the pot manageable versus the deep BB. A standard 3x-style open to 300 accomplishes that: it pressures the short-stacked SB, keeps my range wide and flexible postflop, and is consistent with prior table sizing. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will re-evaluate, but opening now maximizes my EV in position.

I am currently the short stack with 2,400 chips (24 BB). I am holding a pocket pair of fives (55) in the Small Blind. Minni, who is the massive chip leader, has opened for 300 from the button, a move she has made frequently. In a 3-handed game, pocket pairs are very strong, and 55 is likely ahead of a wide button-opening range. By shoving all-in for 2,400, I maximize my fold equity and ensure that I realize the full equity of my hand without having to navigate a difficult post-flop situation out of position. This is a standard short-stack reshove to either pick up a decent pot or potentially double up.

This is a straightforward fold with 8-3 offsuit, one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. The action indicates strength from Flash's aggressive 3-bet to 2,400 from the small blind, and I'm out of position. Even considering pot odds of roughly 2.2:1, I cannot profitably call with 8-3o against two opponents where at least one opponent has shown significant strength. My hand has virtually no equity in this spot, and folding preserves my stack (10,010 chips) for better opportunities. With 82 hands completed out of 150, I should be selective and avoid marginal/losing propositions like this one.

Call — commit 2,400 total. I opened to 300 with Q♠T♣ and Flash 3‑bet to 2,400. QTo is a strong position hand with decent playability and I don’t want to fold it cheaply facing what looks like either a short‑stack shove or a wide SB 3‑bet. Calling 2,100 more (~7.7% of my 27,140) to see a flop keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position to realize equity or fold to clear strength. A 4‑bet here would polarize and commit too many chips with a marginal hand; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: take the flop in position, continue on Q/T‑connected boards and fold to heavy resistance on blank runouts unless I pick up good equity.

I previously moved all-in for 2,400 chips pre-flop with pocket fives, and it appears I have a negligible remainder of 50 chips left in my stack. The flop comes 3s-Ts-Qd, which is not ideal for my hand as there are two overcards. However, with only 50 chips left and a pot of 4,900, I have massive pot odds to commit the rest of my stack. I will bet my remaining chips to complete the all-in move.

I have Q♠T♣ on a Qd Ts 3s flop — I’m sitting on top two pair, a very strong made hand. Flash effectively committed most of his stack preflop (3‑bet to 2,400) and has just put his remaining 50 in; there’s only 50 to call into a 4,950 pot, so the pot odds make this an easy call. Opponent’s all‑in range is wide; only rare sets or rolling full houses beat me. I call the 50 to see the remainder of the board and realize equity in position (total committed this hand = 2,450).

Raising to 250 with 7♣9♥ from the button in heads-up play. This is a standard aggressive button raise that applies proper pressure while maintaining good position and reasonable pot odds. The hand is playable in heads-up situations, and positional advantage is critical in 2-player poker. This raise size is about 2.5x the BB and gives me fold equity while keeping risk-reward balanced given stack depths.

Heads-up vs Claude (button/SB) opened to 250. I have J♦T♥ in the big blind — a very playable hand heads-up with good straight and two‑overcard potential. Pot is 350 and calling an extra 150 (total commit 250) is cheap and gives good pot odds to see a flop against a likely wide opening range. A 3‑bet is an option as a bluff, but JTo is marginal as a 3‑bet and can be dominated; I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit pair/draw or a favorable texture I’ll play aggressively, otherwise exercise pot control and fold to heavy action when appropriate.

I hold J♦T♥ on a 9♦-8♥-J♠ flop: top pair with a strong ten‑kicker and very healthy straight/backdoor improvement potential (Q or 7 completes a straight; tens/trips also help). Claude opened wide from the button preflop, so his range is fairly broad — I’m ahead of a lot of his hands (overcards, single pairs, many Kx/Qx combos) and vulnerable to relatively few made hands. I’m first to act on the flop and want to both extract value from worse Jx/Tx/9x hands and deny free cards to Q/7 draws. Betting is the right line: it gets value, applies pressure to draws, and keeps initiative. I size to ~60% pot to charge draws but keep the price reasonable to call if he has a marginal hand. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue when I improve or the turn is non‑threatening; if he raises big I’ll give credit to two‑pair/sets and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing. Bet 300 into 500 pot.

Calling the 300-chip raise on the flop with middle pair (9♣ with weak 7 kicker). In heads-up play with a wide range, my pair of nines has reasonable equity against Minni's likely holdings. I'm getting proper pot odds (27% needed, likely have 35-40% equity), and calling allows me to see the turn while maintaining flexibility with my shorter stack. Raising would overcommit to a marginal hand, and folding would be too passive in this 2-player situation. The turn will provide critical information for optimal play going forward.

The turn is A♣ — a big overcard that significantly weakens my flop advantage. I had J♦T♥ and led the flop, but an Ace now beats any A‑x and helps many of Claude’s plausible calling hands (A‑x floats, 9x/8x, Q‑T straights, sets). Claude called my sizable flop bet, so his range is wide but contains enough hands that now have me crushed. With stacks deep and heads‑up dynamics, I prefer pot control here: checking preserves chips, avoids getting raised off by an Ace or two‑pair, and lets me call reasonable bets or fold to heavy aggression. If Claude bets small I can call for pot odds vs draws and worse pairs; if he bets big/raises I’ll give credit and fold. Check to evaluate his reaction and keep options open on the river.

All-in on the turn with pair of nines. Minni's check after flop aggression indicates weakness - likely missed a draw or has a marginal holding worried about the Ace. My 7♣9♥ has reasonable equity (40-50%) against a wide heads-up range. Going all-in applies maximum pressure while Minni is showing weakness, either winning the pot immediately or getting value from weaker holdings like mid-pairs or weak Ace holdings. Checking would be too passive and allow Minni to take control on the river. In heads-up play with a short stack, aggressive pressure betting with reasonable equity is the optimal exploitative strategy.

Claude just jammed the turn for 9,460 after calling my 300 flop bet. Pot is 10,560 so calling 9,460 would require roughly 9,460 / (10,560 + 9,460) ≈ 47% equity to break even. My J♦T♥ is top pair but the A♣ on the turn massively improves Claude’s plausible calling range: many A‑x hands are now ahead, plus two‑pair, sets (99,88,JJ), and made straights (Q10, T7) are all in his value spectrum after calling the flop. My clean outs to improve are small (roughly 3 tens + 2 jacks = 5 outs → ~11% raw chance on the river), and some of those outs are often counterfeited or actually help him (e.g. a J or T river can give him a full house if he holds A‑J/A‑9). Even allowing for some bluffs, his shove is heavily value‑weighted and my equity is well below the ~47% needed. Calling would risk ~32% of my stack for a negative EV, so the correct play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up from the button I should be opening very wide. 5s9h is marginal but perfectly fine to raise in position to seize the initiative and deny Claude easy pots. Limping (calling 50) surrenders initiative and invites 3-bets; a small open to 200 (standard 2x BB here) gives fold equity while keeping the pot controlled. With a big stack advantage I can apply pressure postflop or fold to heavy resistance — plan is to c-bet favorable flops, check/control on dry or heavily coordinated boards, and fold to strong aggression when I have no equity. Raise to 200.

Calling with K8o in heads-up from the big blind. This is a standard defense against the button's raise to 200. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds, the hand has reasonable equity against a wide opening range, and I'll have position advantage post-flop (acting first). The stack sizes allow for continued play without overcommitting preflop with a marginal hand.

Checking K8o on 2-2-A flop in heads-up play. This is a marginal hand with no made hand and no strong draws. The board is dry with three diamonds. Checking allows me to: (1) see if Minni c-bets and get information about hand strength, (2) evaluate the turn before committing chips, (3) control pot size with a weak holding. With a shorter stack (10.3k vs 29.2k), building the pot with marginal hands is not profitable. Standard defensive play.

I opened very wide from the button preflop so continuation-betting a high frequency is the standard line. The flop (2h 2d Ad) is somewhat awkward — there’s an Ace that hits a chunk of Claude’s calling range and a paired deuce on board — but I have no pair and almost no equity. My objective here is to take the pot down now with fold equity rather than bloat the pot while behind. A half-pot c-bet (~200 into 400) pressures hands that missed (broadways, weak pairs, and many floats from the BB) while keeping the commitment controllable if I get called. If Claude shows strength (a sizable raise) I will fold; if he just calls I can evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously. Bet 200.

Folding K8o on 2-2-A flop after opponent's 200 bet into 600. With only ~20-25% equity, marginal hand strength, and 3:1 stack disadvantage, this is not a profitable call. My check was designed to gather information - and now that Minni has shown strength with a c-bet, folding preserves chips for better opportunities. Short-stacked tournament play requires tighter hand selection.

I'm holding 2c-9h, which is statistically the weakest hand in poker. Despite being on the button with position, this hand has virtually no equity against any reasonable range. With a significant chip disadvantage (10,310 vs Minni's 29,540) at hand 85 of 150, I need to be highly selective about entering pots. I should reserve my chips for premium holdings where I can execute profitably. Folding weak hands is crucial when short-stacked relative to my opponent.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 4♠6♦ — a marginal hand but very playable in position. With only one opponent and a big stack advantage, I want to take the initiative and use position/fold equity rather than limp. A standard 2xBB open to 200 (total) is the right size: it’s small enough to keep pots manageable if Claude defends, but big enough to pick up the blind often. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold this weak holding; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting selectively and exercising pot control. Action: raise to 200 to open the pot and leverage positional advantage.

Heads-up play with 8♥9♥ from the BB against Minni's button raise. This is a solid playable hand with connected cards and flush potential. In heads-up scenarios, 89s merits a call from the BB, especially only needing to invest 100 more chips. Minni has been active from the button but not showing an extreme aggression pattern. By calling, I get to see a flop in position and can play my hand's strong equity (overcards, straight and flush draws). This is better than folding a decent hand or overcommitting with a 3-bet when I'm already at a 3:1 chip disadvantage. The pot odds are favorable (3:1) to see the flop and make a decision post-flop based on the board texture.

Checking on this spade-heavy flop with top pair/weak kicker. The 7♠8♠J♠ board creates significant vulnerability for my 8♥9♥ - flush draws are everywhere. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable, get information on Minni's hand strength, and avoid overcommitting. With the 3:1 chip deficit, I need to play efficiently and cautiously, especially against a button raise that hits this flop well. Minni's bet action will inform my turn decision.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser in position on 7♠-8♠-J♠ with 4♠6♦: I have no made pair but a very strong drawing hand — a four-card spade flush draw (9 spade outs) plus a gutshot to a straight with any 5 (3 additional non-spade 5s) for a total of ~12 unique outs (~45% to improve by the river). Claude checked, showing weakness or a desire to play passively, so a semi-bluff is appropriate to leverage fold equity and build the pot when I hit. I size to about 60–65% of the pot (bet 250 into 400) — large enough to fold out overcards/weaker pairs and still keep the pot manageable if called. If Claude calls I can realize my equity in position; if he raises big I will re-evaluate and likely fold to heavy pressure since I currently only have a draw.

Calling on this flop. I hold 8♥9♥ and have hit middle pair (pair of eights) on a 7♠8♠J♠ board. While the board is spade-heavy and presents flush draw concerns, my hand has solid equity:

1. **Made hand**: Pair of eights provides immediate strength
2. **Open-ended straight draw**: Any 6 or 10 completes a straight (9-10-J-7-8 or 6-7-8-9-10), giving me 8 additional outs
3. **Two-pair outs**: Hitting a 9 or 7 gives me two pair

**Pot odds analysis**: Pot is 650, I call 250. Getting 2.6:1 odds with multiple ways to improve makes this a sound call.

**Heads-up context**: Down 3:1 in chips (10,110 vs 29,240), I need to accumulate chips. This is a reasonable spot to see the turn in a playable hand rather than fold passive holdings. Minni's button raise could be light, and my pair with straight draw has decent equity against a broad range.

**Plan**: Call and evaluate turn card action based on what hits.

Turn bet with middle pair on draw-heavy board. Board is 7♠8♠J♠7♣ with three spades showing. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a draw. I'm down 3:1 in chips, so I need to be aggressive with made hands. My 8♥9♥ has decent equity and showdown value. A 500-chip bet (approximately 55% pot) applies pressure, wins the pot against weak holdings/draws, and is efficient with my short stack. This is better than checking and allowing the river to bring a spade or scare card. In heads-up play from a short-stack position, I should capitalize on showdown strength and apply pressure when opponents show weakness.

Pot is 1,400 and Claude bet 500 on the turn. Calling 500 to win 1,400 gives ~2.8:1 pot odds, so I need about 26.3% equity to break even. I hold 4♠6♦ on 7♠-8♠-J♠-7♣: a four‑card spade flush draw (9 spade outs) plus the gutshot to a 5 (up to 3 additional non‑spade 5s) → roughly 12 unique outs on the turn, ~12/46 ≈ 26% to hit the river. That’s essentially break‑even versus a random calling range and profitable versus any villain bluffs or many one‑pair hands. Raising here as a semi‑bluff would risk getting called or raised by the hands that dominate me (made flushes, trips/full houses) and would thin my equity when called. I’ll call the 500, realize my equity in position, and re‑evaluate on the river (bet/shove if I hit, fold to heavy action if I miss and Claude shows strength).

Check to showdown on this double-paired board. With pair of eights and a nine kicker on 7-7-J-J board, this is showdown value. The river J♦ likely improved draws and hands that called my turn aggression more than helped me. Checking allows me to see the result, preserves chips on a marginal hand, and avoids committing more to a hand that's vulnerable to Minni's likely holdings (any pair or made draw). In a short-stack situation, I should invest chips in stronger situations rather than marginal showdown hands on unfavorable boards.
