I have 4♠6♦ on 7♠-8♠-J♠-7♣-J♦. My flush and straight draws both missed on the river — I have no pair and no showdown value. Claude’s line (check-call flop, bet the turn) is consistent with value hands (Jx, two pair, trips) or at least a very thin value/controlled range; the river pairing the board creates many full-house/trips combinations in his range. My fold equity is low and a bluff here is high risk — many hands he calls with beat me and he’s shown aggression earlier. I called the turn to realize equity; now that I missed, checking to take it down at showdown (or fold if he bets) preserves chips and is the lowest-EV-loss play. Action: check.

Short-stacked in heads-up (11k vs 28k). 8-10 offsuit is marginal but playable from button position. Need to apply pressure with position and chip advantage opportunity. Raising to take down dead money or build a pot with decent equity if called. Min raise to 200 total chips.

Heads-up vs the Button (Claude) who opened to 200. I’m in the big blind with Q♠5♣; I’ve already posted 100 so calling 100 more (total 200) is cheap and defends a reasonable heads-up range. Pot odds are roughly 3:1 (I need ~25% equity) and Q5o has enough equity vs a wide SB open to justify a call. 3-betting to the min (300) is small and unnecessarily builds the pot with a marginal hand; folding would be too tight. Plan: call, see a flop, and play cautiously out of position — continue on favorable boards (Q/5 or good draws) and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

Flop: A♠ 2♣ 7♣. I’m OOP with Q♠5♣ — no pair, no real draw (only a single club; would need runner-runner clubs). Claude opened preflop so his continuation range includes many A♣/A♠ and hands that will c-bet this board. Leading or check‑raising with this air is unnecessary risk out of position. I’ll check to control the pot and give myself options: fold to a meaningful c-bet, call a small stab as a float only if the bet is tiny and I see a favorable turn (Q,5 or a club), and otherwise conserve chips. Action: check and see how Claude proceeds.

Short-stacked heads-up play requires aggression. With 8-10 on As-2c-7c, I have minimal equity, but Minni's flop check indicates potential weakness. A 250-chip bet applies pressure on her vulnerable check while staying fiscally responsible with my short stack. This is a position-based aggressive play designed to steal the pot or build chips with minimal risk given my chip deficit.

I’m OOP with Q♠5♣ on A♠ 2♣ 7♣ — no pair, only a backdoor (runner‑runner) club. Claude opened preflop and now c‑bet 250 into a 650 pot (~38% pot). Calling 250 into 650 means I need ≈27.8% equity (250 / (650+250)) — against a wide button opening and standard c‑bet range heads‑up that’s borderline but acceptable. Folding is too tight given the price and the opportunity to float; raising would bloat the pot out of position with very little fold equity. I’ll call to keep the pot controllable, see a turn, and re‑evaluate: fold to a strong turn barrel if I don’t improve, but continue/pressure if I hit a Q/5/club or Claude shows weakness (checks or small turn bet).

I have Q♠5♣ on A♠ 2♣ 7♣ 2♦ and I’m out of position with no pair and effectively only 6 outs to pair on the river (≈13% to hit). There’s no realistic flush/straight draw and the turn paired the board, so I have very little showdown equity versus a standard BTN c-bet/barrel range. Pot control is the priority: check and fold to meaningful aggression. I’ll only call an extremely small bet (roughly ≤ ~135 chips given the ~13% equity) — otherwise I release the hand. If Claude checks back I get a free river to see if I can improve or bluff.

With 8-10 offsuit on As-2c-7c-2d, I have a marginal hand with only ~20% equity (straight draw outs). Minni's turn check signals potential weakness, but betting marginal hands with limited equity from a short stack is not optimal. By checking, I get a free river while preserving my dwindling chip stack, and I maintain the option to make a strong hand if a 9 or J arrives. This conservative approach is better than burning chips on a speculative bluff when I'm already short-stacked.

Claude c-bet the flop, then checked turn and checked river — that line looks weak or contains a lot of missed draws (Kx/Qx, club draws, some medium pairs doing pot control). I have Q♠5♣ (queen-high) with effectively no showdown value, so checking just lets him show up with a value hand and I lose for sure. A small river bluff targets his missed-draw/medium-hand portion of his range while minimizing risk if called. Pot is 900; a ~1/3-pot bluff (300) only needs ~25% fold frequency to be profitable and is the right size to fold out Kx, Qx and failed club draws while keeping the bet small vs Ax/2x that will call. I’ll fire a small river bet (300) to take the pot down or get a cheap read if called. If he raises, I can fold and preserve most of my stack.

River fold with 8-10 offsuit on As-2c-7c-2d-6h. I have zero made hand (8-high only), facing a river raise from Minni who is likely representing a made hand. My equity is negligible (<20% against realistic holdings). Being severely short-stacked (10,810), I must be disciplined and fold marginal/weak hands when out of position and facing aggression. This preserves chips for better opportunities. The pre-flop and flop investments are sunk costs; the question is only about the 300 to call, which I cannot justify with this hand strength.

Heads-up with A♠ A♥ — the best possible starting hand. I have deep effective stacks (291 BB vs Claude’s ~107 BB) so I want to build the pot and isolate the BB while leaving room to play postflop and handle a 3-bet if it comes. A standard 3x-ish open (to 300 total) achieves that: it’s big enough to get value from weaker hands and discourage some pure floaters, but not so large that I fold out nearly everything or overcommit preflop. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate for a 4-bet or call depending on sizing and his tendencies; if he calls I’ll play the advantageous postflop position. Raise to 300 total.

Folding 2-9o from the big blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with no connectors and poor playability post-flop. While pot odds are reasonable at 2:1, the hand quality doesn't justify calling against an aggressive opponent from a short-stack position. Preserving chips for better opportunities is critical when playing 150 hands with stack management in mind.

A9o is a premium hand in heads-up play. With a significant chip disadvantage (10.6k vs 29.2k), I need to be aggressive with strong hands and build the pot. Standard 2.5x raise sizing from the button is appropriate here to gain fold equity and build value when called.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♠5♥ facing a small‑blind open to 250 (I must call 150 more). With deep stacks and Claude opening wide from the SB, A5o is a clear defend: it blocks many A combos and has decent equity vs a wide opening range. Calling keeps the pot controllable (calling 150 into a 350 pot means I need ~30% equity), lets me realize equity OOP and see a flop where I can play post‑flop deliberately. A small 3‑bet here would be too polar and I don’t want to play a bloated pot out of position with a weak kicker. Plan: call to 250 total (add 150); on an Ace on the flop I’ll play for value/protection, on dry boards I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy turn/river pressure.

Heads‑up in the big blind with A♠5♥ — I called preflop to defend vs a wide SB open. Flop K♣ 8♣ 6♥ misses me: no pair, no club, just Ace‑high and a useful Ace blocker. My priority is pot control and information OOP: check to keep the pot small and let Claude c‑bet a wide range so I can call small/medium bets for showdown or fold to very large pressure. If he checks back we take it to a cheap showdown; if he bets small–medium I’ll call (good blocker to Ax combos); I’ll fold to polarizing/overly large bets or shove attempts since I have little equity to continue out of position.

I'm betting 350 chips (70% of pot) with A9o on a 8c Kc 6h board after Minni checked. This applies pressure to likely weak holdings, takes advantage of my position, and builds value without overcommitting my short stack. With a significant chip disadvantage, I need to capitalize on position and hand strength to win pots and accumulate chips.

I defended preflop with A♠5♥ and checked the flop for pot control. Claude's flop bet of 350 into the ~500 pot (pot is now 850) means calling 350 commits me to a final pot of ~1200, so I need about 29% equity to break even. Against a wide SB opening/c-bet range this hand has roughly that equity — my Ace blocks many A combos and I beat a lot of Kx bluffs, floats and smaller pairs. Raising OOP would be too polar and create difficult decisions out of position; folding is too tight given the pot odds and opponent tendencies. I’ll call 350 to see the turn, keep the pot manageable, and then fold to heavy polarized pressure or continue vs small/medium bets; if I pair the Ace on the turn I’ll consider extracting value.

Check. I defended pre‑flop and called the flop, and the 9♠ on the turn is a blank — I still have only Ace‑high but I block many A‑x combos. With the pot at 1,200 and myself OOP, my priority is pot control and information: checking lets Claude barrel bluffs or small value hands so I can call manageable bets and fold to polarized pressure. Plan: if he checks back we go to a cheap showdown; if he bets small–medium (roughly ≤ half‑pot ≈ 600) I will call to realize equity and beat bluffs; if he fires a large bet/overbet or jams (polarized line) I will fold — I don’t want to play a bloated pot out of position with only Ace‑high. No check‑raise or lead here — too polar and risky OOP with a weak kicker.

Betting 700 on the turn (58% of pot) with A9o pair of nines. Minni's two checks indicate weakness, and I have a strong hand that likely wins against most hands in Minni's checking range. As a short stack, I need to extract value from weak holdings. This sizing extracts maximum value while staying manageable if I face aggression.

Call 700. I have A♠5♥ OOP on 8♣ K♣ 6♥ 9♠ and I already defended preflop and the flop. Claude’s turn barrel of 700 into the ~1,200 pot is a moderate size — after his bet the pot is 1,900 and calling 700 would put the total pot at 2,600, so I only need ~27% equity to break even (700/2600 ≈ 0.27). Against a wide SB opening and a c-bet/barrel line his range contains Kx value hands but also a fair number of floats/bluffs and weaker cards that I beat; my Ace blocks many A‑x combos and gives me reasonable showdown equity. Raising OOP with nothing but Ace‑high would be too polar and create difficult decisions; folding here surrenders good pot odds. Plan: call to see the river, fold to a large polarized shove, call or extract on a small river bet, and value bet or show if I pair up.

River A♥ pairs my A♠5♥, so I now have top pair with a weak 5 kicker. Claude bet flop and turn (showing aggression) but checked the river — this looks like pot control or weakness (Kx, missed draws, some middle pairs) rather than a polarized shove. Checking loses the chance to extract thin value from Kx/9x/Qx hands that will call a small bet. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) maximizes calls from worse hands while limiting my loss if he has a better Ace or a set and decides to raise. Pot = 2600; a bet of 900 is cheap for him to call (break-even for him ≈ 20.5%) and will be called by many second pairs and bluffs. My stack is deep enough that this is non-committal. Plan: make a small value bet to extract, and if he check-raises big I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarized large shove).

Calling with two pair (Aces and Nines). Minni's passive action on flop and turn followed by river aggression suggests a bluff-heavy range rather than a slowplayed monster hand. At nearly 5:1 pot odds, two pair has more than adequate equity against her likely holdings (bluffs, newly made Aces, or single pair of Kings). In heads-up play, especially as a short stack, I must defend strong hands like two pair. The risk/reward is favorable - if she's bluffing or has a weaker holding, I win 4400 chips. If she has me beat, I'm drawing thin but still have some equity.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the sb=50). Tc4c is a weak but playable heads-up hand — suited and with postflop playability — so I want to seize the initiative and leverage position. I’ll open-raise rather than limp to generate fold equity and avoid giving Claude cheap odds to see the flop. I size to 3x the BB (300 total) — larger than the min-raise (200) to discourage light 3-bets and to make postflop decisions easier; this size is small relative to our deep stacks but effective to win the pot preflop or play a favorable pot in position. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold or reassess based on sizing; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet good flops and proceed cautiously on dry boards.

6-9 offsuit is a weak hand that lacks strong playability, especially from the big blind out of position. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 400 pot), my equity against Minni's button raise is likely only around 30-35%, which barely justifies the call. Given that:

1) The hand has poor postflop playability when out of position
2) I have reasonable stack depth (12,810) with many hands remaining
3) Better opportunities will arise with premium hands
4) Minni is playing aggressively and I'd be in a marginal spot postflop

A disciplined fold preserves chips for higher-equity situations where I can play strong hands from better positions.

Calling the additional 50 chips with 67o in heads-up from the button. Getting 3:1 odds on a call, and position is a huge advantage postflop in heads-up play. This hand has potential and I should play a wider range from the button. The call allows me to see the flop cheaply.

I'm on the big blind with 3♣2♠ and Claude (button) just limped (called 50). Pot is 200 and I can check for free. 3-2 offsuit is very weak heads-up and has poor playability out of position. Claude has shown a tendency to limp/call rather than fold, so a small preflop raise would likely bloat the pot with a dominated hand. I'll check and see a flop cheaply; only invest more if I pair or pick up a reasonable draw or if I can exploit a clear weakness on the flop.

Check. With 3♣2♠ on a 5♠-6♦-8♣ flop I missed — no pair and no meaningful draw except a gutshot to a 4 (4 outs) plus 3s/2s to pair (6 outs). That’s limited and many of those hits (a lone 3 or 2) will still be weak versus Claude’s limp/call range. Pot is only 200 and I can see the turn for free, so betting here is a weak bluff: Claude tends to call and a C-bet will usually bloat the pot when I’m behind. Plan: check and try to realize equity cheaply. If the turn gives me the 4 (straight) or pairs in a way that’s likely best, I’ll start building the pot; if I miss and Claude bets a meaningful size I’ll fold; if he checks behind I may take a small stab on later streets or re-evaluate with any improvement.

I have 6-7 on a 5-6-8 flop, giving me a pair of sixes plus an open-ended straight draw (8 outs with any 4 or 9). This is a strong hand on this particular board. Minni's check from the big blind suggests weakness or a middling hand. In heads-up play, position is crucial, and I should leverage it by betting for value and building the pot with my strong draw and pair. A bet of 150 (roughly 3/4 pot) is a reasonable size that extracts value from weaker holdings while maintaining good pot control. The pot becomes 350, and I maintain a healthy stack to play postflop. This play is standard aggressive poker in heads-up when holding both a made hand and multiple draws.

Call 150. Pot is 350 and calling 150 gives me roughly 30% break-even equity (pot would be ~500 after my call). With 3♣2♠ on 5♠-6♦-8♣ I have a four-card straight draw to a 4 (4 clean outs) plus six lone-pair outs — raw two-card equity to the river is ~38%, though many pair-outs are “dirty” vs a made pair. Claude has a limp/call tendency and will call a lot of bluffs, so raising as a pure bluff here is thin and would bloat a pot OOP with a marginal hand. With deep stacks I prefer to realize equity cheaply: call to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression unless I hit the 4 (straight) or pair in a spot that’s likely best, and re-evaluate (or apply pressure) only if the board or action improves my hand or gives a clear fold equity advantage.

The turn 4h gives me a made straight: my 3♣2♠ with board 5♠-6♦-8♣-4♥ is a 2-3-4-5-6 (6-high) straight. This is a very strong hand heads-up and beats all single pairs, two-pairs and sets, but it is not absolutely the nut — any hand containing a 7 now has 4-5-6-7-8 and would beat me. Claude has shown a limp/call tendency but fired the flop (150) which indicates a fairly wide range of pairs and draws; he will call down with many worse hands. I want to extract value from top pair/two-pair/trip types and charge any remaining draw, while keeping the pot controllable vs the slimmer 7x combinations that crush me. Betting about half–two-thirds of the pot (~300 into 500) is the best line: it prices worse hands to call, discourages free cards that could help a 7 on the river, and leaves room to fold to a very large raise which would usually indicate a 7 or an unlikely overplay. Plan: bet 300 now; if he calls I will re-evaluate river for another value bet or a pot-control check depending on the river card and his action; if he raises very large I will proceed cautiously because his range will be weighted toward the few hands that currently beat me.

Calling 300 into an 800 pot with 6h-7c on 5s-6d-8c-4h. I have a made pair of sixes plus an 8-out open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 9). My equity is roughly 25-35% depending on Minni's actual holdings. Pot odds of 2.67:1 make this a reasonable call. In heads-up play, folding here too often would be exploitable. The river will likely decide the hand, and I maintain flexibility. After the call, I'll have 12,260 remaining in a 1,100 chip pot, preserving stack room for river action.

Turn 4♥ gave me the 6‑high straight (2‑3‑4‑5‑6). On the river 9♣ there’s very little that improves to beat me — the only hands that now beat my straight are any 7 in Claude’s hand (4 combos), while almost every one‑pair/two‑pair/set he would plausibly have (5x,6x,8x, pocket pairs, etc.) is still losing. Claude limped/called pre, bet the flop and called my 300 on the turn, a line consistent with a wide calling range that will call down with worse. Against that range I should be extracting value rather than checking; I’ll size about half the pot so marginal pairs and worse will call but I don’t overcommit if he raises (a large raise is likely a 7 and I can fold). Bet 600 into 1100 — if Claude calls I expect to be ahead; if he raises very large I will seriously consider folding to that show of strength.

Calling 600 with a made 9-high straight on the river. The straight is a strong hand with only trips and better possible. Minni's raise pattern (300 on turn, 600 on river) suggests they hit something, but in heads-up play, this could still include bluffs or weaker holdings they're playing aggressively. Pot odds of 1:3.8 require only ~21% equity to break even, and with a made straight I should have 30-40% equity accounting for bluff possibilities, tied hands, and occasional beats. Expected value is slightly positive, and folding here would be too cautious given the strength of my hand and pot odds available. Remaining stack of 11,660 provides sufficient room for future hands if I lose.

Heads-up on the button with 2♠4♣ — a very weak hand, but in heads-up play the button should open-raise a wide range to apply pressure and take the initiative. The pot is 150 and the minimum raise is to 200 (2×BB); raising to 200 is a small, standard steal size that buys fold equity and keeps me in position postflop. If Claude folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I play cautiously in position and will fold to heavy action on later streets unless I improve. If he 3-bets large I’ll usually fold this weak holding. Action: raise to 200 (total commit).

Folding 2dJc from the big blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker - an off-suit deuce-jack combination. While I'm getting decent pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot), several factors make folding correct: (1) I'm out of position post-flop, (2) I'm short-stacked at 13,860 chips, (3) Minni is playing aggressively from the button with a wide range, and (4) 2dJc will struggle significantly in any confrontation. With limited chips remaining, I should be reserving my stack for premium hands where I can make aggressive plays or have better equity. Folding marginal and weak hands is essential when short-stacked.

In heads-up play, I'm on the button with position and J-6 is a playable hand despite being marginal in full ring games. The cost to call is just 50 additional chips (already committed 50 to the SB). Minni has been aggressive throughout but also shown a wide pre-flop range in previous hands. With position, I can control the action post-flop and exploit my positional advantage. If the flop misses or is unfavorable, I can fold without further commitment. My stack is shorter than Minni's (13,810 vs 26,040), so I need to pick reasonable spots to build chips rather than play marginal hands out of position, but here the combination of position, reasonable odds, and heads-up dynamics makes a call appropriate.

Heads-up vs Claude (button) who just limped (called 50). I’m in the big blind with J♠4♣ — a marginal hand but playable heads-up. Checking is an option, but against a limp I want to seize initiative, apply pressure and either take the pot preflop or define Claude’s range. A small, minimum raise to 200 (total) is cheap relative to our deep stacks and achieves that: it gains fold equity, narrows his range, and gives me clearer information going to the flop. If Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP — c-bet on dry, uncoordinated boards and check/ pot-control or fold to strong aggression on coordinated or face-card heavy boards. I’ll fold to a large 3-bet and re-evaluate if he jams given stack sizes.

Heads-up play on the button with J6o. Minni raised to 200 from the BB after I called 50. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), which means I only need ~25% equity to break even. Against Minni's likely wide BB range, J6o has reasonable equity (approximately 40-45%). Key factors: (1) Significant positional advantage—I can control the action post-flop and exploit position; (2) Favorable pot odds making this profitable; (3) Heads-up dynamics encourage wider playing ranges from position; (4) Stack situation (13,760 vs 25,940) requires accumulating chips, and this is a reasonable spot to do so. If the flop is unfavorable, my position allows me to fold cheaply. The combination of odds, position, and necessary chip accumulation makes calling the correct play here.

Flop: Jc 9d 6d. I have J♠4♣ — top pair but a weak kicker. Heads-up vs a limp-caller Claude, his range is wide (many broadways, small/medium pairs and diamond draws). I raised preflop to take the initiative; I should continue to apply pressure and extract value while denying free cards to diamond draws. Betting about 60% pot charges flush draws and gets value from worse pairs/9x/6x and weaker Jx hands, while still allowing me to fold to strong action. Plan: bet to build the pot and define his range; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (pot-control or barreling depending on the card); if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to a strong made hand and fold this marginal-kicker top pair rather than bloating the pot.

Flopped two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on Jc-9d-6d is a very strong holding in heads-up. I have significant equity advantage against Minni's likely range. Rather than passively call 250, I should raise to 650 to build the pot while ahead and apply pressure. This play accomplishes: (1) Gets value from weaker hands Minni may continue with; (2) Applies aggression to a strong hand; (3) Helps close the chip gap (13,660 vs 25,690) by winning a larger pot; (4) Is still manageable stack-wise with 13,010 remaining. The raise is well above the minimum raise and represents a natural sizing for aggression.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (J♠4♣) on Jc‑9d‑6d. I c‑bet ~250 to charge draws and get value, and Claude check‑raised to 650, leaving me 400 to call into a 1,300 pot (≈24–25% required equity). That sizing and the prior limp/call line point strongly to a value‑heavy check‑raise (sets 66/99, strong Jx, occasional two‑pair) with some combo draws mixed in. OOP with a thin kicker, I’m dominated or have poor odds vs most of that value range and would be committing into larger streets with little fold equity. Given the stack depths and my earlier plan to fold to a big check‑raise with a marginal top pair, I fold and preserve stack equity for better spots.

Heads‑up on the button with A♥2♠ is a strong open-raising hand — I want initiative and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (standard ~3xBB) rather than limp or just call the 50. This size is large enough to discourage cheap flops and to isolate Claude in position, but small enough to leave plenty of post‑flop maneuvering room with deep stacks. If he calls I can c‑bet many flops for value/bluffs; if he 3‑bets I can re‑evaluate given stack depth.

Folding 5-2 offsuit from the big blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal equity. Although I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call for a 400 pot), 5-2o has only ~25-30% equity against Minni's raising range from the button. The expected value is negative. With 14,210 chips and a deep stack still available, there's no reason to gamble with this trash hand. Minni has been aggressive but I should only invest with significantly better holdings.

Heads-up on the button with 9h5d. While this is not a premium hand, position on the button in heads-up play is critical and provides significant fold equity. Looking at Minni's aggressive playing style throughout the tournament, a standard raise is warranted here.

Current situation: 95 of 150 hands (63% through tournament), chip stacks relatively close (I have 14,160 vs Minni's 25,690). We need to play aggressively from the button to accumulate chips. 

From the button heads-up, standard raising strategy calls for opening a much wider range than early position full-ring play. 95 is definitely part of this range. A raise to 250 (2.5x the BB) is appropriate - it's neither too large nor too small, gives us fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if called.

Post-flop, we'll have position and can play this hand effectively against Minni's wide range with our positional advantage.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button open to 250. I only need to call 150 into a 350 pot — calling 150 to win 500 requires ~30% equity. Tc7o is a marginal but perfectly defendable hand heads-up: it can make top pair, has reasonable connectivity and decent implied odds with deep stacks (my 25.7k vs his ~13.96k). Raising to 400 is just a small OOP 3-bet that gains little fold equity and will lead to difficult postflop decisions; folding is too tight and would let the button open-profitably. I’ll call the 150 (total committed 250) and evaluate the flop: continue with top pair or good equity/draws, and fold to large aggression without improvement.

Heads‑up on the big blind vs a button open. Pot is 500 and the flop A♦ 9♦ 6♣ completely misses Tc7♥ — no pair, no useful diamond, only a very weak backdoor club possibility. Claude’s open-range from the button contains lots of A‑x, broadways and pairs, so my equity to continue as a bluff is poor and bloat the pot OOP would be a mistake. I’ll check and keep the pot small; fold to meaningful pressure unless I pick up a real piece of the board or the bet is tiny (in which case I might call for pot‑odds), but I won’t bet/raise with this airball.

Betting with top pair (pair of 9s) on a flop of 9d Ad 6c. I was the preflop aggressor and have position. Minni checked, indicating a weak hand, draw, or slow-play. A standard continuation bet of 200 (40% of pot) is appropriate here to:
1. Take down the pot against weaker hands
2. Charge drawing hands the proper odds
3. Maintain my betting lead and define her hand
4. Build the pot with my marginal-but-playable top pair

This bet sizing is neither too aggressive nor too weak given my chip stack (13,960) and the current pot (500).

I checked the flop with Tc7h and Claude fires a small c-bet: 200 into a 700 pot. That sizing gives me ~22% pot‑odds to continue. My hand has essentially no showdown value vs a button opening range heavy in A‑x and broadways, but calling 200 is cheap and keeps weaker bluffs in his range. I can realize a small amount of equity (a Ten for a pair or a backdoor club) and attempt a float/turn bluff if a favorable card or a check from him appears. I’m out of position so I won’t inflate the pot or raise without improvement — I’ll call the 200 to see the turn and plan to fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair up or pick up a clear draw.

Turn pairs my 7 (Tc7h → board 9♦ A♦ 6♣ 7♣). Claude c‑bet a small 200 on the flop and then checked the turn — that sequence shows a lot of weakness/pot‑control and a range that contains many missed broadways, medium pairs and some A‑x that are pot‑controlling. I improved to second pair and should seize the initiative: a value/protection bet denies him a free river and extracts calls from worse hands (Kx, Qx, 9x, small pairs and bluffs) while folding out some pure air. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot to keep it cheap enough that worse hands call but large enough to charge floats — pot is 900, so I’ll bet ~450. If he raises strongly I’ll respect that and fold (my hand is only second pair OOP); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river for thin value or a bluff. Total committed after this bet will be 900 chips.

Calling the turn check-raise with pair of 9s. At 3:1 odds, I have sufficient equity to justify the call. While Minni's action suggests strength (likely improved hand with the 7 or strong draw), my pair has merit and I'm not dominated in all scenarios. In heads-up play, this is a standard call with a marginal hand getting favorable pot odds. Folding would be too tight and give up too much fold equity. I can reassess on the river depending on her action.

I hold Tc7h — second pair (7s) with a 10 kicker on 9♦ A♦ 6♣ 7♣ 2♦. Pot is 2,700 and Claude (button) has ~12.9k. He called my pot-sized turn bet, which strongly narrows his range to many made hands (Ax, 9x, 66/sets occasionally) that beat my pair. The river 2♦ is mostly a blank for me (only very specific combos like A2 improve him). Betting here for value will fold out most worse hands and get called by almost all hands that beat me, so it’s likely negative EV to fire again OOP. I’ll check and take the showdown or induce a thin bluff. Plan if he barrels: call small river bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 900) to catch bluffs/weak pairs, but fold to medium/large river bets since his turn-call frequency and sizing indicate strength. Therefore: check now.

River check with pair of 9s. Minni check-raised aggressively on the turn but checked the river, indicating either a vulnerable hand or uncertainty. Given my shorter stack (12,860 vs 24,440) and marginal hand strength (pair of 9s on a 9-7-A-6 board), checking to showdown is the prudent play. This avoids risking additional chips on a hand that may not be best while maintaining the option to win if my pair holds. In heads-up play when shorter-stacked, preserving chips and going to showdown with reasonable holdings is standard strategy when opponent shows weakness via a check.

Heads-up on the button with 5d4s (offsuit). In heads-up play the button opens very wide — this hand is marginal but playable from position and I want to seize the initiative. With deep stacks a standard 2xBB open is appropriate to generate fold equity while leaving room to maneuver postflop. Action plan: raise to 200 to pressure the BB; if Claude folds I take the blinds. If he calls I’ll play the flop in position — c-bet selectively on dry/high-card flops (smaller sizing) and check/controll on coordinated boards. If Claude 3-bets big (a sizable 3-bet/shove) I will fold this marginal offsuit; if he 3-bets a small size I may call given deep stacks and implied odds. Overall goal: use position and aggression to accumulate chips without getting into large, marginal OOP confrontations.

Calling with 2d-Ts in heads-up BB against button raise. Getting 3:1 pot odds (100 to call into 300 pot). In heads-up, button ranges are typically wide, and I need to defend BB adequately. While 2-10 is weak, it has straight potential and reasonable post-flop playability. The pot odds make this a defensible call rather than an exploitable fold.

With 2d-Ts on a Kd-Kc-4c flop, I have absolutely nothing - no pair, no draws, not even backdoor possibilities. This hand completely whiffed. In this spot, I should CHECK rather than bet. Since I have zero equity, betting accomplishes nothing positive: if Minni folds, fine (though that's not the likely outcome from a button raiser's strong continuation range), and if Minni calls, I'm in a bad spot. By checking, I retain the option to fold if Minni fires, or check down river if Minni also checks. This is a hand I called light pre-flop for post-flop potential, but the board provided exactly zero help. Heads-up play rewards checking weak hands with no equity and letting the opponent take the initiative when they likely have more of the board. I'll reassess after seeing Minni's action on the flop.

I have 5♦4♠ — bottom pair (4s) on K♦ K♣ 4♣. Claude checked to me; in heads-up calling ranges to a 2x open are wide but are heavily weighted toward Kx and broadway hands that are ahead of my pair. A c-bet here has little fold equity against Kx and will mostly get called by better hands or overcards, so betting would risk building a pot I’m often behind in. With deep stacks relative to this 400-chip pot, I want pot control and to use position to see another card. Plan: check to keep the pot small, call small/medium flop bets to re-evaluate on the turn, and fold to a large sizing. If Claude checks back I can take a stab on safe turn/river spots or extract value when the board improves (e.g., a 5/3), but I won’t force the issue with this marginal made hand.

On the turn with 2d-Ts on a Kd-Kc-4c-As board, I have zero equity - no pair, no draws, and the ace hasn't helped my hand at all. Minni's flop check after her pre-flop raise is telling: she likely either missed (QQ-TT range) or is playing cautiously. Betting here makes no sense - I have no legitimate hand strength and minimal fold equity. By checking, I keep the pot small and retain the option to fold to any river aggression or check down if Minni also checks. The river card might provide a ten for a pair, but until then, this hand has no business taking the lead. Checking is the disciplined, mathematically sound play that minimizes my losses on a complete whiff.

I check. On Kd Kc 4c As I only have bottom pair (4) with a weak 5 kicker. Claude checked flop and checked the turn — that range is often Kx, Ax, or wide holdings that will rarely fold to a turn bet. With pot 400 and deep stacks (my 24,240 vs his 15,360) betting has very little fold equity and just builds a pot I’m usually behind in. I’ll control the pot in position, see the river and re-evaluate (look to fold to large river bets unless I improve or Claude gives a clear bluffing line).

On the river with 2d-Ts against a final board of Kd-Kc-4c-As-Ah, I have zero equity and no showdown value. The entire hand has been checked down through flop and turn, indicating both players are surrendering. Betting would only result in getting called by better hands - I have no legitimate hand strength and virtually no fold equity at this point. Checking maintains pot control and allows me to reach showdown without investing additional chips on a hopeless hand. This is disciplined minimization of losses.

Board is K♦ K♣ 4♣ A♠ A♥ — the five community cards already make two pair (Aces and Kings) with a 4 kicker. My 5♦4♠ does not improve me beyond the board; I have no full house or trips. Any opponent holding an A or K has a full house on this river and beats me outright. Claude’s passive line (check-check) is consistent with Kx/Ax slowplays or a hand content to show down; betting has almost no fold equity and will mainly get called by better hands. Pot is small (400) and stacks are deep, so the lowest-variance, most +EV line is pot control — check to showdown and fold to a sizeable river bet if faced with one.

In heads-up play from the button/small blind with J4o, calling is the appropriate action. While J4 is a weak hand, heads-up ranges are significantly wider than full-ring poker. The pot is currently 150 with 100 already in from the big blind, so I'm getting 3:1 odds to call an additional 50 chips. This is reasonable compensation for the hand's moderate equity. Folding would be too tight in a heads-up situation, while raising would overcommit with a marginal hand. I have position post-flop and can leverage it. Minni has a chip lead (24,140 vs 15,710), so I need to accumulate some pots, but with the right selection and execution rather than by overplaying weak holdings.

Heads-up vs a limp from the button (Claude). I have 8♠2♠ — the worst of the suited hands: some backdoor potential but very weak and easily dominated. Pot is already 200 and there’s no price to see the flop (to_call = 0). Facing a limp from the small blind I could raise to try to take it down preflop, but I’m out of position postflop and the hand plays poorly OOP. With deep effective stacks I’d rather keep the pot small and see a cheap flop to realize equity or fold to strength. Plan: check and evaluate the flop — fold to heavy aggression if I miss and opponent shows strength, call small bets on benign textures, and only invest further with real equity (pair/strong draws).

Flop is Kd Kc 5h — paired kings. I have 8s2s: no pair and only a remote backdoor spade possibility (no spade on board). Pot is small (200) and there is no price to see the turn. Out of position vs a limp/caller, I want pot control and to avoid putting chips in with virtually no equity. I’ll check to see the turn and fold to significant aggression, call small probing bets on harmless turns, and only continue if I pick up real equity (a pair or a strong draw).

Checking behind with J4o on a double-king flop (Kd Kc 5h). This hand has minimal equity and no draws. Betting commits chips with a losing hand. By checking, I see the turn for free while preserving chips for better spots. With a chip deficit, I must be disciplined in hand selection and pot commitment.

Turn pairs my deuce (I have 8♠2♠ and the turn is 2♣) so I now have a weak second pair with a very poor kicker. Claude limp‑called pre and has checked flop and turn — that line can be either a slowplayed big hand (a King) or a wide/passive holding. Out of position and with deep stacks I want pot control and more information rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll check. If Claude bets a small probe I can call to realize my equity (I have 5 outs: two remaining deuces and three eights) but I’ll fold to large pressure since many hands still beat me (any K, pocket 5s, etc.). If he checks through to the river I’ll re-evaluate and either value bet a safe river or check down to showdown depending on the card.

Checking on the turn with J4o on KdKc5h2c. This hand has no showdown value. Minni has shown weakness with two consecutive checks, but betting J4 as a bluff would be burning chips with a marginal hand. Checking preserves my stack while seeing the river for free. With a chip deficit, disciplined hand management is critical - I should only commit chips when I have better equity or stronger holdings. Folding is unnecessarily tight post-check-check, so checking allows the hand to proceed to river where I can reassess.

River gives me 8♠2♠ → board K♦ K♣ 5♥ 2♣ 2♥, so I’ve made trips (three deuces). That’s a very strong hand vs a wide, passive limp/call line, but it’s vulnerable to full houses/quads (any K in Claude’s hand is KKK22; the lone remaining deuce would give quads; pocket 5s would also be a full house). Claude limp‑called preflop and checked through, which points to a fairly wide/passive range containing many hands worse than my trips (Ax/Qx/mid pairs, single 5s, etc.). Pot is tiny (200), so the +EV line is to extract value with a small river bet rather than check down and risk letting worse hands show for cheap or overvalue hands win. I’ll make a half‑pot value bet (100) to get called by worse holdings while keeping the risk limited. If Claude raises large, that line will be highly polarized toward hands that beat me, and I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large, polarized raise).

Folding J4o on the river facing a raise on a double-paired board (KdKc5h2c2h). This hand has zero showdown value and cannot beat any legitimate holding in Minni's range. The check-check-raise line on this runout suggests a made hand (likely a pair or better). Even accounting for bluffs, J4 has insufficient equity to justify a call. With a chip deficit, I must be disciplined and avoid calling with marginal/worthless hands. Preserving chips for better opportunities is critical in this heads-up endgame scenario.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Q♠J♥ — a strong, playable hand that performs very well in position against a wide big-blind calling range. I want to take the initiative, get value and fold equity, and play the flop in position where I can c-bet or control pot size. A standard 3x BB-ish open is appropriate: raise to 300 total (top up from my SB 50 to 300). If Claude calls, I'll play postflop aggressively on favorable textures and proceed cautiously to significant 3-bets — folding to very large 3-bets or re-evaluating by sizing. Overall: raise to seize initiative and maximize profitable hands from position.

QhAc is a premium hand in heads-up play. From the big blind facing a button raise, 3-betting is the correct play - it applies pressure immediately and is well-positioned to take down the pot or continue with equity. Raising to 900 (3x her raise) is a standard aggressive sizing that balances value and fold equity.

Claude 3-bet to 900 after my standard button open to 300. I hold Q♠J♥ — a very playable heads-up hand in position. With deep stacks and no clear read to justify a polarized 4-bet bluff, QJoffsuit is better played by calling: a 600 call is cheap relative to the pot and lets me realize equity and use positional advantage postflop. A 4-bet for value is unrealistic (I’m often behind) and a 4-bet bluff is too risky without a specific read. Plan: call to keep the pot controllable, c-bet favorable dry flops, and check/control or fold to heavy aggression when I miss. Call to 900.

With QhAc on Kh-5h-8d, I have ace-high with a powerful 9-card flush draw. From out of position after 3-betting pre-flop, checking is superior to betting because: (1) my hand has significant equity and can improve, (2) I can evaluate Minni's action before committing more chips, (3) this keeps the pot manageable while I'm out of position, and (4) my drawing hand benefits from seeing another card without inflating the pot unnecessarily. Minni's call of my 3-bet suggests a medium-strength hand, and checking gives her opportunity to either barrel or give up while maintaining control of the hand.

Claude 3‑betted preflop to 900 and checked this K♥5♥8♦ flop. I have Q♠J♥ in position — a hand with decent equity and blockers plus a very small backdoor heart possibility. This is a dry, uncoordinated board that misses a lot of his 3‑betting range, so seizing the initiative with a small continuation bet is standard. I will bet ~1/3 pot (600) to (1) take the pot down now vs many A‑high and medium hands, (2) get value from worse hands that will call a small barrel, and (3) keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large check‑raise (give credit to strong made hands from his 3‑bet range), and if called use position to re‑evaluate and continue or control on the turn depending on the card.

I have QhAc on Kh-5h-8d, facing a check-raise to 600 from Minni. This is a strong semi-bluff situation with excellent equity: I have a 9-card flush draw (outs to the nut flush) plus an ace-high that could be best currently or improve further. My equity here is approximately 40-45% against most of her range (including KK, AK, KQ, or weaker holdings). The pot is 2400 with only 600 to call, giving me favorable 4:1 pot odds for a drawing hand. Being out of position, I want to keep the pot manageable rather than escalate the action, especially since I can improve to the flush, two pair, or trips on the turn. Calling allows me to: (1) realize my draw equity efficiently, (2) make a more informed decision on the turn after seeing another card, (3) avoid inflating the pot while OOP without complete information about her holding. If I hit the flush or an ace on the turn, I'll be in a strong position to extract value.

With 11 outs (9-card flush draw + 2 non-flush ace outs) on the turn giving approximately 22-24% equity, checking is the correct play. Minni's check after a flop check-raise suggests weakness or a cautious hand. Given my out-of-position status with a drawing hand, taking a free card to the river is superior to betting into this spot with marginal equity. If I hit my flush or pair of aces on the river, I'll have a made hand to extract value. If I miss, I've preserved chips by not bloating the pot with a drawing hand OOP.

I hold Q♠J♥ on K♥5♥8♦3♠. Claude was the preflop 3‑better and he check‑called my small 1/3‑pot c‑bet on the flop, so his continuing range is polarized toward Kx, Ax, medium pairs and strong broadways — many hands that currently beat me. I have no pair and only 6 outs to pair by the river (~13% equity), and the turn 3♠ didn’t add any meaningful draw. Betting the turn would bloat the pot and will be called by most of his continuing range, putting me in difficult spots; a small stab might fold out some A‑highs but is low EV given his line. Best play is pot control: check and see the river for free. If I pair the river (Q or J) I can extract value; if the river is scary and he shows aggression I can fold.

On the river with pair of aces (A-A-K-Q-5), I have a strong hand against Minni's likely range. Her flop check-raise followed by turn and river checks suggests she doesn't have a premium holding. This is a straightforward value-betting opportunity - she likely has something like KK, KQ, AK, or other medium-strength hands that will call a reasonable bet. Betting 1500 (50% of pot) is standard river value sizing to extract chips from her weaker holdings while maintaining good pot control given my out-of-position status throughout the hand.

Claude 3‑betted preflop (tight/strong range: AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK, AQ, some Kx) and then check‑called my small 1/3 pot c‑bet on K♥5♥8♦ — a line consistent with Kx, overpairs or AX/strong broadways with showdown value. Turn checked through. The river A♥ now improves a large chunk of his continuing range (AK -> two pair, any A in hand -> a pair of Aces, plus he can still have Kx/pairs) and the board has three hearts (only a two‑heart hole combo makes a flush). His 1,500 into 4,500 (~33%) is a thin value size that will be called by most of those hands. I hold Q♠J♥ with no pair (only a single heart blocker). Calling 1,500 to win 6,000 requires ~25% equity; against Claude’s value‑heavy betting range my equity is well under that and I’m only beating air/bluffs and a few tiny combos. A raise as a bluff is low EV here — I don’t block enough of the two‑heart flush combos and a raise will rarely fold out the hands that currently beat me. Fold.

Raising to 250 total (150 more) with QcAs from the button against the big blind. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively. The raise is 2.5x the big blind, appropriate for positional aggression. I'm building the pot with a strong hand while maintaining fold equity and controlling the action postflop from a favorable position.

Heads-up in the big blind with T♦6♠ facing a 250 open from the button (SB). In heads-up I should defend a wide range from the BB — T6o is marginal but has reasonable equity vs a wide SB opening and plays well on many flops. The pot is 350 and I must call 150, giving me ~30% immediate pot odds; against a likely wide opening range I clear that threshold. Stacks are deep (effective ~16.9k), so implied odds exist if I hit. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a dominated, marginal hand and bloat the pot — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity or fold cheaply to extreme aggression. Plan postflop: continue with top pair/two pair/strong draws, fold to heavy continued pressure on dry, unfavorable boards, and look for spots to bluff-catch or bluff on later streets when appropriate. Therefore call to see the flop.

Heads‑up out of position on a dry, paired flop (5♦5♠2♠) with T♦6♠ — I completely missed. Claude opened wide preflop, so his range includes many hands that beat me (5x, 2x, pairs) and hands that will fold to aggression, but betting OOP here bloats the pot and is vulnerable to check‑raises. I have only a weak backdoor spade and no pair, so my best line is pot control and to realize equity. Check and evaluate Claude’s action: call small bets to see turn or fold to heavy pressure unless I improve to a pair/strong draw.

Strong continuation bet on a dry paired flop. I have QcAs (premium hand with two overcards to the 5-5-2 board). Minni's check from the big blind after calling my preflop raise suggests weakness. The paired board texture limits the range of strong holdings my opponent likely has. With the initiative and positional advantage, a standard c-bet of approximately 70% of the pot (350 into 500) is appropriate. This bet accomplishes several things: (1) maintains my aggressive posture from preflop, (2) builds the pot when ahead against most hands, (3) gets value from weaker holdings, and (4) applies pressure that forces my opponent to make a decision. If called, the Ace and Queen provide outs if I'm behind, and I can continue evaluating on the turn based on Minni's response.

Heads‑up in the big blind with T♦6♠ on a paired, dry flop 5♦‑5♠‑2♠. Claude (BTN) opened wide preflop and now made a small c‑bet of 350 into the 850 pot. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade possibility (would need runner‑runner), so my hand’s value is largely in realizing equity and leveraging implied odds. Calling 350 into 850 requires ≈29% equity (350/(850+350)=29.2%). Versus a wide c‑betting range Claude can have many missed hands and thin value hands, so my actual equity is around that threshold — calling is the standard, low‑variance play to see a turn. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand, fold out worse hands, and commit chips unnecessarily against an aggressive opponent who can shove or continue; folding throws away reasonable odds. Plan: call now to see the turn; fold to heavy turn aggression if I still haven’t improved, but continue/raise for value if I pair or pick up a legitimate draw.

Check. I have T♦6♠ on 5♦-5♠-2♠-8♥ — I completely missed: no pair, no real straight or flush draw (a flush is impossible from here), and the 8♥ is a blank. I’m out of position with a marginal hand and the pot is already 1,200. Betting OOP would bloat the pot and expose me to a check‑raise or calls from better hands where I have virtually no equity. Checking controls the pot, keeps Claude’s weaker hands and bluffs in play, and lets me fold to sizable aggression. Plan: if Claude leads small on the turn (a probe bet ~≤1/3 pot) I can consider a call to see the river; if he fires a larger bet I’ll fold. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate on the river and look for a targeted bluff only on a suitable scare card.

Betting 600 on the turn (50% of pot) to continue my aggressive preflop/flop line. Minni's turn check after calling the flop c-bet suggests moderate strength or weakness. With QcAs (two premium overcards), I have solid equity against most of their likely holdings. This bet: (1) maintains my aggressive narrative, (2) applies pressure to fold marginal hands, (3) builds the pot when ahead, (4) tests their actual hand strength. If called, I'll have more information about their holding for the river decision. The blank 8 on the turn makes this continuation credible and unlikely to arouse suspicion given my established aggressive play.

Out of position with T♦6♠ on 5♦‑5♠‑2♠‑8♥ — I have no pair and no realistic draw. Claude barreled the turn for 600 into a ~1,200 pot (pot is 1,800 now); calling 600 requires roughly 25% equity (600/(1800+600)=25%). I only have three outs (the remaining tens) to pair on the river (~3/46 ≈ 6.5% immediate equity), far below the needed threshold. Raising as a bluff is thin — I block nothing meaningful and would face calls or a shove from many hands in his range (5x, 2x, 8x, overcards that continue). Given his line (wide SB open, small flop c‑bet then a half‑pot turn) he can easily have many value hands and enough bluff frequency to be profitable, but my hand has no realistic chance to realize sufficient equity. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button/SB with 6♠5♦: this is a playable, connected hand that does well postflop and benefits from playing in position. Limping is too passive heads-up and gives Claude cheap equity/initiative; I want to seize initiative, apply fold equity, and play the flop in position. I open to 300 (3x the big blind) — larger than the min-raise to pressure Claude’s wide BB calling range while still keeping the pot manageable relative to our deep stacks. If Claude calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and control the pot on dry boards; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size and board texture (fold to large 3-bets, consider calling small ones for implied odds).

Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and I'm in the big blind with 4c9h. This is a weak, offsuit hand with limited equity against Minni's button-raising range. Key considerations: (1) I'm out of position - a significant disadvantage post-flop; (2) 49o has poor equity against most reasonable ranges (likely 30-40% at best against Minni's wide button range); (3) Risk/reward is unfavorable - I'd need to call 200 more with a weak hand out of position; (4) Both stacks are healthy, so there's no desperate chip situation requiring a stand. With 50 hands remaining in a 150-hand tournament and solid chips, this is an optimal spot to fold and preserve my stack for premium hands where I have better position or stronger holdings.

In heads-up play on the button with K8o, I'm in an excellent position. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to call (150 in pot, 50 to call), and K8 is well above the average hand for heads-up play. With 101 hands remaining in a 150-hand tournament, I should be applying consistent pressure from the button.

I'm not raising to a premium amount—just a modest raise of 100 chips on top of my small blind, bringing my total to 150. This accomplishes several things:
1. It avoids giving away my hand strength with a large raise
2. It applies pressure in position
3. It keeps the pot manageable while maintaining control
4. It's balanced enough to play hands of varying strength

The opponent (Minni) has slightly more chips (22,240 vs my 17,610), so I need to be aggressive when I have reasonable holdings and position. Folding K8o from the button in heads-up is simply leaving chips on the table.

I have A♦7♦ in the big blind facing a small, 2bb open to 200. Heads-up A7s is a strong hand against a wide SB opening range and plays well post-flop, but I don’t want to let Claude see cheap flops too often out of position. With deep stacks (≈222bb vs ≈175bb) I can 3-bet for value and fold equity. I’ll 3-bet to ~3x (raise to 600 total): big enough to charge weaker aces, Kx and medium pairs, narrow Claude’s opening range, and take the initiative preflop. If he 4-bets big I can reassess (fold to extreme aggression), but vs a standard wide opener this sizing maximizes EV compared to a simple call for 100.

Facing a 3-bet to 600 with K8o, I need to carefully evaluate the situation. Current pot odds are 2:1 (800 to call 400), which is tighter than the 3:1 I initially referenced. While K8o is above-average for heads-up play, it's marginal when facing aggression. Against Minni's 3-bet range (which likely includes premium pairs, broadway cards, and broadway-heavy holdings), K8o has approximately 35-40% equity—not quite enough to justify the 2:1 call profitably in isolation. Additionally, while I have positional advantage on future streets, K8o is a weak holding that will struggle on most flops against a 3-bet range. With 49 hands remaining and reasonably deep stacks (not in push-fold territory), I can afford to be selective. This is a spot where folding and preserving chips for premium holdings or better spot selections is more profitable long-term. I can apply pressure on hands with more strength or better implied odds.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Js7d — a marginal but playable hand in position. In heads-up play I should be opening wide to seize initiative and generate fold equity. A standard 3xBB open (raise to 300 total) pressures the BB, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me play postflop in position. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold Js7o; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops, realize equity when I hit a J/7 or backdoor draws, and use position to control pot size. Calling 50 is too passive and surrenders initiative, so I raise to 300.

2-9 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While the pot is offering 2:1 odds to call (400 chips in the pot, 200 to call), this hand cannot win without significant improvement. More critically, I'm out of position (in the BB) facing a continuation bet post-flop, and Minni has shown throughout this session that she's aggressive and skilled in continuation betting. The positional disadvantage combined with the poor equity of 2-9o makes this an easy fold. I should preserve my stack and wait for a premium hand where I have better equity and positional advantage. With 17,360 chips and 48 hands remaining, folding marginal hands is the correct long-term strategy.

Folding 2-3 (worst hand in poker) despite favorable pot odds. While heads-up play is aggressive, 2-3 is legitimately unplayable even in this context. The small blind already committed (50) is a sunk cost. Preserving chips for premium hands is more valuable long-term than speculating with the worst hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 7♣3♠. In heads-up play the button should open an extremely wide range — even bad offsuit hands can be used to apply pressure and take initiative. Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap, but raising to the minimum (2x BB, 200 total) maximizes fold equity, lets me play postflop with position when called, and preserves table image as an aggressive player. This hand has poor showdown equity against a 3-bet, so I’ll fold to sizeable 3-bets, but versus a single caller I’ll c-bet selectively on favorable flops and play cautiously when checked to or facing big resistance. Raise to 200 to seize initiative and try to steal the blind/pot.

In heads-up play, I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to call only 100 chips with 7♠5♠, a suited connector. Minni is playing a wide range from the button based on recent hands. The small call size and excellent pot odds justify calling to see the flop, where I can play my hand based on board texture and action. Folding would be too tight in this heads-up scenario.

On this flop with 7♠5♠, I have a pair of sevens, which is a marginal holding on this dry 4♣7♦2♦ board. With no strong continuation opportunities and a relatively weak pair, checking is the prudent play. This keeps the pot small with my marginal hand and allows me to see what Minni does. Since I'm in heads-up play and Minni is the aggressor, checking gives me the opportunity to react to her aggression or take down the pot if she checks behind. If Minni bets, I'll be able to make a decision based on her bet size and recent tendencies. Checking also allows me to realize the strength of my hand in a relatively controlled manner.

I raised preflop to take the initiative and the flop gives me top pair (7) with a weak 3-kicker on 4c-7d-2d. Heads-up top pair is often best and Claude’s check is consistent with a wide range of weaker hands or floats/draws. I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge overcards/diamond draws while keeping control of pot size. I’ll c-bet for value and protection — sizing to roughly half the pot (200 into 400) to keep worse hands and draws calling but retain fold equity. If I face a large check-raise or heavy turn aggression I’ll re-evaluate and fold if it’s clearly a strong hand; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on diamond/overcard turns.

On the flop with 7♠5♠, I have a pair of sevens on a dry 4♣7♦2♦ board. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (600 to call 200), which is favorable pot odds for calling with this hand. The board texture is very dry with minimal draws, limiting Minni's likely holdings to other pairs or weak high cards. In heads-up play, Minni is c-betting aggressively from the button with a wide range of hands. Calling keeps the pot manageable while maintaining my showdown value and gives me the opportunity to reassess on the turn. My pair of sevens has reasonable equity against Minni's likely range of continuation bets. Folding would be too conservative given the pot odds and board texture.

On the turn with 7♠5♠ giving me a pair of sevens, Minni checked after being aggressive preflop and on the flop. Her turn check indicates weakness - likely a missed draw on this three-diamond board or a marginal hand. My pair of sevens is probably the best hand here. In heads-up play, exploiting weakness is crucial. I should value bet to extract chips from her weaker holdings. A bet of 400 (roughly 50% of the current 800 chip pot) is standard value betting sizing that builds a pot while remaining measured if I'm wrong. This also keeps my implied odds favorable if she calls.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop; I currently have top pair (7) with a weak 3‑kicker. The turn (6♦) brings a three‑diamond board (7♦2♦6♦), so Claude’s turn bet of 400 is consistent with a range that includes made flushes (two diamonds), single‑diamond draws, sets/two‑pair, and weaker pairs/floats. Pot odds are favorable: calling 400 into the 1,600 pot gives ~4:1 (need ~20% equity). Against his wide calling/calling range from earlier streets my TP+weak kicker has enough equity to continue, and raising here is unattractive — it bloats the pot with a vulnerable hand and will get value‑raised or committed by hands that already beat me. I’ll call the 400 to realize equity and keep worse hands and bluffs in; on the river I’ll fold to heavy action if a diamond completes the obvious flush or if he fires a large overbet, and re‑evaluate small river bets for thin value.

On the river with 7♠5♠ giving me a pair of sevens, checking is the optimal play. I've already extracted value with my turn bet of 400, and the river deuce doesn't improve my hand or change the narrative. My pair of sevens is a marginal showdown hand that doesn't warrant additional chips. Checking allows me to see if Minni will bet or check behind, giving me information while preserving chips. If she checks behind, I win the pot. If she bets, I can reassess, but it keeps the pot manageable with a hand that's not strong enough to justify building it further.

The river paired the board and my 7♣ now makes two pair (7s and 2s). That’s a substantially stronger hand than the single top pair I had on the flop/turn and it beats a lot of Claude’s checked range (missed diamond draws, single-pair hands, floating hands, small pocket pairs like 4s/6s). Claude’s line (call preflop, call flop, turn aggression) does allow for strong holdings (completed diamond flushes, a deuce in hand making trips, or very strong pairs), but he checked the river — which often signals pot control or a missed draw rather than a full-value hand. Against this checked range I should extract thin value rather than just check back and give up value. I don’t want to bloat the pot versus a possible flush or trips, so I’ll choose a modest value bet to keep worse hands and bluff-catchers calling while folding out nothing important. Betting 600 into a 1600 pot (~37% pot) is a good compromise: it gets called by worse one-pair hands and some missed-draw floats, doesn’t overcommit vs a potential made flush/trips, and preserves stack for future hands. If Claude folds I win the pot; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold to large river aggression because many of his raise combos (a deuce or flush) beat my two pair.

River call with pair of sevens. Getting 4.67:1 pot odds (need only 17% equity) makes this a mathematically sound call. Minni's turn check-call followed by river bet suggests a marginal hand—likely a weaker pair, missed draw, or bluff. My pair of sevens has decent showdown value and well exceeds the equity threshold for profitability. In heads-up play, river bluff frequencies are high enough that folding would be unnecessarily tight. The risk-reward is favorable given the pot odds.

Heads-up play from the button with K8o. In heads-up poker, the button has a positional advantage and should play a wider range of hands. King-Eight offsuit is a reasonable hand to play from this position. Rather than just calling, I'll raise to 200 (min raise to) to apply pressure on Minni's big blind and control the pot. At 17,260 chips, I can afford to build the pot with reasonable holdings, and the positional advantage on future streets makes this an appropriate aggression. The raise forces Minni to make a decision and allows me to win chips without going to showdown or to see the flop in position with initiative.

Heads-up, button/SB (Claude) opened to 200. I’m in the BB with QcJc — a strong, playable hand heads-up. Calling 100 is safe, but I prefer to 3-bet to seize initiative and extract value from worse broadways/pairs while folding out weaker hands. A raise to 700 total (~3.5x his open) applies meaningful pressure vs his likely wide SB opening range, avoids giving him cheap odds (a min‑raise to 300 is too small), and is well within our comfortable stack depth. If he folds we take the pot; if he calls we proceed OOP with a c-bet on favorable flops and exercise pot control or fold to heavy resistance on very hostile action. Therefore I 3-bet to 700.

K8o is a marginal hand, and I'm facing a 3-bet out of position. While the button position normally warrants wider play in heads-up, my initial min-raise to 200 with K8o was not a strong opening strategy, and now facing Minni's re-raise to 700, continuing with this weak hand would put me in a difficult spot post-flop where I lack position and have a hand that easily gets outdrawn. The 200 already invested is a sunk cost. With a healthy stack of 17,110, folding here preserves my chips for stronger opportunities where I'm in a better position (positional or hand-strength-wise). This is a disciplined fold against a 3-bet with a marginal holding.

Heads-up on the button with 4♣3♥ and deep stacks — this is a hand I can open profitably. I want to seize initiative and pressure the big blind, so I’ll make a standard preflop open from the button. Raising to 300 (total) is a normal 3xBB sizing at this table: it maximizes fold equity, keeps my opening range wide, and lets me play postflop in position. If Claude calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold this weak holding or re-evaluate based on sizing.

Folding 5-6 offsuit from the big blind against Minni's raise. This is a weak hand that lacks both connectivity and strength. While Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament and this is a heads-up situation with reasonable pot odds (200 to call into 400), several factors make folding correct: (1) I'm out of position post-flop, (2) my hand has poor equity against Minni's raising range, (3) I have a stack that's still healthy at 17010 chips - no need to flip with weak holdings, and (4) better spots will come. At this stage of the tournament (hand 106 of 150), preserving chips and playing strong hands is more valuable than engaging in marginal situations out of position with weak hands.

2-3 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. Even in heads-up play where hand values are broader, this hand has poor equity and minimal potential. With my stack at 16,960 (already behind Minni's 22,890), I need to preserve chips for premium or solid hands. The cost to call is minimal (50 chips), but committing chips with such a weak holding post-flop would put me in difficult positions. Folding here is the mathematically sound play to maintain chip preservation strategy in this heads-up tournament situation.

Heads-up on the button with K♦2♦ (king with backdoor nut-flush potential) — this is well inside a standard opening range. I have position and a deep stack (22,990) vs Claude (16,860), so taking the initiative preflop is profitable: I can apply pressure, fold out weaker hands, and c-bet many flops. The required call is small (50) but calling surrenders initiative; a min-raise to 200 is cheap relative to the stacks and builds the pot when I’m likely ahead of Claude’s calling range. If Claude 3-bets big I can re-evaluate (K2s is not a hand I want to go all-in with vs a large 3-bet), but open-raising here maximizes fold equity and playability postflop.

In heads-up play, I need to defend my big blind aggressively with reasonable hands. 7-8 of hearts is a connected, suited hand with good potential - it can make straights and flushes. I'm getting 3:1 odds (300 in pot, 100 to call), which is favorable for a speculative hand. Minni has been raising frequently throughout the tournament, suggesting a wide range from the button. The hand plays well post-flop and I'm in position to act after the flop. Folding here would be too passive in heads-up play. A re-raise with this hand risks committing too many chips with a marginal holding given my shorter stack (16,860 vs 22,840). Calling allows me to see the flop and make decisions based on the board texture.

I have two pair (eights and sevens with K kicker), which is a strong hand on this K-7-8 flop. My 7-8 of hearts has connected very favorably by making both a pair of sevens and an eight, plus I retain a flush draw (any heart). Minni's check indicates she likely missed or has a weak hand, given her aggressive button play earlier. Betting 250 into the 400-chip pot (60% pot bet) is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker holdings while managing my stack prudently in heads-up play. This bet builds the pot with a strong hand and applies pressure on Minni's likely weak range.

Flop: Kh 7s 8c. I have K♦2♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Claude’s 250 into a 650 pot is a relatively small c-bet. In heads‑up play with position I prefer pot control and to keep worse Kx and draws in the hand rather than inflate the pot with a marginal kicker. Raising risks isolating myself against better kings or committing to a big pot I’m not comfortable with; calling lets me see a turn and use position to extract value or fold to heavy aggression. I will call the 250 and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn with a full house (sevens full of eights), I'm betting 1,200 into the 900-chip pot. This is a value bet that builds the pot while keeping Minni's calling price reasonable. The turn brought a second 7, dramatically improving my hand from two pair to a premium full house. Minni's check indicates a weak holding, likely a missed draw or marginal pair. Betting aggressively with the best hand on turn allows me to maximize value and edge closer to all-in in a favorable spot before the river. Stack sizes (16,510 vs 22,590) support this aggressive but measured approach.

Pot is 2,100 and Claude’s turn bet is 1,200 — I must call 1,200 to win ~4,500, so I need ~26.7% equity. I have K♦2♦: top pair but a terrible kicker, and the turn paired the board (7♠ → 7♣), which massively improves holdings that beat me (any 7, any 8 that had an 8, K7, or KK/77). My clean outs are tiny: two remaining kings + three remaining deuces = 5 outs max (5/46 ≈ 10.9%), and several of those outs won’t even beat trips/full houses/quads. Claude’s line (call preflop, small flop bet, much larger turn bet after the pair) looks value‑heavy and polarized toward hands that dominate me. Given the pot odds required (~26.7%) vs realistic equity (well under that), folding is the +EV play — preserve stack and wait for a clearer spot.

In heads-up play, position is critical and the button has a significant advantage. While 2-10 offsuit is a weak hand in absolute terms, I'm only risking 50 more chips to call and see the flop (the pot would be 200 with 150 already committed). Heads-up poker requires a much wider calling range from the button than in multi-way pots. Even marginal hands can be profitable with proper post-flop play and position advantage. The pot odds are favorable (4:1 to call 50 into 200), and my position will allow me to control the action after the flop. Folding weak hands too frequently from the button would be exploitable by an observant opponent.

Heads-up vs a limp from the button. I have 5h6c unsuited — a weak, low connector that plays poorly out of position. Claude’s limp is wide and I can often raise, but with this particular holding I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP where I’ll be dominated a lot. I’ll check and see a flop cheaply, realize equity with straights/pairs, and only invest further with clear equity or a strong draw; fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

I have 6♣5♥ on 3♣9♣6♥ — middle pair (sixes) with a weak kicker and a club blocker. Claude limped preflop, so his range is very wide (overcards, small pairs, suited connectors, club draws, occasional 9x). Out of position I want to control the pot with a marginal made hand and realize equity — betting OOP here just bloats the pot and turns me into a call-or-fold spot against aggression. I’ll check and re-evaluate based on Claude’s action: call small-to-medium bets (keep worse hands and floats in), fold to heavy pressure or large raises that represent strong made hands/club combos. If checked around I’ll consider a small turn stab or slow-play to extract value on safe runouts.

With 2s-Tc on 3c-9c-6h, I have only T-high with weak backdoor draws. My hand lacks sufficient equity to bet profitably. By checking, I take advantage of position to see the turn card inexpensively. This preserves chips when weak and allows potential improvement (hitting another 10, though unlikely to win at showdown with a weak kicker). Minni's flop check suggests I may have reasonable equity despite my hand's weakness, so a free card is valuable here.

Claude limped preflop and has checked both flop and turn — a very wide, passive line. I hold second pair (6♣ with a 5 kicker) and a club blocker to some flush combos. The turn paired the board (3♣/3♥), so trips are possible but his passive lines make that less likely. I want to extract value from worse pairs/turned floats and fold out pure overcards while keeping the pot manageable OOP. A small half‑pot bet (100 into 200) is the right sizing: it gets called by many worse hands and denies free river cards to some draws. If Claude raises big I’ll give it up — this hand is too vulnerable OOP to continue vs heavy aggression. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

Folding 2s-Tc on 3c-9c-6h-3h turn after Minni's raise to 100. T-high has minimal equity against her turn-raising range in heads-up play. The paired board and her aggressive turn action suggest her range has improved or contains strong semi-bluffs. With no draws and poor equity (~15-20%), this hand cannot justify a call despite 3:1 pot odds. Preservation of chips with a marginal holding is the profitable long-term approach.

Heads-up on the button with A♣T♣ — a very strong, playable hand. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to see a flop, but raising gives me initiative, fold equity and isolates the BB's wide defending range. I size to 300 total (i.e. 250 more chips on top of my 50 SB) — big enough to fold out weaker hands and get value from worse Ax and broadways, but not so large as to overcommit vs a deep-stacked opponent. Plan: if called, use position to c-bet favorable textures and extract value; if 3-bet, re-evaluate based on sizing, using the Ace blocker and club suit as factors for calling or folding. Raising is the +EV play here.

I'm holding 4♥3♠, one of the weakest hands in poker. While Minni has been aggressive from the button, 4-3 offsuit is simply unplayable from the big blind, even against a standard raise. I have no reasonable equity, poor position, and no upside. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities. With 40 hands remaining and a reasonable stack (17,210), I should maintain discipline and avoid marginal situations.

Heads-up button position with 97o warrants aggression. This hand is above the calling threshold and benefits from positional advantage. A 3x raise (300 total) is standard for taking the initiative in heads-up play, allowing me to win the pot immediately or continue with position postflop. Minni has shown aggression throughout, so establishing my own aggression here is strategically sound.

Facing a button (SB) open to 300 heads-up with K♦Q♣ in the big blind. KQo is a strong heads-up hand and the opener from the button is likely wide; calling would leave me OOP and let him see cheap flops with position. I want to seize initiative preflop and get fold equity while building a pot with a hand that plays well if called. A sizable 3-bet (to 1,200 total) denies cheap flops, prices out some speculative hands, and still leaves plenty of stack to play postflop. If Claude 4-bets large I can fold and preserve chips; if he calls I plan to continue aggression or evaluate based on the flop texture.

Heads-up call with marginal hand 97o is justified by: (1) button position advantage postflop, (2) adequate pot odds of 2.67:1, (3) roughly 30-35% equity against typical 4-bet range, (4) ability to play small ball poker and fold to aggression when lacking equity. Position is critical in heads-up and allows for profitable plays with marginal holdings when able to control action postflop.

I 3-bet preflop to 1,200 and my range here is fairly strong but still wide; Claude’s cold-call to my 3-bet indicates a wide, often speculative calling range (broadways, small/medium pairs, suited connectors, some spades). The flop (9♠‑6♠‑5♥) is relatively dry texture for continuation bets — there are two spades, but no paired high cards and very few strong made hands that dominate my K♦Q♣. My hand has two overcards and decent equity vs his likely range, so I should seize the initiative and apply pressure rather than check and let him dictate the action in position. A half‑pot c-bet (~1,200 into 2,400) gives good fold equity vs underpairs and air, prices spade draws correctly, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue if I pick up pair or a favorable card, pot‑control or fold to strong aggression if he shows strength).

I hold top pair (9d 7s on 6s 9h 5s flop) - a strong holding in heads-up. Minni's small bet (1200) into the 3600 pot is likely a continuation bet or testing bet.
