My top pair gives me significant equity advantage against most hands in Minni's range. Raising to 3600 (3x bet) accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) extracts value from weaker holdings, (2) applies pressure to force Minni into difficult decisions, (3) takes advantage of positional strength. The raise size is proportional and shows conviction without overcommitting. In heads-up play where aggression is rewarded, raising with top pair is the clear value play.

I 3‑bet preflop with K♦Q♣ and c‑bet the dryish 9♠‑6♠‑5♣ flop to seize initiative. Claude’s check‑raise to 3,600 is a strong line but can be a mix of top pair (9x), medium pairs, and a lot of spade semi‑bluffs from his cold‑call preflop range. Pot is 7,200 and calling costs me 2,400, so I need ~25% equity (2,400/(7,200+2,400)). KQ has sufficient equity vs a wide check‑raising range (and reasonable equity vs spade draws) to justify a call and keep the pot from getting out of hand OOP. Raising here with only overcards would bloat the pot and put me in uncomfortable spots against a polarized continuing range. Plan: call to see the turn — if I pair K/Q or see a harmless card I’ll seize initiative; if a spade completes or Claude barrels heavily I will fold to strong action.

I 3‑bet preflop and called Claude’s flop check‑raise with K♦Q♣ because I had two overcards and decent equity. The turn 4♣ is a harmless, low card that doesn’t complete obvious draws and Claude checked it — a sign that a lot of his check‑raise range missed this street. I want to seize the initiative and fold out weak pairs/floaters while denying free rivers to draws. Betting ~half the pot gives good fold equity without overcommitting OOP: pot is 9,600 so I size to 4,800. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river (and fold to a large raise, since I’m only on overcards).

Calling 4800 with top pair (9-7) on a 6-9-5-4 board. The 3:1 pot odds justify the call against Minni's range, which likely contains many hands I'm ahead of (draws, medium pairs, AK). While Minni could have flopped a set, my top pair is strong enough in heads-up play to warrant continuing. The call preserves my remaining stack (7610) while keeping the pot competitive. On the river, I'll have clearer information to make a final decision based on the board completion and Minni's action.

I built the pot with a preflop 3‑bet and continued on the flop and turn, but the river 2♠ completes a three‑spade board (6♠‑5♠‑2♠) and strongly helps Claude’s perceived check‑raise/call range. His lines (open -> cold‑call 3‑bet -> big flop check‑raise -> call turn) are consistent with many value hands (9x, medium pairs) and some two‑spade combos that now make a flush. I hold K♦Q♣ with no spade — essentially just two overcards and very little showdown equity. To bluff here I’d need Claude to fold a large fraction of his range; a shove would need ~28–29% fold frequency to break even given the pot (19,200) and his stack (7,610), which is unlikely after his turn call. Betting risks being called by better hands or crushed by a flush; checking preserves chips and lets him show or bluff first. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling a tiny pure‑bluff sizing).

River value bet with top pair (9-7). Minni's turn aggression followed by river check strongly indicates weakness - likely a missed draw or marginal hand. My pair of nines is strong here and has significant value. Betting 4K (roughly 1/5 of remaining stack) extracts value from Minni's range while applying pressure. This bet size creates a decision point without overcommitting, allowing me to preserve chips if called. The river 2s doesn't improve drawing hands and likely hasn't helped Minni's range. Value extraction with top pair is the standard play in this spot.

Board is 6♠‑9♥‑5♠‑4♣‑2♠ and I hold K♦Q♣ (no spade). Pot is 23,200 and Claude bet 4,000; calling would cost 4,000 for a break‑even equity of ~4,000/(23,200+4,000) ≈ 14.7%. Claude’s line is very value‑heavy: cold‑called my preflop 3‑bet, check‑raised the flop big (3,600), then called my large turn bet (4,800). The river 2♠ completes a three‑spade board, so many of Claude’s plausible continuing hands now have a flush (or already had strong pairs like 9x/6x). My KQ offsuit has almost no showdown value on this river except vs pure air/bluffs. Given the earlier aggression and the completed spade, the frequency Claude is bluffing is likely well below the ~15% threshold, so a call is a losing long‑term play. Raising as a bluff would risk a large portion of my stack into a range that beats me and is unlikely to fold enough. I preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range and take the initiative. Q♦5♣ is a marginal holding but is fine to raise in position to pick up the blind or play a controlled pot in position. I’ll make the standard min-raise to 200 total — it gives me fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with a mediocre hand. If Claude 3‑bets big I’ll fold; if he calls I can use position to continue or fold based on the flop texture.

Minni is short-stacked (12,990 chips) and raising from the button with a wide range due to chip pressure. I have J3s in the BB, which is a reasonable holding for defending against a button raise. At 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), I have sufficient equity against Minni's likely wide raising range. J3s is playable post-flop with position knowledge - I can evaluate on the flop and potentially bluff or value bet depending on the board texture. Folding here gives up profitable equity. This is a standard BB defense call.

On the flop with J♥3♥ against Minni's wide raising range from the button. I have a pair of 3s with a Jack kicker - a decent but not dominant hand on a 7♠3♣8♠ board. Minni is critically short-stacked (12,990 chips) and desperate to accumulate chips. By checking, I accomplish several things: (1) I let Minni take the betting initiative as a short-stacked player who may want to commit chips, (2) I gather information about his hand strength, (3) I avoid building a pot when ahead against his likely range of unpaired hands and overcards, and (4) I keep flexibility for future streets. If Minni bets, I can evaluate his likely holdings (likely hands like underpairs, air, or draws) and decide whether to call or fold based on pot odds. If he checks, we get a free card which is valuable given the two-spade board and potential flush draws. Checking is more profitable than betting into a wide range from a desperate short stack.

Heads‑up on the button I opened a very wide range preflop so I have a range advantage to c‑bet a dry flop. Board is 7♠‑3♣‑8♠ — fairly dry but with two spades. I have Q♦5♣ (only one overcard, no spade), so this is not a hand I want to build a big pot with, but it’s good to take the initiative and pick up the pot or charge draws. Claude checked, which often means a miss or a weak draw in his calling range. I’ll make a standard half‑pot continuation bet (200) to get fold equity, deny free cards to spade/runner runner draws, and keep the pot manageable. Plan: if he folds I scoop the 400; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and will be willing to fold to sizable aggression since I currently have only Q‑high.

Calling 200 with J3 hearts against a short-stacked desperate player on a 7-3-8 board. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds and have a pair of 3s with jack kicker plus backdoor flush draw. Against Minni's wide range, this is profitable to continue. My stack allows me to potentially stack him if favorable cards come on turn/river. Checking then calling is the right approach here - I gathered information and now am continuing with reasonable equity.

Turn action with J♥3♥ on 7♠3♣8♠4♣. I have a pair of threes with a jack kicker, which is likely the best hand against Minni's desperate wide range from the button. The 4♣ on the turn doesn't improve my hand but also doesn't damage it.

Key factors:
1. Minni is critically short-stacked (12,790 chips) and desperate to accumulate chips
2. My pair of 3s is likely ahead of his range of overpairs, air, draws, and unpaired hands
3. The turn brought a blank that doesn't change the dynamics much
4. Checking gives Minni a free card and allows him to get creative on the river
5. Betting builds the pot when ahead, extracts value from his desperation, and puts real pressure on his short stack to make a decision

A bet of 400 balances value extraction while still giving Minni room to make a marginal call with weak holdings. This gets money in while ahead and takes control of the hand. Against a short stack playing wider ranges, betting for thin value is more profitable than checking and allowing free cards.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the dry 7♠‑3♣‑8♠ flop, but on the turn (7♠ 3♣ 8♠ 4♣) I only have Q♦5♣ — no pair, no real flush, and a one‑card gutshot to a 6 plus three queens to pair. That’s about 7 outs (~15% equity). The pot is 1,200 and Claude’s turn bet is 400, so calling 400 risks 400 to win 1,600 (≈20% required equity). My actual equity (<≈15%) is well below the price, and Claude’s line (check‑call flop then lead turn) is consistent with many made hands or household semi‑bluffs that continue applying pressure. A raise would be an unnecessarily thin bluff against a deeper, aggressive stack. Folding preserves my stack and lets me wait for a better spot in position.

Call 50 more to complete the 100 chip commitment to see the flop. Heads-up play allows wider hand ranges from the button, and Js4d offers decent equity with position advantage. Favorable 4:1 odds justify the call. I can leverage positional advantage post-flop to make decisions based on board texture and opponent action.

Heads-up, the button (Claude) just limped/called — a sign of a wide, often weak range. Q♦8♣ is playable in this spot and has good blocker/value vs limp-heavy ranges. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll make a standard preflop raise to isolate and gain fold equity. If he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll continue as the preflop aggressor (c-bet favorable flops, pot-control on marginal boards). I’ll be ready to fold to a large 3-bet shove but can call or play postflop against a small 3-bet depending on sizing and reads.

Heads-up play dramatically widens hand ranges. With Js4d on the button, I'm getting 4:1 pot odds (400 pot, 200 to call) which provides sufficient equity to call. While J4o is marginal in general, position on the button is a significant advantage in heads-up play. The Jack gives me a premium card, and the four provides backdoor opportunities. Minni's aggressive raising pattern suggests a wide range, meaning my hand has reasonable equity. Post-flop, I can leverage position to make better decisions based on board texture and Minni's action. The favorable odds and positional advantage justify the call.

I was the preflop aggressor vs a limp/call range — Claude will have a lot of weak hands but also many Ax and some clubs that connect with this flop (Ac 9c 5s). Q♦8♣ has no pair but has a backdoor club angle and reasonable blockers to some draws; I don’t want to give Claude a free card and I want to fold out pure air and small K/QXs. A standard continuation bet is appropriate: I’ll bet about half the pot (300 into 600) to apply pressure and keep the pot controllable. Plan: if he folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll pot-control on the turn and re-evaluate (check behind to realize equity, or raise only with a clear improvement); fold to a sizable raise since my hand has poor showdown value vs an aggressive response.

Call 300 to see the turn. Pot odds of 4:1 justify this call—I need only ~20% equity and J-high should exceed that against Minni's wide heads-up range. Position on the button is a critical advantage, allowing me to make informed decisions post-turn based on board texture and Minni's action. While Js4d is marginal in absolute terms, heads-up dynamics and opponent tendencies make this a profitable continue.

I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop, but Claude flat‑called. The turn 6♥ is a blank for Q♦8♣ — my backdoor club route is gone and I have only queen‑high. Claude’s flop‑call range (lots of Ax, some 9/x or small pairs and occasionally draws) dominates or calls a turn bet, so betting now would bloat the pot with very little fold equity and poor equity myself. I want to control the pot, avoid getting raised off a marginal holding, and see a cheap river to reevaluate. Check and plan to fold to large aggression or call small river bets as a bluff‑catcher when appropriate.

Minni's turn check suggests weakness. With Jack high and no viable remaining draws, checking is superior to betting because: (1) it allows me to see the river for free, (2) it avoids committing more chips with a marginal hand, (3) it preserves pot control, and (4) it gathers more information about Minni's hand strength. I retain the option to respond to river action based on whether I improve.

I check. On the river I only have Q♦8♣ (queen-high) on Ac 9c 5s 6h Kh and the pot is 1,200 with no bet to face. Claude’s line (limp/call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, check turn) is consistent with a lot of Ax and some small pairs or marginal hands; many of those hands beat me or will call a river bluff. A river bluff here has limited fold equity against his likely range, and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with virtually no showdown value. Checking realizes whatever equity I have, keeps the pot small, and lets me fold to real aggression (I’d only consider calling a very small river bet as a bluff‑catcher — otherwise fold).

Minni's back-to-back checks (turn and river) indicate weakness in a heads-up scenario. My Jack-high has minimal showdown value, making a check-and-hope strategy inferior to betting for fold equity. A 500-chip bet (40% of the 1200-chip pot) applies appropriate pressure without over-representing my hand. This sizing is consistent with value betting a marginal hand and exploits Minni's weak action. In heads-up play, aggression in response to weakness is profitable. If called, I lose most showdowns, but the fold equity here is significant enough to make betting profitable.

I checked the river with only queen‑high after c‑betting the flop and controlling the turn. Claude’s line (limp/call pre, call flop, check turn, small 500 into 1700 river) is most consistent with thin value hands — Ax and Kx — which dominate my Q♦8♣. Calling 500 into a 1700 pot requires ~22.7% equity (500 / (1700+500) ≈ 22.7%). My actual showdown equity versus Claude’s likely value-heavy range is well below that, so calling would be a losing play long‑term. A raise would be a large, high-variance bluff against a range that often has me crushed. I preserve stack and fold this marginal bluff‑catching spot.

Heads-up on the button with 7♥8♥ and deep stacks (≈121bb). This is a very playable, suited connector that benefits from initiative and position postflop. I want to take the lead, deny Claude free flops with his wide BB defending range, and build a pot I can play from position — so I’ll raise rather than just complete. I choose a standard 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) — bigger than the minimum to get more fold equity but small enough relative to stacks to keep maneuverability. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often fold or proceed cautiously); if he calls I’ll use position to extract value or apply pressure on favorable flops.

In heads-up play with a significant chip lead (27,710 vs 11,890), I need to defend my big blind aggressively. While K♥2♣ is a weak hand, the button raise is standard in heads-up dynamics. Calling 200 more chips allows me to see the flop with position and stack leverage. If I hit the King or find a profitable spot post-flop, my superior stack gives me significant leverage. Folding here would be too passive and allow Minni to run over the remaining hands.

I hold K♥2♣ on a K♣Q♠7♠ flop, giving me top pair but with a dangerously weak deuce kicker. While my pair is likely ahead of Minni's current holdings, this board has significant vulnerability: the flush draw (Q♠ and 7♠) creates multiple ways for Minni to improve, and my kicker leaves me beaten to any Q or 7 he might hold. With a significant chip advantage (27.5k vs 11.9k), I don't need to maximize value from marginal holdings—instead, I should minimize risk. Minni's check indicates likely weakness or a middling hand. Betting here could invite him to raise with a draw or marginal pair, putting me in a difficult spot. Checking allows me to see the turn safely while controlling pot size, keeping my chip lead intact. This defensive approach with an inferior kicker is sound, especially out of position in heads-up play where stack preservation matters as much as accumulation.

Heads-up on the button with 7♥8♥ on Kc Qs 7s. I’ve paired my 7 (middle pair) on a relatively dry, two-overcard board and Claude checked to me — a sign of weakness from his BB calling range (could be air, overcards, or a small pair). I want to take the initiative in position to fold out pure overcards and collect value from worse pairs and some draws while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. I’ll make a standard continuation bet of ~50% pot (300). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (barrel on safe blanks or check/control vs dangerous cards); if he raises large I’ll concede this medium-strength hand and fold.

Calling 300 with K♥2♣ on K♣Q♠7♠. While I have top pair, my weak deuce kicker is vulnerable to any Q or 7 in Minni's range. However, my pair is likely ahead at this moment. Given my significant chip advantage (27.5k vs 11.9k), I can afford to play this safely by calling and seeing the turn. The pot odds (3:1) are reasonable for a pair. Calling keeps the pot manageable and avoids overcommitting with a weak kicker out of position. If the turn is blank, I can reassess; if it improves Minni's hand visibly, I have enough chips to control the situation. Folding would be too passive against this short-stacked aggression, and raising risks getting bluff-raised with my vulnerable holding.

With K♥2♣ on K♣Q♠7♠J♣, I have top pair but a dangerously weak deuce kicker. The J♣ turn card is concerning, as it could have improved Minni's range significantly (Q-J, J-x, or gutshot straights). Betting here would invite raises with draws or marginal holdings, putting my vulnerable top pair in a difficult spot. Given my substantial chip advantage (27.2k vs 11.6k), defensive play with stack preservation is correct. Checking allows me to see the river safely and control pot size while protecting my chip lead. If Minni bets, I can evaluate his aggression, but checking now is the prudent decision against his likely range on a turn that probably helped him.

Turn is Jc on Kc Qs 7s and I hold 7h8h — middle pair with an 8 kicker. Claude called my flop c-bet then checked the turn, which looks like pot control/weakness or a marginal made hand. The Jack is a fairly dangerous card (completes broadway straights like A-T or 9-T and helps many hands that currently beat me: Kx/Qx/Jx), so barreling here would bloat the pot with a medium-strength hand and expose me to check-raises or river jams. With deep stacks I want to control the pot and keep the initiative to the river where I can re-evaluate. I’ll check to induce bluffs or get a free showdown on safe rivers; I’ll call small river bets for showdown value but fold to large pressure unless I improve.

I hold K♥2♣ on a completed board of K♣Q♠7♠J♣T♠, giving me one pair (Kings) with a dangerously weak deuce kicker. The river card (T♠) is particularly concerning as it completes the high end of the straight (Q-J-T-9-8). My pair is highly vulnerable to Minni's range, which could contain Q-J, J-9, 9-8, or other made hands.

Given my substantial chip advantage (27.2k vs 11.6k), I don't need to extract maximum value or even win this specific pot. My priority is managing risk and preserving my lead. Checking accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) it allows me to get to showdown cheaply if my pair is best, (2) it avoids the risk of a raise that would put me in a difficult decision with a marginal hand, and (3) it lets me evaluate Minni's river action. If Minni bets, I can assess the bet size and his likely holdings before deciding whether to call or fold. Betting with K♥2♣ on this board would be overly aggressive and invite difficult situations. Checking is the prudent defensive play that preserves my chip advantage while minimizing downside risk.

Board K♣ Q♠ 7♠ J♣ T♠ — I have middle pair (7h) with an 8 kicker. Claude called my flop c‑bet, then checked both turn and river. That line looks like weakness or pot control (Kx/Qx/Jx/some marginal pair) rather than a big value/aggro hand. The river T also completes dangerous broadway straights (A‑10 or 9‑10) that beat me. Betting here would be thin — most hands that would call a river bet (K/Q/J/T, two‑pair, straights) already beat me, and many bluffs that fold are limited. I want to keep the pot small, induce a bluff, and take a free showdown with a marginal hand. Action: check. If Claude now fires a small river stab I’ll call for showdown value; I’ll fold to large river jams.

Raising with 9-8 suited connector on the button in heads-up play. I have a significant chip lead (2.5:1), positional advantage, and should apply pressure to Minni's short stack. A raise to 250 (2.5x the BB) is a standard aggressive play that takes advantage of my advantages and forces Minni to make a decision with her short stack.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♠3♥ facing a button open to 250. Pot is 350 and I need 150 more (total commit 250). That’s a relatively small call for a deep-stacked heads-up spot and I only need ~30% equity to justify a call. Claude’s button opening range will be wide, so A3o has good defensive value and blocks Ax combos. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a weak kicker and inflate a pot I’ll have to play out of position; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity or use postflop skills. Plan: call to see the flop; on A-high or dry flops look for value/continuation opportunities, on coordinated or highly unfavorable flops proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

I have A♠3♥ on Q♠3♠K♣ — a weak made hand (pair of threes) with the nut-spade blocker and a backdoor spade/fold-out possibility. Claude opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains a lot of Kx/Qx/overcards that currently dominate me, but it also contains many hands I beat or can get to fold. Out of position and with deep stacks, I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than build a bloated pot with a vulnerable hand. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs/c-bets, and call reasonable single-street bets; I’ll only continue to big aggression if I improve (another 3 or an Ace) or get correct pot odds vs a small c-bet.

Continuation bet on the flop with OESD. Minni checked, indicating weakness. With a 2.5:1 chip lead and position, I should apply pressure. The bet size of 350 wins the pot immediately if she folds, or I retain equity with my 8 straight outs if called. Heads-up aggression with fold equity is the correct play here.

Call 350. I have A♠3♥ on Q♠3♠K♣: second pair with the Ace blocker. Claude opened wide on the button so his c‑bet range contains lots of Kx/Qx/overcards but also many bluffs and small pairs I beat. Calling 350 into the current pot (pot would be ~1,200 after my call) requires ~29% equity — reasonable versus a wide heads‑up c‑bet range. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll check‑call to realize equity and see a turn; fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, and only start raising/putting big pressure if I improve to trips/ace or pick up a very favorable runout.

I hold A♠3♥ on Q♠ 3♠ K♣ 9♠ — second pair (threes) with the Ace kicker and a one-card nut-spade draw (9 outs, ~19.5% to hit the flush on river). Claude opened the button (wide) and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains lots of Kx/Qx, some spade combos, pocket pairs and bluffs. The turn spade makes the board much more coordinated and increases the chance he already has a spade or completed a stronger hand. I’m out of position with a marginal made hand — I don’t want to bloating the pot with a vulnerable holding.

Plan: check and control the pot. Checking lets me call a reasonable turn bet (keeping worse hands and bluffs in), and fold to large turn raises that polarize to flushes/trips/bigger hands. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and look to extract a small value bet on a safe card or check down on dangerous cards. For now: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid committing with second pair on a three-spade board.

Bet 600 on the turn with a pair of nines. Minni's passive checking indicates weakness, and my aggressive line (raise pre-flop, c-bet flop) should continue to apply pressure. With a 2.5:1 chip lead in heads-up play, I should exploit her short stack and weak tendencies. A bet of approximately 50% of the pot (600 into 1,200) is balanced - strong enough to win the pot immediately if she folds, fair value extraction if she calls with a weaker holding, and maintains my positional and stack advantage.

Pot is 1,800 and Claude has bet 600 — I need ~25% equity to make a break-even call. I hold A♠3♥: a pair of threes with the nut-spade blocker and a four‑to‑the‑nut flush (9 spade outs) plus additional clean outs to improve (two remaining threes and three remaining aces). Naively that's ~14 unique outs (≈30% on the river), and even after discounting combinations where his hand already has a flush this is comfortably above the 25% threshold. Claude opened wide from the button and has been c-betting; his turn stab is a relatively small sizing that can include a mix of value and bluffs. Out of position with a marginal but equity‑rich holding, I prefer to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity rather than inflate the pot with a raise. Plan: call the 600 to see the river; if I hit a spade/A/3 I will shift to value extraction, if I miss I will check and fold to large river aggression while calling smaller river bets selectively.

I have A♠3♥ on Q♠ 3♠ K♣ 9♠ 4♠ — with the Ace of spades in my hand this is the nut (A‑high) flush. Claude checked the river, so he either missed or is showing down a lower spade/medium pair; very few hands can beat my holding (only a rare straight‑flush). Pot is 2400 and both stacks are deep, so I should extract value rather than check and give a free showdown. I size to get called by single‑spade flushes and worse K/Qx — a ~50% pot value bet is a good compromise between denying equity and keeping marginal callers in. Bet 1,200 into 2,400. If Claude check‑raises large it’s almost certainly a bluff or an extremely unlikely nut that beats me; I will re‑evaluate but I’m inclined to call reasonable raises with the nut flush.

River check-raise on a 4-spade board (Q♠3♠K♣9♠4♠) with my pair of 9s and weak 8 kicker. Her river check-raise indicates a strong hand - likely a completed flush, strong pair, or straight. Despite 3:1 pot odds, my hand is too weak here. My equity is approximately 20-25% against her polarized range, which is insufficient. Folding preserves chips when my hand is too marginal on this dangerous board texture. She's been short-stacked and passive until this moment, suggesting this raise represents genuine strength rather than a desperation move.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦3♥ and deep stacks (127bb vs 271bb). In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range — Q3o is marginal but playable in position. Raising gains initiative, creates fold equity, and lets me use position postflop to realize equity or apply pressure. I’ll open-raise to the standard 2xBB (200 total). If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold this dominated holding; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops or play cautiously when behind.

Raising to 500 with K8o in heads-up play. This is a standard 2.5x raise on Minni's 200 bet. K8 is a solid hand in heads-up situations, and with my significant stack advantage (27k vs 12.5k), I want to apply pressure and build a pot I can win with my range advantage. The raise is appropriately sized to gain control without being reckless.

I opened to 200 on the button with Q♦3♥ expecting to use position postflop. Claude 3-bet to 500 — a sizable isolation raise that is likely weighted toward value (strong broadways, pairs, A‑x) and will dominate my hand. Q3o has poor blocker value for a credible 4‑bet bluff and is easily dominated, so a 4‑bet is a bad bluff and calling would commit me to multi‑street decisions with a weak holding. I’ll stick to my preflop plan for big 3‑bets and fold to avoid playing a dominated hand out of position against a strong range and preserve my stack for better spots.

I'm holding pocket 8s in heads-up play from the button (small blind). This is a solid starting hand in 2-player poker. Given my positional advantage and significant chip lead (27,360 vs 12,490), I should play aggressively with premium hands. A raise to 250 (roughly 3x BB) applies appropriate pressure while building the pot. This is a standard aggro play for a pocket pair in heads-up poker where I can leverage my position and stack advantage.

Heads-up situation vs a single raiser to 250. K♦2♥ is marginal but playable in the big blind — the raise is small so calling 150 to make it 250 total gives ~30% pot odds and lets me realize equity and keep Claude’s range wide. I don’t want to 3-bet light with this offsuit hand out of position and risk facing a larger 4-bet from a deeper stack; calling preserves stack depth for postflop play. Plan: see the flop, proceed aggressively with top pair or strong draws, fold or control the pot when completely missed and facing heavy action.

Heads-up on a K♣ Q♠ T♦ flop I hold K♦2♥ — top pair but a terrible kicker. Claude opened preflop from the button so his range is wide (AQ/AJ, KQ/KJ, Qx, Tx, JT, some pairs, suited connectors). With no bet to me on the flop I want to take the initiative with a small value/protection bet: it charges Q/T/J-type hands and straight/connector draws (and gets called by worse kings and many Q/T/J hands) while keeping the pot manageable if I’m dominated. A modest bet (~40% pot) is optimal: it gets value from worse, denies equity to chasing cards, and lets me fold to significant resistance (a large raise) because my kicker is weak. Bet 200 into the 500 pot; if Claude raises big I’ll fold or re-evaluate severely, if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

Heads-up play with pocket 8s on a K-Q-T flop. Minni's small raise to 200 (roughly 28% pot) suggests she's probing rather than committed. With a 27k-to-12k chip advantage, I have leverage to apply pressure. Raising to 600 (3x her bet) generates fold equity, tests her hand strength, and puts her in a tough position. My pocket pair has reasonable showdown value even against overcards, and the raise gives me the initiative. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up poker with good pot control.

I c-bet 200 on Kc Qs Td to protect a very weak‑kicker top pair, but Claude check‑raised to 600. The pot is 1,300 and I face 400 to call (~23.5% pot odds). On this dry broadway flop a check‑raise is heavily weighted to value (sets: KK/QQ/TT, two‑pair KQ/KT, made straights like AJ or J9, and stronger kings like KJ) — there are very few realistic bluff/semi‑bluff combos here. My K2 has poor showdown equity versus that range and a raise would be thin (getting called/raised by better kings and sets). Calling commits more chips with a hand that is often dominated. Preserve the stack and wait for a clearer spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button with TcJh — a very playable, high-card hand that performs well postflop versus a wide big-blind defending range. Calling 50 is too passive (gives him a cheap flop and no fold equity); min-raising to 200 is acceptable, but I prefer a standard 3xBB open to take the initiative, extract value from worse hands, and price out speculative holdings. My stacks are deep enough that a 300 open keeps future options flexible and leverages my positional advantage. Total commit will be 300 (includes my posted 50). If Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing, but versus a call I’ll play aggressively in position.

Calling with K4o in heads-up from the BB. Minni's raising range from button is extremely wide given his aggressive play style throughout the tournament. My pot odds are 2:1 (risking 200 to win 400), and K4o has sufficient equity (~35%+) against a wide range. With my 2.3x chip lead, I can afford to play more marginal hands and evaluate post-flop. The call is +EV.

Checking K4 on a K-high flop. With a significant chip lead (2.3:1), I can play conservatively with this marginal hand. Checking allows me to: (1) keep the pot small, (2) stay out of position post-flop without building a pot I'm reluctant to commit to, and (3) evaluate Minni's flop action. Against his wide range, top pair with weak kicker plays better on small pots where I can fold to aggression when called. The board is relatively dry, so checking doesn't cost much equity.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor and Claude checked the flop, which is a dry K-3-2 rainbow. His check represents a wide range that often missed this flop (lots of broadways, small pairs and marginal hands after calling preflop). My TcJh has decent showdown equity vs that wide calling range and is a good candidate for a continuation bet to take the pot now and extract value from worse Kx draws, Jx/Tx and small pairs that will call. I size up to ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) — big enough to apply pressure and fold out many marginal hands while still getting called by worse. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (fold to a sizable raise, control the pot vs Kx/sets).

Calling the flop raise with K4o. Against Minni's wide, aggressive range, I have approximately 35-40% equity, which exceeds the 22% needed at 3.5:1 pot odds. With a significant chip lead (2.4:1), I can comfortably play this marginal hand and evaluate the turn. The flush draw and top pair provide additional equity and showdown value. My previous decision to check was sound; now calling allows me to see further improvement while keeping the pot manageable given my weak kicker.

On the turn with K♥4♥ versus the 2♥3♠K♠8♥ board, I'm checking to maintain pot control and exploit Minni's aggressive tendencies. The 8♥ is somewhat neutral for my hand—it doesn't significantly improve K4, but it does complete a flush draw that has reasonable equity (~18% to hit the river). My holdings: top pair (weak kicker) plus a flush draw gives me approximately 30-35% equity, which is reasonable equity even if Minni bets. With a 2.4:1 chip lead in heads-up play, I can afford to take a cautious line here. Checking accomplishes three goals: (1) keeps the pot manageable at 1400, (2) avoids committing more chips with a marginal holding, and (3) maintains information advantage by seeing how Minni reacts on the turn. If he checks back, I hit the river with my draw alive and showdown equity. If he bets, I can call with my equity against his wide range and evaluate the river card. This conservative approach aligns with my previous flop check—I'm sacrificing some thin value upside to maintain favorable risk/reward.

I c-bet the K-3-2 flop (400) and Claude called, then checked the 8h on the turn. With TcJh I have no pair and only ~6 clean outs (three J's and three T's) to improve on the river (~12–13%); my equity vs his flop-calling range (Kx, small pairs, some broadways) is low. Betting the turn here would be thin value (most callers beat me) and a bluff risks calling by Kx/pairs — so pot control is optimal. I’ll check to realize equity cheaply, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river: bet for value if I pair, target a river bluff only if the board and action give a clear fold equity, otherwise fold to a large river bet.

Checking the river with K♥4♥ on 2♥3♠K♠8♥3♣. Minni's back-to-back checks on turn and river indicate weakness despite his aggressive tournament style. Top pair with weak kicker has showdown value but is marginal. With a 2.4:1 chip lead and 1400 in the pot, I should avoid building larger pots with vulnerable hands. Checking allows me to see the showdown, win against air/weaker holdings, and control variance. Betting here risks getting called by better pairs or check-raised, neither of which is desirable with K4. This aligns with my previous pot-control strategy throughout the hand.

I have TcJh and missed the board — no pair and only 6 clean outs to improve (three J's, three T's). I c-bet the flop and was called, Claude then checked the turn and checked the river after the board paired with a 3. Claude’s check-call line on the flop indicates a fairly sticky range (Kx, small pairs, occasional set), so a river bluff has very limited fold equity and a value bet would mostly get called by hands that beat me. Pot control is the clear +EV line here: avoid bluffing into a range that will call and preserve my stack for better spots. I’ll check and take showdown rather than firing a thin, likely-unprofitable bluff.

Raising to 200 from the button with A3o in heads-up play. Position is critical in heads-up poker, and I should be playing a wide range from the button. A3 has decent playability post-flop with an ace, and the raise maintains initiative while being appropriately sized (2x BB). My chip lead allows me to play aggressively without risk of elimination.

Claude (button/SB) opened to 200 — wide heads‑up range. I’m BB with 7♦9♥ (marginal but playable heads‑up). Pot is 300 and I need to call 100 more, so my break‑even equity is 100/(300+100)=25% — quite attractive vs a wide opener. With deep stacks and being out of position, min‑3betting OOP with 7‑9o is suboptimal (I’d rather call and see the flop). Plan: call one street, evaluate flop texture (check/call dry boards, continue on favorable draws/pairings or fold to heavy aggression). Fold to a large 3‑bet. Action: defend the blind and see the flop.

I defended the blind with 9♥7♦ to see a flop. The board (Tc 6s Td) is very dry and actually pairs the board — I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw. Out of position heads‑up I want to control the pot and gather information. Checking keeps cheaper options open: I will only call very small continuation bets (roughly ≤100 into the 400 pot — break‑even ~20%) because my chance to pair by the river is only ~24%, but I’ll fold to medium/large C‑bets (≈>150–200). I won’t lead or bluff‑raise here since I lack blockers and fold equity. If Claude checks back I take a free turn and re‑evaluate.

On a paired ten board (Tc 6s Td), Minni checked after calling my pre-flop raise, showing weakness. With A3o, I currently hold ace-high, which is a reasonable hand in this spot. I have several advantages here: positional advantage as the button, a significant chip lead (28,360 vs 11,240), and initiative from my pre-flop raise. A bet is the correct play to apply pressure, define Minni's hand, and potentially take down the pot immediately. Betting 250 chips (roughly 60% of the 400-chip pot) is a standard continuation bet sizing that maintains aggression without overcommitting. Even if called, I have an ace with two more streets to improve, and I'll retain position advantage. This aggressive line is well-suited to heads-up play where chip accumulation is critical, and my stack advantage allows me to play profitably.

Checked the flop OOP with 9♥7♦ and Claude C‑bet 250 into a 650 pot. Calling 250 would require ~27.8% equity (250/(650+250)=27.8%). My realistic chance to improve to a pair by the river is only about 24% (≈6 outs over two cards), so a call is -EV. The board (T♣ 6♠ T♦) is paired and fairly dry; I have no pair, no meaningful draw and few blockers to bluffraise credibly. Claude opened wide preflop, but this medium‑sized c‑bet (>150–200) fits my criteria to fold OOP. With deep stacks there’s no need to gamble for a marginal equity deficit — fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠9♥ — a strong, playable hand in position. The pot is only 150 and calling 50 is cheap, but I prefer to take the initiative and leverage position postflop. Table opens have commonly been to 300; a min-raise to 200 is small and invites wider action/3-bets, so I’ll raise to a standard 300 total to apply pressure, build the pot with initiative, and use position to c‑bet dry flops. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold to a large shove/overly large 3-bet), and continue with a c-bet on favorable flops.

Minni raised to 300 from the button/small blind. I'm in the big blind with 5h 3h, facing a 200-chip call into a 400-chip pot—offering 2:1 pot odds. While 5h 3h is a weak hand, the odds are favorable in this heads-up situation. My stack is deep at 28,660, and Minni is relatively short at 10,940, suggesting they may be widening their range. I can play this hand profitably post-flop with position awareness. The hand has some connectivity potential (gutshot and flush draws possible). Given the pot odds and deep stacks, calling is the mathematically sound decision here.

With flopped trip 5s on a 5s 8c 5c board, I have a strong hand in heads-up play. Minni's short stack (10,940) and pre-flop aggression suggest they're widening their range. I should bet to extract value while they can still call with weaker holdings. A bet of 375 (roughly 2/3 of the 600 pot) is a balanced value-bet that builds the pot while remaining reasonable. This hand is ahead of most of their pre-flop range and should win a significant portion of the time. Going all-in would be excessive and potentially scare them away; checking gives up value and allows scare cards. A standard value-bet is optimal.

Heads‑up on the button with Q♠9♥. I opened preflop and Claude called, so his range is wide. Flop 5♠ 8♣ 5♣ is fairly dry but paired; Claude’s 375 into a 975 pot is a relatively small C‑bet that looks like a wide continuing range (probe bets, floats, small pairs, some draws). I don’t have a made hand (just Q‑high) and I don’t want to overcommit with a thin bluff‑raise — raising to 750 would bloat the pot out of position facing many hands that currently beat me. Pot odds to call are ~28%, and Q9 has enough equity vs a wide c‑betting range to continue in position. I’ll call to see the turn and re‑evaluate: if I pair the Q I’ll extract value; if Claude barrels big on a blank I can fold and preserve chips.

Full house (5s full of 8s) is a premium hand. Minni checked the turn despite a paired board, suggesting a weak/medium holding. At 10,565 chips, they're short-stacked and desperate. Betting 6,500 into the 1,350 pot extracts value while giving them a difficult decision - they'll likely fold unless they have trips or better, in which case I'm likely ahead given their stack desperation has probably loosened their range significantly. This aggressive value-bet maximizes expected value in a heads-up endgame scenario.

Turn brought 5♠ 8♣ 5♣ 8♥ — the board is double‑paired. Any 5 or any 8 in Claude’s range is now a full house. Claude’s line (small flop probe then a huge 6,500 turn bet into a 7,850 pot) is exactly what you’d expect from value (slow‑played sets/pairs that turned into a full house) and is unlikely to be a bluff often enough to justify a call. I hold Q♠9♥ with no pair and no meaningful outs — against a value heavy range my showdown equity is essentially zero, and if Claude also doesn’t hold a 5/8 we usually just chop the board. Calling 6,500 requires ~45% equity to be break‑even; I don’t have that. Shoving as a bluff could fold out some marginal hands but is high risk versus a likely made hand. Fold and preserve my 10,565 stack for better spots.

Heads-up on button against critically short-stacked opponent (10.5k vs my 29.4k). 2c4d is weak but fold equity is enormous in this spot. Minni must win hands to survive and will be forced to fold many weak holdings. Raising to 300 applies manageable pressure while exploiting their short-stack desperation. Goal is to win the pot immediately and accumulate chips to put tournament away.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 (I’m BB). I hold 7♠4♠ — a weak hand but suited with some backdoor equity. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (calling would put 300 total into the pot), so my break-even equity is 200/600 = 33.3%. Versus a typical wide HU open-range this hand has enough equity and postflop playability to justify a flat rather than folding or cold 3-betting out of position. I want to keep the pot manageable and use position on later streets to realize equity or control the pot, so I’ll call and see a flop.

I have 7♠4♠ on 8♣ K♠ 7♥ — a marginal pair with a weak kicker and only backdoor spade potential. Claude opened preflop so his range includes many Kx and broadways; this flop hits his range hard and he'll often c-bet. I'm out of position, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets me call reasonable c-bets, and fold to a large raise that clearly represents a King or better. Check and reassess on the turn based on his sizing.

Minni is critically short-stacked (10,265 chips) and just checked the flop, indicating weakness. While I have 2-high with no draws, I have position and the initiative. I've been aggressive pre-flop to exploit Minni's short-stack desperation. Continuing this aggression on the flop with a c-bet applies manageable pressure and leverages Minni's desperate situation - they need winning hands to survive and will be forced to fold marginal holdings. The 300 chip bet is proportionate to the 600 chip pot and consistent with my pre-flop aggression. My goal is to accumulate chips and finish this tournament effectively. Even though my hand has no showdown value, fold equity is the primary equity here.

I have 7♠4♠ on 8♣ K♠ 7♥ — middle pair with a very weak kicker and only a remote backdoor flush (needs both turn and river spades). Claude opened wide heads‑up preflop, so his c‑bet range will include lots of Kx, broadways and some air/bluffs. He bet a modest 300 into a 600 pot; calling 300 requires ~25% equity. My hand is ahead of his bluffs and many non‑pair holdings and has reasonable showdown value, so calling is +EV. Raising out of position with second pair and a bad kicker is risky: I’d often get reraised or commit big with an inferior hand. Folding is too nitty given the pot odds and cheap price to see the turn. Plan: call, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy aggression on a king or clearly strong line, but look to extract value or realize equity on favorable turn cards.

I check. The turn 8h pairs the board (8c 8h Ks 7h) and my 7♠ is now only second pair with a very poor kicker and a remote backdoor flush. Claude opened wide pre and c-bet the flop, so his continuing range contains lots of Kx and broadways (and the occasional 8x) that currently dominate my hand. Betting out of position as a bluff is thin — Kx will often call and stronger hands can raise me off the pot. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize showdown value against bluffs/mid‑strength hands, and gains info on Claude’s willingness to continue. Plan: check and re-evaluate on Claude’s action — call small/medium turn bets when sizing suggests bluffs, but fold to large aggression that clearly represents Kx/8x/trips and re-assess on the river.

Minni is critically short-stacked (9,965 chips) and has checked twice, indicating clear weakness. I'm betting 500 into a 1,200 chip pot to apply manageable pressure while maintaining my aggressive line that's been successful throughout the hand. With 2-4 (worst hand), fold equity is my primary equity. A 500 bet is proportionate and forces Minni to make a decision with their limited resources. Given the checkdown pattern and Minni's desperate situation requiring winning hands to survive, this bet has high fold equity and allows me to accumulate chips toward finishing this tournament.

Call 500. Claude opened wide HU and barreled the flop; the turn 8h paired the board and my 7♠4♠ is now just second pair with a very poor kicker and no realistic flush (backdoor missed). He’s betting 500 into a 1700 pot; calling 500 to see the river requires only ~18.5% equity, so calling is cheap and keeps many weaker hands and bluffs in his range. Raising out of position with second pair/terrible kicker is too risky and folding is unnecessarily nitty given the pot odds. Plan: call and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to heavy river aggression if a King/8 or a line that clearly represents trips/Kx, but value/raise or call down if I pair up to two pair/trips or if he shows weakness (checks or small bet).

Claude checked the river after barreling flop and turn. My 7♠4♠ is only second pair with a terrible kicker and no realistic redraws. Given his earlier aggression his check likely represents either a medium-strength hand (Kx/Qx) or a pot-control line — both beat me. Betting for thin value is unlikely to get called by worse and exposes me to a costly raise; bluffing is risky heads‑up vs a wide but aggressive opener. With pot = 2200 and I’m out of position, the lowest-variance, +EV play is to check and take my showdown — realize my equity without bloating the pot or risking a tough fold to a raise.

River with 2-4 (zero showdown value) - I must bet for fold equity. Minni's check-through pattern combined with their critical short-stack status suggests a weaker holding. A 1,200 chip bet (55% of the 2,200 pot) maintains the consistency of my aggressive line while applying final pressure. This bet gives Minni a tough decision with limited resources - they need to win hands to survive, and a marginal holding will face terrible pot odds (needing to call 1,200 to win 3,400). Since I cannot win at showdown, fold equity is my only path to the pot. The bet size is proportionate and forces Minni into a binary decision with their tournament life on the line.

River: board 8c Ks 7h 8h Qd, I hold 7s4s — second pair with a terrible kicker. Claude was the HU opener and barreled flop and turn; his river bet of 1200 into the current pot gives me to‑call 1200 with the pot listed as 3400. Calling 1200 to win ~3400 requires ~35% equity. Given his line (preflop open, flop c‑bet, turn continue, river bet) his continuing range is heavily weighted toward value: Kx (including KQ/K7/KT/etc.), any 8x (including trips or two‑pair combos), and mid/high pocket pairs — all of which beat my pair of sevens. The hands I realistically beat are a small subset of pure air/missed broadways and tiny pocket pairs; I estimate my equity is well below the ~35% needed. Raising is a thin, high‑variance bluff from out of position with a poor blocker and a deep opponent stack; it’s unlikely to fold enough value hands and would risk far more of my 9465 chips. For stack preservation and EV, the correct play is to fold.

I’m on the button (small blind) with A♣J♥ heads-up — a strong opening hand in position. Calling 50 would be passive and give the big blind good odds to see a flop with a wide range; I want to seize initiative, build the pot for value when I connect, and deny equity to speculative hands. A raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) is standard at this table size: it’s big enough to generate fold equity and keep worse A-x/J-x hands in, but small enough relative to my ~94BB stack to leave plenty of postflop maneuvering room. If Claude 3-bets, I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing, but open-raising to 300 is the best immediate play.

With 7h Qc in the big blind out of position against Minni's raise, this is a clear fold. While 7Q has some merit in heads-up play, I'm significantly out of position and the hand is relatively weak. With 28 hands remaining and a substantial chip advantage (3.3:1), I can afford to be selective. Minni's aggressive raising pattern suggests she's widening her opening range, but I should exploit my chip advantage by playing tighter from out-of-position and waiting for premium hands or better spots. This conservative approach maximizes my edge in the endgame while preserving my stack.

Raising to 300 (2.5x BB) from the button with J4o in a heads-up situation. This move leverages my significant chip advantage (30k vs 9.5k), button position, and the fact that in heads-up play, J4 is a reasonable hand to open. The raise size allows for fold equity while maintaining flexibility post-flop if called. This is standard positional aggression in a short-handed match with a chip advantage.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have pocket nines (9♠9♥) with ~94BB effective — a clear strong hand preflop. Calling 200 to see a flop OOP is an option, but I prefer to 3-bet for value/isolation: Claude's opening range from the button will be wide enough that 9s9h gets value from Ax, broadways and smaller pairs, and a 3-bet also reduces multiway/overcard problems. I don't want to just make the minimum raise (too small and it invites marginal callers and tricky OOP postflop spots), so I size to about 3x his open to 900 total. This extracts value, applies pressure, and keeps weaker hands in. If Claude 4-bets big I'll re-evaluate (fold to extremely large 4-bets, call smaller ones depending on sizing and reads). Plan: raise to 900 and play postflop for value if called.

Calling Minni's 3-bet with J4o in heads-up play. With a 3.5:1 chip advantage and button position, I can profitably play post-flop where I'll have positional control and the ability to leverage my stack. The pot odds (600 to call, 1200 in pot = 2:1) are acceptable for a marginal hand when I can play position and chip advantage effectively. Folding gives up equity in an advantageous situation; re-raising thin is unnecessary given my already-dominant position.

I 3-bet preflop and Claude called. Flop is 3♥ A♣ 3♦. My 9♠9♥ is a vulnerable pair here — I’m dominated by almost all A‑x combos, which make up a large chunk of his flatting range, and there are almost no real draws to protect against. Betting will mainly get called by better hands (Ax, bigger pairs) and only fold the worst hands, so it’s not a +EV line. I prefer to control the pot and keep his bluffs in — check and evaluate. Plan: check now; if he leads I’ll call small‑to‑medium bets to realize equity and fold to very large overbets/shoves, and if he checks back I’ll take a free turn to barrel blanks or bet for value when appropriate.

Checking with J4o on a 3h-Ac-3d flop. I have jack-high with zero draws and no credible betting story. Minni's 3-bet range is heavily weighted toward strong Aces or high pocket pairs; a bluff here has poor fold equity against her likely holdings. My massive 3.4:1 chip advantage allows me to play selectively rather than force value from weak holdings. Checking to see the turn card is the appropriate risk management play - multiple overcards could improve my hand, and I can control pot size while in position.

Check. I 3‑bet preflop with 9♠9♥ and Claude called; flop (3♥ A♣ 3♦) we both checked — his check on an Ace‑heavy board suggests pot control or a wide non‑value call rather than a straightforward value bet with many Ax. The turn 7♦ is essentially a blank for my hand: it doesn’t improve my nines and only brings a small portion of his range (7x) that now beats me. Betting here would get called or raised by most hands that currently beat me (Ax, overpairs) and won’t fold enough better hands to make a bluff profitable. I want pot control and to realize equity, so I’ll check to the river and re‑evaluate. Plan: if he leads a small/medium bet on the river I’ll call for pot odds; fold to large overbets/shoves indicating strong value. (Pot 1,800; my stack 8,665; opponent ~29,535.)

Checking turn with J4o on 3h-Ac-3d-7d. I have 4-card flush draw (any club completes), giving me solid equity. With a 3.4:1 chip advantage over Minni, there's no need to force marginal action. Minni's twice-checked line suggests weakness. Checking to see the river card is the lowest variance approach - either I make my flush and can value bet the river, or I check down with my weak holding. This leverages my dominant position optimally.

Board A♣ 3♥ 3♦ 7♦ 6♣ vs my 9♠9♥. Claude flat‑called my 3‑bet preflop and then checked flop and turn — a very pot‑controlled line consistent with A‑x or a wide, non‑committal flatting range. The Ace combos in his range dominate my nines and make up a large portion of his calling/flatting hands. Betting the river would mostly be called by better hands (A‑x, some turned pairs) and won’t fold enough worse hands to be +EV. There are also very few believable bluffs to target on this runout. I’ll take the safe line and check to showdown. If he fires a small/medium river bet I’ll call for pot odds; I’ll fold to a large overbet/shove representing strong value.

Checking the river with jack-high. After Minni's 3-bet pre-flop and her twice-checked line through the turn and river, she likely holds either a weak broadway hand that whiffed (AK/AQ) or a medium pocket pair. My J-high rarely wins against a 3-betting opponent. Betting here would be a marginal, variance-increasing play that's unnecessary given my 3.4:1 chip advantage. Checking to showdown is the disciplined play - it preserves chips in a tournament where stack preservation matters, avoids getting exploited by her potential check-raises with strong hands, and lets the hand resolve naturally. With my dominant position, I can afford to take showdowns on weak holdings and accumulate chips through selective aggression on better spots.

Heads-up on the button with K♠7♠ — a playable, suited king that does very well in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but in heads-up I want to open-raise a wide range to seize initiative and isolate the big blind. Minimum raise is 200, but I prefer a slightly larger, standard 3x-BB sizing to get value and fold equity while keeping good postflop playability. Plan: if Claude calls I’ll use position to c-bet dry textures and control pot on coordinated/wet boards; call small-to-medium 3-bets and proceed cautiously; fold to very large 3-bets/shoves without a strong read. Raise to 300 to open the hand aggressively from the button.

Aggressive raise to exploit chip advantage and short-stack situation. With 26 hands left and 3:1 stack lead, I should apply maximum pressure to Minni's 10k stack. The raise to 700 forces him into a tough decision without a premium hand, and even if he calls, I have plenty of chips to maneuver post-flop. This is a theoretically sound aggressive play in heads-up that prioritizes winning chips over hand strength.

Claude 3-bet to 700 after my button open to 300 with K♠7♠. This is a small-to-medium 3-bet heads-up and K7s plays well in position (suited, king blocker). Pot is 1000 and I need to call 400 more (total 700) which gives ~28.6% pot odds — good versus a likely wide BB 3-bet range. My preflop plan was to call small-to-medium 3-bets and use position postflop: I’ll call to see a flop, c-bet thin on dry boards, control the pot on coordinated/wet boards, and fold to large turn/river aggression or very large 4-bets/shoves. Call 700.

Continuation bet on the flop with bottom pair. The 6d gives me a pair of sixes, which is vulnerable to Minni's range but potentially the best hand. In heads-up poker with my 3:1 chip lead, I should maintain aggression. A bet of 800 (57% of the 1,400 pot) is a standard c-bet sizing that keeps the initiative, applies pressure to Minni's short stack, and allows me to control the hand. This sizing is standard for continuation betting with a moderate holding and works well given my aggressive pre-flop re-raise established the narrative that I have a strong hand.

I opened preflop and called Claude’s small 3‑bet, so I’m the preflop aggressor-caller and I have position. Flop Q♦ T♣ 6♦ is relatively dry and Claude’s 800 into a 2,200 pot prices me at ~26.7% (800/(2200+800)) to call. K♠7♠ has one high overcard (king) and a blocker to some Kx combos, but no pair and no real draw — equity is marginal but likely in the mid-20s–30% versus a wide 3‑bet/c‑bet range. Raising as a bluff is too risky without a pair or a strong draw (Claude’s 3‑bet range contains a lot of hands that will continue), and folding here surrenders good price and positional advantage. I’ll call 800 to realize equity, then reevaluate on the turn — folding to large turn aggression unless I improve or see a clear bluff opportunity.

Check the turn to control pot size. The Jack completes too many broadway combinations that beat my pair of sixes. With my 3:1 chip lead and Minni short-stacked, I can afford to take a cautious line here. Checking allows me to: (1) see if Minni shows strength with a bet, (2) avoid committing more chips with a marginal hand on a dangerous board, and (3) still maintain win opportunities if both of us check to river. This balances aggression with prudent risk management given the vulnerable nature of my holding.

Claude checked the turn after c-betting the flop, which is a sign of weakness or pot-control. My K♠7♠ has only one overcard and a tiny backdoor (runner-runner) spade possibility — not a made hand — so pure showdown value is low. Heads-up this is a clear probe/bluff spot: a half-pot bet will fold out many of Claude's missed c-bets (small pairs, Ax, floating hands) while keeping my risk reasonable. I choose a 1/2-pot probe (1500). If he folds I take the pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and be prepared to check/fold to significant aggression unless I pair the King or see a very clear bluff-catching spot. If he raises large I’ll concede and fold to preserve stack.

Call the turn raise with pair of sixes and gutshot straight draw. I have approximately 32% equity (8 outs) against Minni's range, facing 4:1 pot odds that require only 20% equity. This is a mathematically sound call given the favorable equity-to-odds ratio and my 3.75:1 chip advantage. The short-stack dynamics favor aggressive play, and my drawing potential provides additional win possibilities beyond my current pair.

Check the river to control pot size and reach showdown with marginal pair of sixes. The board's development (broadway cards and paired tens on river) combined with Minni's aggressive turn play suggests my pair is vulnerable but potentially best against busted draws. With a 3.5:1 chip advantage and Minni short-stacked, reaching showdown allows me to win the pot without additional risk if he cannot improve. If my sixes are beaten, the damage is already committed. Checking is the prudent risk-management play with a marginal hand on a dangerous final board.

Check. I have K♠7♠ — no pair and only king-high. Claude 3‑bet preflop, bet the flop, then checked the turn and called my 1,500 probe; that line contains a lot of medium‑strong/value hands (AQ/AK, KQ, Qx, JJ/QQ and even TT), and the river T pairs the board which materially improves his range (QQ/JJ → full house, TT → quads). My bluff equity is small (K blocker helps a little) and any realistic bluff is likely to be called by hands that already beat me. Pot is 6,000 and my remaining stack is ~7,465 — a large river bet would commit a big portion of my stack for poor expected value. I’ll check to reach showdown; if Claude then leads a large river bet I’ll fold since I have no showdown value.

Heads-up button play with chip advantage justifies an aggressive raising strategy. While 4-10o is weak in absolute terms, it falls within the standard heads-up button raising range. Raising maintains pressure on the short-stacked opponent and keeps them from exploiting tight play. Position and chip advantage make this a standard aggressive play.

Heads-up vs a button open to 200. In the BB with K♣3♥ I’m getting good pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot) so defend rather than fold. Hand is too weak/too often dominated to 3-bet as a bluff, and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position without a real hand. Plan: call to 200 total, see the flop and continue only with top pair or strong draws; on dry boards be ready to check-fold to heavy aggression.

Heads-up OOP on 5♥-6♣-2♣ with K♣-3♥. I only have K-high with a backdoor gutshot to a 4 (4 outs, ~19% to hit by river) and a remote runner-runner club flush. Claude opened wide from the button so he'll c-bet often, but I don't have a made hand or a strong draw to build the pot out of position. Best line is pot control: check and see his action. Plan: if he bets small I'll call to realize my equity; fold to heavy pressure. If he checks back I take down the pot.

Heads-up play in position with a significant chip advantage (32k vs 7k) makes this a standard continuation bet spot. While 4-10o whiffed completely on the 5-6-2 flop, I have several favorable factors: (1) positional advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, (2) opponent is short-stacked and played cautiously post-flop with a check, (3) this board is relatively dry with limited drawing possibilities, (4) Minni likely folded many weak holdings pre-flop. A reasonable c-bet of 200 (50% of pot) maintains aggression and is sized to fold out marginal holdings while not overexposing myself. The short stack's limited resources make fold equity particularly valuable here. This is standard aggressive button play in heads-up.

Heads-up OOP on 5♥-6♣-2♣ with K♣3♥. Claude’s bet is small (200 into the 600 pot) and he opened wide from the button, so his c-bet range includes a lot of bluffs/missed overcards. Calling 200 gives me pot odds of 200/(800+200)=20%. I have ~4 clean outs to a 4 (≈16% to hit by the river) plus a remote runner-runner club possibility and occasional showdown value as K-high versus his weakest c-bets. The call is cheap relative to my 7.2k stack and keeps weaker hands in his range; I’ll see the turn and fold to heavy turn pressure if I don’t improve. Call 200 to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

Check. I only have K-high with no pair and only a backdoor club possibility plus a 4 as a gutshot to complete a 2-6 straight (4 outs → ~8–9% to hit on the river). The turn A dramatically improves Claude’s range (Ax, pairs, sometimes slowplays), and he opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop — so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with air. Pot control is the best line: check and see if he barrels (fold to heavy pressure; call small probing bets to realize equity), or take the free river if he checks back. Preserves my 7k stack vs his deep stack and keeps future options open.

Checking the turn with air (4-10o) after the scare ace. While I have position and a chip advantage, continuing to fire on this card without any hand is printing money for Minni to call with any piece. The ace likely hit her range, making a bluff less effective. Checking preserves my stack, allows me to see the river for free, and keeps me flexible for river action. Short-stack dynamics mean I want to control the pot size with no hand. If she bets river, I fold; if she checks, I can reassess showdown value and river dynamics.

Check. Both of us checked the Ace on the turn and I only have K-high (K♣3♥) with no pair and only one club — not a made flush. Betting here would be a thin bluff that won’t fold many of Claude’s reasonable pairs or Ax hands and risks chips against his deep stack. Pot control and taking the cheap showdown is the best line: check and call any tiny, clearly blocking probes, fold to meaningful river aggression.

River showdown with air (4-10 offsuit) on a 5-6-2-A-7 board. Minni checked to me after a triple-check line (flop, turn, river). While her checking pattern suggests weakness, I have absolutely nothing and will lose to any made hand. Betting here doesn't generate meaningful fold equity given her short stack (7k) and desperate situation - she'll call with any piece given 3:1 pot odds. Checking to showdown maximizes my expected value by: (1) avoiding getting called by better hands, (2) preserving any showdown value my 10 might have against pure air, (3) keeping my chip advantage intact without forcing marginal bluffs. With 25 hands remaining and a significant stack advantage, conservative play is appropriate here.

Heads-up on the button with 7♦6♦ and ~78bb effective (stack 7,815). In heads-up spots you should open very wide from the button to seize initiative and use position. 7♦6♦ is playable postflop (flush/straight potential) and benefits from fold equity preflop. A standard 2xBB (raise to 200 total) is the right sized open here — it applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me favorable postflop options (c-bet favorable textures, check/protect on dry boards, or fold to strong resistance). I’ll raise to 200.

Raising to 500 with AJ from the big blind against Minni's short stack. This sizing (2.5x their raise) applies appropriate pressure while maintaining a rational bet size. With a 4.2x chip advantage and a premium hand, aggression is warranted. This raise either takes down the pot preflop or builds a pot where I have significant postflop advantage with AJ and position. The 300 additional chips I'm adding represents only ~1.5% of my stack, allowing me to play for a substantial pot against a vulnerable opponent.

I opened to 200 from the button with 7♦6♦ to seize initiative. Claude 3‑bet to 500; facing 300 to call into a ~700 pot, the immediate pot odds are ~30%. 7♦6♦ is a good flatting candidate heads‑up: suited, connected, plays very well in position on deep (~76bb) stacks and has decent equity vs a wide 3‑bet range. A small 4‑bet bluff here is suboptimal — I don’t have premium blockers and Claude can easily call or 5‑bet with his deep stack. I’ll call 300 more (500 total) to realize equity and use position postflop: c‑bet small on favorable flops, control the pot or fold to heavy aggression on A/K‑heavy boards, and extract on straights/flushes when I hit.

Betting 700 on the flop with AJ against a short stack. This represents 2/3 pot sizing (pot is 1000). I have strong equity with an overcard, OESD, and two-card combinations to improve. My 4.3:1 chip advantage makes aggression appropriate here. Standard continuation bet sizing maintains pressure while keeping Minni in the hand if she has any draw or pair.

Heads‑up in position with 7♦6♦ — I hold second pair (7s) with two diamonds (a backdoor/future flush potential) on Q♦‑7♣‑T♣. Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop bet 700 into 1700) represents a fairly wide but often strong range (Qx, Tx, overcards, some pairs, bluffs). Calling 700 requires ~29% equity (700/(1700+700)=29%), and my pair plus turn/improvement outs has reasonable equity and excellent position to realise it. Raising here is thin: I’d be building the pot with a marginal hand and no strong blockers; folding is too tight given price and playability. Plan: call, see the turn — barrel or check back depending on Claude’s action; fold to heavy turn aggression if he represents clear stronger hands.

Betting 1400 on the turn with top pair/strong kicker (AJ). The Ace is a significant card that improves my hand from the flop. Against a short-stacked opponent, this bet applies pressure while extracting value from drawing hands (open-ended straights, flush draws) or medium pairs. The sizing represents approximately 58% of the pot, which is standard value-betting sizing that keeps her in the hand while building the pot with a strong holding. With 4.6:1 chip advantage and position through the hand, aggression is appropriate.

I have 7♦6♦ and currently just second pair (7s) on Q♦-7♣-T♣-A♠. Claude 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop and now barrels the Ace on the turn to 1,400 into a 3,800 pot — a line that heavily favors value (A‑x, Q‑x, T‑x, sets, big pairs). To call I need ~1400/(3800+1400) ≈ 26.9% equity. My realistic improvement outs on the river are tiny: two remaining 7s (trips) + three remaining 6s (to make two pair) = 5 outs → 5/46 ≈ 10.9% to improve. Even allowing for some bluffs in his range, my raw equity is far below the price. Raising is thin (I have poor blockers and would be building a big pot with a marginal hand against a deep, aggressive opponent). Given the pot odds, the turn card and Claude’s line, folding is the prudent, +EV play — preserve my stack, wait for a better spot and re‑attack in position when the equity and price are aligned.

Raising with T-K from the button in heads-up play against a short stack. With 5x the chip advantage, I can apply pressure with this premium broadway hand. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x the BB) is appropriate to build the pot, maintain aggression, and force Minni into a difficult decision while maintaining healthy risk management.

Heads-up vs button who opened to 250 (2.5x BB). I’m in the big blind with 7♦9♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position post‑flop against a wide button range. Calling 150 more (250 total committed) gives ~30% pot odds and lets me use position to see a flop, control the pot and fold to big aggression on dry boards. A tiny min‑raise 3‑bet here isn’t worth bloating the pot with a marginal hand and I don’t want to fold equity away by folding preflop. Plan: call, evaluate on the flop (continue with pairs/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure when missed).

Check. I’m OOP with 9♠7♦ and have a single pair (7s) on an A‑high flop (3♠ A♦ 7♥). Claude opened a wide button range preflop so this board hits a lot of his range (many Ax and broadways) — my hand is decent for showdown but often second‑best. Betting out OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and give villain an easy call with most A’s; checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity, and preserves the option to extract value on later cards if I improve. Plan: call small–medium c‑bets (~<= half pot) to see a turn, fold to very large/overbet sizing or heavy aggression, and if he checks back consider a small turn stab or check down without improvement.

Continuation bet on a dry flop with broadway overcards. Minni's check from BB indicates weakness. With a 5x chip advantage in heads-up play, I can apply healthy aggression here. A bet of 325 (65% pot) is standard for maintaining pressure while getting value from worse hands. My T-K has strong equity against most of Minni's likely holdings (weak pairs, overcards, draws), and the position/initiative strongly favors continuation.

Flop: 3♠ A♦ 7♥ — I hold 9♠7♦ (middle pair, weak kicker). Claude opened the button wide to 250 and now c‑bet 325 into a 500 pot (~65% pot). Against a wide HU button range many A‑x and broadways will be in his range, but there are also a lot of missed hands I beat. Calling 325 to win a ~1150 pot requires ~28% equity — my pair has at least that vs his wide range, so a call is +EV. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and cede too much to Aces; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to control the pot and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to very large/overbet/raise that strongly polarizes to AA/sets, value‑bet small if I improve or check‑down if checked to on later streets.

Turn analysis: I have 9♠7♦ on 3♠ A♦ 7♥ 3♥ — two pair (7s & 3s). Pot = 1150, opponent (button) opened pre and c‑bet the flop, so his range is heavy in A‑x and broadways. That range contains many hands that currently beat me (any Ace gives Aces+3s; any 3 gives trips; A3/full houses are possible) so my two‑pair is vulnerable. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position against a range that often dominates me. Checking controls the pot, lets bluffs continue and keeps worse hands in that I can extract value from, and preserves the option to call a reasonable bet or fold to large polarization. Plan: check now. If Claude checks back I’ll evaluate the river; if he bets a small-to-medium sizing (roughly <= ~1/2 pot) I’ll call to get value from missed hands/bluffs; if he barrels pot-sized or raises big I’ll lean to folding because that sizing likely represents Ax/sets/full houses that beat me.

Turn brings a second three (3h), creating a 3-3-7-A board. Minni checks again, indicating weakness or a modest holding. With T-K, I have broadway overcards (K-high currently) with significant equity. In heads-up play with a 5.4x chip advantage (32,760 vs 6,090), I should continue applying pressure. Minni's consecutive checks on flop and turn suggest they lack conviction in their hand. A controlled bet around 700 maintains aggressive momentum while building the pot if I'm ahead and potentially winning it outright if they fold a marginal holding. This also sets up a manageable river decision. The turn card (pairing the board with a 3) is relatively neutral for my T-K, as it's unlikely to have helped their range significantly if they're checking weak. I'll continue my aggression with this turn bet.

I have two pair (7s and 3s) on 3♠ A♦ 7♥ 3♥. Claude opened the button wide, c‑bet the flop and now bets 700 into the pot (pot is 1850). Calling 700 to win 2550 requires ~27.5% equity. His line is heavy in A‑x and contains some 3x/7x combos that beat me, but it also contains many missed broadways and bluffs that I currently beat. Raising would bloat the pot out of position against a range that often dominates me and risks a big shove; folding surrenders too much equity. I’ll call 700 to control the pot, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and re‑evaluate on the river (call small river bets for value, fold to large/overbet/raise that strongly polarizes to full houses/sets/Ax).

I check. I have two pair (7s & 3s) on a very textured board (3♠ A♦ 7♥ 3♥ 5♦) but my hand is vulnerable to a number of full houses/trips (A3, 33, 77, etc.). Claude opened wide, barreled flop and turn, then checked the river — that line can be pot‑control with an Ace or medium hand or a disguised very strong hand, and it also leaves a lot of Ax and missed broadway hands in his range. Betting here would only extract thin value from some A‑x and weaker hands while risking a check‑raise shove from the few full houses/trips that beat me. With the pot at 2550 and my stack 5390, I don’t want to bloat the pot or face a polarizing raise. Checking to take it to showdown is the safer, higher‑EV play.

River decision with T♥-K♦ on a 3♠-A♦-7♥-3♥-5♦ board. Minni has checked the river after checking and calling on both flop and turn. My hand is king-high, which is marginal and functions primarily as a bluff-catcher at best. Given Minni's passive pattern (check-call-check-call-check), they likely have at least a weak pair or an ace that they're holding for showdown value. With only 5,390 chips remaining and the pot at 2,550, any river bet here would essentially be a bluff, and Minni's calling pattern suggests they'd likely continue if called. Additionally, with a 6x chip advantage (32,060 vs 5,390), I'm in a position where I can afford the conservative play and take the showdown. If Minni was on a completely missed draw, I win at showdown. If they have a pair or ace, I avoid risking additional chips on a bluff that won't fold out better hands. This is optimal risk management given my hand strength and positional circumstances.

Heads-up on the button with T9o — a very playable, positionally advantaged hand. I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure to the big blind and build a pot I can play postflop. Standard heads-up sizing is to open to ~3x the BB here, so I’ll raise to 300. If Claude calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to extremely large 3-bets with this speculative hand).
