Folding 59o from the BB against Minni's raise. While Minni is short-stacked and likely wide, 59 unsuited lacks the strength to profitably play out of position. Even with 2:1 pot odds (200 to call to win 400), the hand's weak equity and positional disadvantage make folding the standard choice. Disciplined play with proper hand selection is more valuable than marginal calls.

2-5 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't justify a call or raise even in heads-up play. With a 4:1 chip lead and 22 hands remaining, there's no compelling reason to risk chips with marginal holdings. Better opportunities will arise with stronger cards. Standard fold.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 3♣2♥ is junk heads-up but it's a reasonable steal attempt from the button — I want initiative and to define Claude's range. I will make a minimum raise to 200 (total) to apply pressure and pick up the blinds; if Claude folds I win the pot, if he calls I keep position and will c‑bet/fold selectively postflop. If he 3‑bets strongly I will relinquish the pot (this hand has poor equity vs a 3‑bet).

Calling with J2o against Minni's short-stack desperation raise. Despite weak hole cards, the pot odds (3:1) are favorable against her wide push range, and position as the big blind allows me to play post-flop with information advantage. With 31,810 chips versus her 7,890, accumulating chips from desperate short stacks is a key tournament strategy. Standard play to see the flop.

Betting 400 into 400 pot with top pair on dry flop against short-stacked desperation raise. Minni's wide pre-flop range is dominated by my pair, and building the pot while she's desperate is optimal tournament strategy. This bet size establishes immediate pressure and either takes down the pot or builds value when she calls with weaker holdings.

I have 3♣2♥ and completely missed this flop (6♥ J♠ 4♣). My only real draw is a one-card gutshot to a 2-3-4-5-6 straight (four 5s) and I can pair a 2 or 3, but those pairs are vulnerable vs the likely parts of Claude’s range (Jx, middle pairs). Claude’s bet is 400 into a pot of 800 (I need ~33% equity to justify a pure call); my clean equity to a made straight by the river is ~16.5% and pairing outs are often non‑winners, so calling is marginal but reasonable because it’s a small price (only ~5% of my stack) to see the turn and I have position. A raise would be an aggressive bluff that risks a much bigger commitment against a very deep, sticky opponent who calls wide — not ideal with this holding. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the initiative in position, and fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve.

Checking the turn with J2o (top pair weak kicker) on a T♦ runout. The Ten significantly expanded Minni's possible holdings and made my top pair vulnerable to straights and better pairs. With her short stack, checking allows controlled pot management and preserves chips when my hand is marginal. This gives her the option to bluff or check back, while I maintain flexibility for a river decision with complete information. Against a desperate short-stacked opponent, this prudent approach maximizes my long-term equity while minimizing exposure with a weak kicker.

Claude checked the turn after raising the flop. With 3♣2♥ on 6♥ J♠ 4♣ Td I have very little — only 4 outs to a straight (the 5s) and 6 outs to pair a 2 or 3 (10 total ≈ 22% to improve on the river), and most pair-outs are likely second-best to Claude’s flop-raise range (Jx, mid pairs). The pot is 1200 and it’s free to see the river; betting the turn would be a thin bluff into a deep, sticky opponent and unnecessarily inflates the pot when my equity is limited. I’ll check to control pot size, realize my equity for free, and plan to fold to meaningful river aggression unless I actually improve.

I'm holding a full house (Jacks full of Sixes) on the river, which is an elite-strength hand. Minni's short-stack range likely contains many weaker holdings given her desperation pre-flop raise and turn check-back. Rather than checking and giving her a free showdown, I should extract value with a reasonable bet. A 700-chip bet (approximately 58% of the pot) represents solid value sizing—substantial enough to build my stack while remaining callable for weaker pairs or two-pair combinations. Given the tournament stage (hand 130 of 150) and my chip advantage, accumulating chips from short-stacked opponents is critical to tournament success. This bet balances value extraction with pot control, maximizing my long-term equity.

I have 3♣2♥ on 6♥ J♠ 4♣ T♦ 6♦ — I missed completely. The pot is 1,900 and Claude’s river raise is 700, so a call requires roughly 700/(1,900+700) ≈ 27% equity. I have essentially no showdown equity (no pair, no completed straight/flush); the only way I win is if Claude is bluffing. His line (call pre, bet flop 400, check turn, bet river 700) is much more consistent with value hands (Jx, T, 6x, or a set) than with the ~27% bluff frequency I’d need. With a healthy stack and many hands left, folding preserves chips and avoids hero-call mistakes. I fold.

Raising to 300 total (250 additional to my SB). In heads-up play with a 4.4x chip lead, I should be playing a significantly wider range and applying constant pressure. Q3o from the button has reasonable fold equity and showdown value. Minni's short stack (7,390) means they'll fold many hands. This aggression is essential for tournament closure.

Heads-up vs the button's open, I should defend relatively wide in the big blind. 7♣6♣ is a strong suited connector with good equity and playability. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (total contribution 300), so my pot odds are ~33% — good enough to continue. With ~74bb effective I prefer to see a flop and realize equity rather than 3-bet light and inflate the pot out of position. Plan: call, play the flop aggressively on favorable textures (club/connected) and proceed cautiously vs heavy action on scary boards.

Flop: 9c 8d 2h with 7c6c in the BB. I have a very strong drawing hand — an open-ended straight draw (outs to 5 or 10, ~8 outs) plus backdoor club equity. Heads-up this is a great semi-bluff: decent raw equity to hit by the river (~31%+ with a little extra from backdoor clubs) and good fold equity against a wide button opening range. I want to take the initiative, fold out many single-overcard hands and small pairs, and build the pot when I hit. Betting ~50% pot (300) is the right size here: it applies pressure, keeps worse hands from freely seeing turns, and leaves maneuvering room on later streets. Plan if called: continue on turns that improve my draw (5/10/club) and be disciplined vs large raises — fold to heavy polarized aggression, call to realize equity if the price is reasonable.

Folding Q3o to a check-raise on this flop. While heads-up play rewards aggression, Q3 is too weak facing Minni's check-raise which typically indicates real hand strength (likely a pair or broadway cards). With a 4.4x chip lead and 32,210 chips, I should avoid bloating the pot with marginal holdings. There will be better spots to accumulate chips and close out this tournament.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also the small blind) with T♥2♠ and ~77bb effective. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop, but in heads-up I want to keep pressure and open a very wide range from the button. T2o is marginal but playable with position, so I’ll take the initiative with a minimum raise to 200 to pick up the blind or get value when Claude calls with worse. I’ll keep the sizing small to preserve stack and fold to a large 3‑bet (avoid playing dominated off‑suit junk out of position); if called I’ll proceed cautiously post‑flop — small c-bet on favorable textures, control pot on overcards/dry boards.

I'm in the big blind heads-up with K-10o against Minni who has raised to 200. With a massive chip advantage (32110 vs 7590), Minni is playing short-stack poker and opening a wide range from the button. K-10o is a solid hand in this dynamic and plays well post-flop. Minni is likely shoving light with their short stack if action continues, so I should call here to see a flop where I can capitalize on my range and positional advantages. The pot is small relative to my stack, and calling allows me to play in position after the flop.

On the flop of Jh-8s-8h, I have K-10o with an open-ended straight draw and likely the best hand currently. My massive chip advantage (32,010 vs 7,590) means Minni is playing survival poker. I should bet to build the pot and apply pressure. A bet of 600 is a standard continuation bet (1.5x pot) that forces Minni to make a decision with their short stack. If I check, Minni will likely shove regardless given the desperation of their situation, so betting lets me control the action and build a pot I'm likely to win."

I opened wide from the button and (per plan) c-bet the flop. Claude’s action is a check-raise to 600 on Jh 8s 8h — a very strong line in heads-up that is heavily weighted toward value (Jx, an 8 in hand for trips, overpairs like 99+/TT+, or other strong holdings). I hold T2o with essentially three clean tens as outs. That gives me only ~12–13% to make a pair by the river, but calling 600 into the current pot (~1600 after his bet) requires ~37.5% equity to be profitable. I have neither the equity nor a reliable bluff plan out of position against a deeper stack here, so this is a clear fold to conserve chips and pick better spots with my ~77bb stack.

4h 2d is one of the worst hands in poker and should be folded even in position. With a large stack advantage over the short-stacked Minni and only 17 hands remaining in the tournament, I can afford to be selective and wait for better spots. Folding weak hands from the button against a short stack is standard strategy.

Heads-up on the button with A♥9♠ (deep-stacked ~76BB). This is a strong opening spot — I want the initiative and postflop position. Min-raise is 200, but I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to generate fold equity, deny cheap equity to weaker hands, and build a pot I can play in position. If Claude calls I’ll use position to see flops and c-bet selectively; if he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (likely fold to a large 3-bet, consider calling/squeezing against a smaller one). 300 is a standard, value-oriented heads-up open that preserves my stack and maximizes EV.

Calling 200 to see the flop. With pot odds of 2:1 and Minni's short stack aggression in heads-up, 6-10 offsuit is marginally playable. Can evaluate post-flop and use stack advantage to outplay Minni in position.

With 6♥ T♣ on a double-ace flop (A♣ A♠ 3♥), I have zero made hand value and minimal equity. Minni checked, indicating potential weakness, but this board strongly favors Minni's preflop raising range. Rather than invest chips with air, I check and maintain my stack advantage. If I hit on the turn or board stabilizes, I can reassess. This conservative approach preserves chips while keeping flexibility for better opportunities.

I have trip Aces (Ah on Ac–As–3h). This is an extremely strong, but vulnerable, hand (the main counter is a full house from pocket 3s or the unlikely Ad slowplay). Claude checked, so I should be extracting value from worse A‑x hands and medium pocket pairs while denying a free card that could turn into a full house. A ~2/3‑pot bet (400 into 600) is big enough to get value from single‑ace hands and most pocket pairs, but not so huge that he folds all worse hands. If he calls I’ll continue to extract on safe turns; if he check‑raises large I’ll be wary (pocket 3s or Ad) and re‑evaluate / fold to extreme aggression.

Folding 6♥ T♣ on an A♣ A♠ 3♥ flop. With zero made hand strength and marginal equity against Minni's bet, combined with a huge chip advantage (32k vs 7k), there's no value in calling. The hand has no real draw potential on this specific board texture. Better opportunities will come in the final 16 hands, so preserving stack integrity is the priority.

In heads-up play with a significant chip advantage (4:1), position on the button is extremely valuable. While Tc5d is a weak hand in general, it's playable from the button against a short stack. I'm raising to 150 to apply pressure and maintain aggressive play - either winning the blind immediately or playing postflop with positional advantage. This aggression is appropriate given the chip lead and remaining hands in the tournament.

Claude (button/SB) opened to 200. I’m in the big blind with 3h2h — a weak but suited, connected holding with backdoor straight/flush potential. The pot is 300 and I only need 100 to call, giving roughly 4:1 pot odds (~20% equity needed). Against a wide button raising range this is an easy defend; I don’t want to 3‑bet this raggy hand out of position. Plan: call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, fold to heavy postflop aggression when missed, and continue only with clear equity (pair/two‑pair/strong draws) or good reads.

I defended preflop with 3h2h for the price, but this flop (6d As 7c) is terrible for my hand — no pair, only runner‑runner wheel/backdoor flush possibilities. OOP vs a button raiser who often has Ax or at least a pair, my realized equity is very low and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a pure bluff. I’ll check to see a free turn and only continue if I pick up a clear piece (pair/two‑pair) or a strong draw; otherwise fold to meaningful pressure.

Continuation bet on the flop after establishing pre-flop aggression. With a significant chip lead in heads-up play against a short stack, I should exploit Minni's weakness (his check). Tc5d has marginal equity but my range advantage from the raise justifies the c-bet. A bet of 200 applies pressure while keeping risk proportional to the pot. If Minni folds, I win immediately; if he calls/raises, I get valuable information. This aggressive approach is appropriate given the tournament stage (hand 135/150) and my substantial chip advantage.

Preflop call was fine, but this flop (6d As 7c) completely misses 3h2h. Claude’s 200 into a 600 pot means I must call 200 to win 800 (need ~25% equity). My chance to pair a 3 or 2 by the river is ~24.1%, runner‑runner straight adds ~1.5% — borderline at best. Worse, Claude (button raiser) often has Ax or a pair here, so many of my small pair outs still lose and I’ll be out of position facing further pressure. There’s effectively no realistic flush possibility (no hearts on board), so my realizable equity is poor. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct — fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♦8♥ — a playable, positionally-advantaged hand. With ~77 BB effective I want to take the initiative rather than just completing; raising gives fold equity and lets me play pots in position. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB) — larger than the minimum 200 to discourage light 3-bets and to extract value when called. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold/call depending on sizing); if he calls I’ll C-bet appropriate flops and use position to pressure weaker holdings. Total committed (including my 50 SB) = 300.

With 4-3 offsuit in the big blind, I'm holding one of the weakest hands in poker. While Minni is short-stacked and likely raising light from the button, the mathematics are marginal at best. Facing 2:1 odds to call 200 to win 400, I'd need about 33% equity to break even, and 4-3 offsuit likely has only around 30-35% equity against Minni's wide raising range. More importantly, with only 15 hands remaining in this 150-hand tournament and a significant chip lead (32k vs 7.4k), folding marginal weak hands is the prudent approach. Even with a wide button range, post-flop play with this hand will be challenging, and I don't need to risk chips on marginal situations when the positional advantage is working against me. Preserving my chip lead and avoiding difficult post-flop decisions is the correct strategy here.

Heads-up play with significant chip advantage vs short stack. Q8o is above-average strength in 2-player situations. Raising exploits Minni's desperation and forces them into difficult decisions. Standard heads-up aggression with a reasonable hand.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket eights (8h8c). Claude opened to 250 from the button — a fairly wide heads-up raise historically. I only have 150 to call, pot is small, and my stack (7,740) is deep enough to play a big pot postflop, but I’m out of position after the flop. Pocket eights are a strong, proactive hand heads-up: they’re ahead of most of the button’s opening range (lots of broadways, suited connectors, weak aces) and can get value or fold out hands that play poorly against a set/overcard-dominated flop. I want to take the initiative, get value, and gain fold equity to prevent cheap flops where I’m OOP. A standard 3-bet sizing around ~3.5x the open is appropriate — big enough to exert pressure but leaves plenty of chips for postflop play. If Claude 4-bets large I can re-evaluate (he’ll often show very strong hands), but against a call I’ll be playing a pot with a strong pair and good equity. Action: 3-bet to 900 total.

Heads-up play with massive chip advantage over desperate short stack. Q8o is a premium hand in 2-player situations and has solid equity against Minni's likely desperation range. Raising to 2,000 (approximately 2.2x the reraise) applies maximum pressure while inviting mistakes. This raise is appropriately sized to capitalize on Minni's vulnerable position - either winning the pot immediately or getting called by worse hands. Position and chip advantage strongly favor aggression here.

Call 1100 more (2000 total). Pocket eights heads-up are strong enough to continue vs a moderate-sized 4‑bet: Claude’s 2000 sizing is not an all-in shove and often contains a mix of value and bluffs in heads-up play. The pot is 2900 so my call (~27.5% break-even) plus deep-ish effective stacks (~69bb) gives good implied odds to set‑mine and realize equity postflop. Folding is too tight here and a thin 5‑bet/shove would be overly committal with a medium pair OOP. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously — extract value on safe textures or fold to heavy, committed action on overcard‑heavy boards.

Check. I’m out of position with pocket eights on A♦2♦7♥ after Claude’s large 4‑bet preflop — his continuing range is heavily weighted toward Ax, big pairs and occasional 2x/sets. 88 is an underpair to the board and has decent equity vs non‑Ace hands but is behind most value hands Claude will have, so betting as a bluff is unattractive (I don’t block A♦ or diamond combos and can’t comfortably fold out top pair). Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity and keep worse hands and bluffs in the field, and gives me the option to call a reasonable c‑bet or fold to extreme aggression. Plan: check‑call small/standard bets to set‑mine and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to a shove or very large overbet if the action becomes highly polarized toward value.

Heads-up with massive chip advantage over desperate short stack. Pre-flop aggression established momentum; Minni's flop check suggests weakness despite the ace-high board. Q8o has showdown value and can benefit from fold equity. Bet 3,000 to apply strong pressure on Minni's remaining 5,840 stack, forcing a difficult all-in decision. Standard continuation-bet aggression in a 2-player pot where position and stack dynamics favor continued pressure. This bet wins immediately against most weak holdings and maintains equity when called.

Call 3000. I’m out of position with pocket eights on A♦2♦7♥ — an underpair to the Ace but still has meaningful equity vs a 4‑better’s continuing range (Ax, some pairs, and occasional bluffs/draws). The pot is 7000 and calling 3000 gives ~30% break‑even; I estimate my raw equity vs Claude’s flop‑betting range in the mid‑20s to low‑30s plus strong implied odds to hit a set. Raising (shove) would be too committal — I don’t block A♦ and would often get called by better hands — folding here surrenders too much fold equity. Plan: call to keep worse hands/draws in and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to a shove or very large turn overbet unless I improve.

Check. The turn (T♦) makes the board much nastier for my hand: I’m an underpair with no diamonds and there are now three diamonds on board, which completes any two-card diamond combo Claude might have. Claude’s line (open, 4‑bet, large flop c‑bet) is heavily weighted to Ax, big pairs/sets and two‑diamond combos — hands that either already beat me or have me crushed. I only have ~2 outs to a set and I’m short-stacked (2,840) into a 10,000 pot, so shoving or leading here would be overly committal and will be called by most value hands. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity for the river, allows me to fold to large turn aggression, and still gives me a chance to realize equity if Claude checks back. Plan: check and fold to an over‑shove or very large denial bet; consider a call only to small/medium bets where my remaining stack and river outs justify the price.

Heads-up with dominant chip advantage (27k vs 2.8k). Qh8d on Ad 2d 7h Td board. Minni's turn check indicates weakness. With an open-ended straight draw (7 outs) plus additional overcards, I have meaningful equity to hit the river. Checking the free card is superior to betting a marginal Queen-high holding - the draw value combined with board texture that hit Minni worse than myself makes this optimal. Maintains balanced strategy while preserving chip advantage.

Heads‑up on the river with 8♣8♥ vs Claude after my 3‑bet and his 4‑bet. Board: A♦ 2♦ 7♥ T♦ 5♠. I’m an underpair to the Ace and there are three diamonds — the board now heavily favors Ax, tens, sets and diamond flushes, which are exactly the kinds of hands a 4‑bet/large flop‑better is likely to hold. My remaining stack (2,840) vs the pot (10,000) means an all‑in bluff would require Claude to fold ~22% of his range to be profitable; given his line (4‑bet pre, c‑bet flop) he will have a lot more showdown value than that and will call a river shove or small value bet with many hands that beat me. I don’t have river outs (it’s the last card) and I don’t block enough of his strong combos. Betting here mostly gets called by better hands and folding equity is limited, so the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and go to showdown — fold to any meaningful river raise if he leads out after my check (I’ll fold to heavy action).

River value bet with Queen-high in heads-up position. Minni's check-turn, check-river pattern indicates weakness or marginal holdings. Betting 3,000 applies maximum pressure to desperate short stack while extracting value from worse holdings. This size forces an all-in decision for Minni (leaving 2,840 unpotted) while building the pot if called. Standard aggressive heads-up play that exploits the passive line and chip disadvantage Minni faces. Position and stack advantage strongly favor this betting action.

I check-fold this river. Recap: I have 8♣8♥ on A♦-2♦-7♥-T♦-5♠ after 3‑betting pre and calling Claude’s 4‑bet. Claude’s line (4‑bet preflop → large flop c‑bet → check turn on the diamond runout → small/targeted river bet) is weighted heavily toward hands that beat me: A‑x, tens, sets and made diamond flushes — exactly the combos a 4‑bettor will continue with. The pot is 13,000 and I must commit my last 2,840, so I’m getting ~18% break‑even equity (2840 / (13000+2840) ≈ 17.9%). My underpair doesn’t block Aces or diamonds and has virtually no river outs — I’m only good vs pure bluffs. Given Claude’s line and deep stack, his bluff frequency here is likely well under the ~18% required to justify an all‑in call. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♠ J♦ — a strong hand in position. Pot is 150 and calling is only 50, but I want to seize initiative and build value while maintaining fold equity. With ~28 BB I’ll open to a standard heads-up sizing of 3x BB (300 total) to isolate the big blind’s wide range and play post‑flop in position. If Claude 3-bets to a reasonable size I’ll consider calling to see a flop; I’ll fold or re-evaluate against an extreme (very large) 3-bet/jam since AJs isn’t a favorite vs a top-heavy 3‑bet range.

Calling 200 with pocket threes against Minni's short-stack desperation raise. With a 14:1 chip lead and Minni playing a wide range from the button with only 2,540 chips, my small pair has reasonable equity. The pot odds are favorable (2:1 to win 600), and pocket pairs always retain showdown value. This is a natural spot to take a flop with chip advantage.

Checking the flop with pocket threes on a 6s-8c-4d board. I'm out of position against Minni's short stack (2,540 chips), and checking accomplishes several strategic goals: (1) it lets Minni bluff or overvalue weaker hands, (2) I retain showdown value with my pocket pair, (3) I'm risking nothing to see another card while maintaining my huge chip lead. With position disadvantage and a marginal hand, checking is more prudent than forcing action when Minni will act last. This allows me to control the pot size and let her make mistakes.

Heads-up in position with A♠J♦ on a dry 6♠-8♣-4♦ flop. I raised preflop and Claude just checked — this board has very little equity for draws and my ace-high has good showdown value against a wide calling range. A continuation bet will fold many worse hands (complete air, medium connectors) and get value from worse aces/king-highs, while keeping the pot manageable given my ~25 BB stack. Size for max fold equity without overcommitting: ~50% pot (300). If Claude check‑raises big I'll respect that and likely fold; if he calls I continue cautiously on the turn.

Calling 300 on the flop with pocket threes. Minni is severely short-stacked with only 2,240 chips and just bet 300 into the 900 pot on a 6s-8c-4d board that favors my hand. I'm getting 2:1 direct pot odds (300 to win 900 after my call), which is reasonable for a small pocket pair against her wide desperate range.

Key considerations:
1. Chip advantage: I have a 14:1 chip lead (36,860 vs 2,240), so losing this hand is acceptable
2. Equity: My pocket pair is ahead of many hands in her wide short-stack raising range (A-high, K-high, Q-high, J-high, unpaired broadways, etc.)
3. Pot odds: 2:1 is favorable for my equity percentage
4. Board: 6-8-4 is relatively dry for big cards, making my small pair more valuable
5. Minni's desperation: She's in "shove mode" and will likely commit her remaining 1,940 chips soon regardless

Calling allows me to reach showdown cheaply while maintaining maximum chip advantage. If a high card comes that doesn't make her an obvious hand, we'll likely see all-in action on the turn, which is fine given my position.

Call and proceed to turn.

Turn brought the 7c to a 6s-8c-4d board. I'm checking with pocket threes, allowing Minni to act with her desperate 2,240 chip stack. The turn card doesn't help my hand, but this is the correct approach for several reasons: (1) Minni is in total shove mode and will commit her stack regardless, (2) if she shoves, I'll be getting approximately 1.54:1 odds (2,240 to call for 3,440 total pot), which is near sufficient for pocket threes' equity against her wide range, (3) with my 16:1 chip lead, I can afford to see showdown and take calculated flips, (4) checking maximizes the pot while keeping her in the hand with her desperate all-in tendency. This is position-aggressive play designed to extract maximum value from her stack.

Check. I raised pre and c-bet the dry 6♠-8♣-4♦ flop and Claude just called; the turn 7♣ is a relatively blank — only a 5 really completes a straight — and Claude checked. Given his flop call his range is heavily weighted to made pairs (4s/6s/8s or pocket pairs/overcards) and occasional weak A-x; my A♠J♦ has decent showdown value versus his wide calling range but is frequently behind. Pot is 1200 and my stack is 2240 — I don’t want to bloat the pot with thin value or get raised off a marginal hand. I’ll control the pot by checking the turn and evaluate the river: call small-to-medium river bets for value, fold to large overbets that look like a made hand or a check-raise line.

River value bet with made 4-high straight on 6-8-4-7-5 board. Minni is severely short-stacked at 2,240 with a wide range from the button/SB. Betting 600 (50% pot) extracts value and applies critical pressure on her remaining chips. While she may have improved the straight with a 9 or matched it with a 3, my straight is a legitimate holding that warrants aggressive value extraction on the final street. This is more effective than checking, which surrenders value and allows her to escape with weak hands cheaply. The bet forces her to either fold (I win immediately) or call (seeing showdown with a decent equity hand).

Call. The 5 on the river completed a 4-5-6-7-8 straight on board, so my A♠J♦ only plays the board; the only way Claude beats me is with a 9 in his hand (which makes 5-6-7-8-9). Given the line (call on the flop, check the turn, half‑pot river bet) and combinatorics, the chance he holds a 9 is well under 50% (roughly ~17% by raw combos), so calling the 600 is +EV — if we tie we net +600, if he has the 9 we lose 600. Raising has no fold equity and just risks more when we’re usually chopping, so the correct play is to call the 600.

Folding 4-2o from the button in heads-up play. While Minni is short-stacked (2,740), I have such a dominant chip lead (37,110) that I don't need to gamble with one of the worst hands in poker. My positional advantage is better utilized by playing stronger holdings where I have better equity. 4-2 offsuit is a clear fold even in heads-up; I'll wait for better spots to exploit Minni's desperation and short stack.

Heads-up on the button with 7♣9♥ — a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability in position. I have ~28 BB (2,840), so I want to take the initiative rather than just completing. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) achieves that: it denies cheap equity, isolates the BB, and keeps the SPR manageable if we get called. Plan: if Claude calls we’ll play postflop aggressively with position and c‑bet when appropriate; fold to a large 3‑bet (or re-evaluate if the sizing/read suggests a light 3‑bet). I’ll raise to 300 to seize initiative and apply pressure.

Calling 200 to see a flop with K8 suited in heads-up play against a critically short-stacked opponent. Minni has only 2,590 chips and is in push-fold mode. K8 suited has decent equity (42-45%) against a wide short-stack range, and the 2:1 pot odds make this call profitable. With my large chip advantage, I can play exploitatively post-flop and make strong decisions based on board texture.

Betting 400 on the flop with K8 suited after flopping a 4-flush on 5-9-10 with two spades. Against a critically short-stacked opponent in heads-up play, I'm applying appropriate pressure while holding excellent equity (flush draw + overcard). This semi-bluff bet has strong fold equity and good showdown value if called. The bet size relative to the pot (400 into 600) and Minni's remaining stack (2,590) creates a decision point that leverages my chip advantage and hand equity.

Flop is 5♠ 9♠ Tc and I hold 9♥7♣ — a pair of nines with a weak kicker, but I’m in position. Claude’s 400 into a 1000 pot is a relatively small sizing that represents a wide range (missed overcards, spade draws, marginal tens, or a probe). Pot‑odds for a call are attractive (~28% break‑even) and calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in while controlling the pot size. A min‑raise to 800 would protect but also needlessly bloats the pot and can get me committed versus tens/sets or a check‑raise. I’ll call 400 to use position on the turn and re‑evaluate (bet for value if I improve, or fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t).

Turn action with K♠8♠ on 5♠9♠10♣5♣. I have a powerful four-flush (8 spade outs to make a flush) plus overcards. Minni is critically short-stacked with only 2190 chips remaining and just checked to me.

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: 4-flush is a strong draw with substantial equity, especially since any spade gives me a likely winner
2. **Positional advantage**: Minni checked, showing weakness. I should leverage this.
3. **Stack pressure**: With Minni at 2190 chips and pot at 1400, a well-sized bet creates a difficult decision for his push-fold range
4. **Equity advantage**: My flush draw gives me solid equity against most of his holdings

Action: BET to apply pressure, charge him for river equity, and potentially take down the pot. A bet of 700 is appropriately-sized - not too large to be polarizing, but substantial enough to create real pressure on a short stack. This continuation of aggression is standard in heads-up play against a desperate opponent.

I have top pair (9) with a very weak kicker on 5♠ 9♠ Tc 5♣. Claude’s small flop bet (400) and modest turn bet (700) are consistent with a wide HU range (Tx, medium pairs, probes and some bluffs) but also include hands that already crush me (any T, trips/boats with 5x, trips 9x). Raising to the min (1400) would bloat the pot and commit a large portion of my 2,190 stack with a marginal hand that fares poorly vs the stronger parts of his range; folding is too nitty given my decent showdown equity and position. Calling 700 gives correct pot control and roughly 25%+ break‑even equity to see a river, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in the pot, and lets me use position to fold to heavy river aggression or extract thin value if he checks. I will call 700 and re-evaluate on the river.

River value bet with K♠8♠ king-high flush. The 10♠ completed my draw while likely giving Minni lower flush completions or pairs. With his critical 1490-chip stack, a bet of 1000 applies appropriate pressure—it's large enough to require a decision but not so large that it's unnecessarily polarizing. This extracts value from his lower flushes, pairs, and draws that called the turn. Most hands that beat me (A♠x♠) will call anyway; most hands that lose (K-high flush or worse) will fold. The bet size puts him in a pot-committed situation where any call closes the action, and my king-high flush is a massive favorite against his likely holdings.

I have two pair (Tens and Nines) with a 7 kicker on Ts Tc 5s 5c 9s. Pot is 3,800 and Claude bet 1,000; calling 1,000 to win 4,800 requires ~20.8% equity. The only hands that absolutely crush me are any Ten or any Five (full houses) and pocket 9s — combinatorially those full-house combos are ~17% of all holdings, and single-9 hands with a kicker >7 add roughly another ~4%, so only ~21% of all combos beat me versus an unweighted range (I win the rest). Claude’s river sizing is small and his line is consistent with a mixed range (thin value + bluffs), not a pure value-only shove. Raising here just bloats the pot and commits me against the portion of his range that dominates me (T/5), and folding gives up far more than the pot odds allow. I’ll call the 1,000 to realize my decent showdown equity and let him show his range.

In heads-up play against a short-stacked opponent (390 chips), Jc6c is a reasonable hand with decent equity. Calling to see the flop is optimal - I maintain position for post-flop play while keeping the pot small with a marginal hand. The suited cards provide additional equity and I can make strong hands (top pair, flush draws) on many flops. With my significant chip lead, there's no need to overplay a one-gapped suited hand here.

I’m extremely short-stacked (~3.9 BB) in the big blind with A♣9♣ heads-up. SB just called to see a flop, so I can check or raise. With this stack depth any small raise leaves me with negligible fold equity and poor postflop options; A9s has good heads-up equity and performs well shoved. I want to maximize fold equity and either take the pot now or get all-in with a hand that fares well against a wide calling range. I will shove my remaining stack (total commitment including my posted BB).

Calling 290 more (total 340 committed this hand) with Jc6c in heads-up position. Reasonable hand with decent equity against Minni's wide BB range, excellent pot odds at 2.7:1, and positional advantage post-flop. Massive chip lead allows for playing marginal hands profitably in position. The Jack-six suited has potential to make strong hands (top pair, straights, flushes) on many flops.

Board Jd 9s 4d, I hold A♣9♣ — top pair with the best kicker heads-up. Pot is 780 and my remaining stack is 100 (no bet to call). With such a short effective stack there’s little benefit to checking or a small probe bet: I want to maximize fold equity and extract value now. Claude cold‑called my preflop raise and will have a wide range (we beat many hands, but lose to Jx/9x), so shoving/betting my remaining 100 is the highest‑EV line — I either take down the pot immediately or get called by worse and still have good showdown equity. Bet all‑in for 100.

Calling 100 chips with top pair (Jacks with 6-kicker) on Jd9s4d. Excellent pot odds of 8.8:1 and a made hand provide strong value. Massive chip lead (39K+) allows profitable play in marginal situations. Heads-up ranges are wide, so top pair has significant equity. Maintaining position and keeping pot manageable while progressing to the turn.
